I would love to know some of the unexpected and odd things yall have found in your labs....this stuff fascinates me. Most people dont look, some of us that do may have wished we didnt...it can be quite the can of worms!

After listening to Dr Jackson last night I realize I am going to have to revisit a lot of old tests and be sure that everything that was once there is truly gone.

The oddest thing I can remember finding in one of my labs was African Sleeping Sickness....WTH!?!

What strange stuff have yall found lurking in your bodies???

### JoeBranca — 2013-11-14T21:10:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weirdest-lab-finds.8759/post-100725>

IvanDrago - I am right there with you in a lot of ways. In labs drawn last week, everything looks great but then there is the super low T (209!) and low Free T, not to mention a crisis level BUN/creatinine ratio of 27. I certainly don't look or feel the part. I'm also CT'ing several times a week in the Pacific, ketogenic-ish epi-paleo and blocking blue light at night, getting direct sun in the day, throwing back tons of RO water and mineral water, but ... I work in IT at a university in a city in southern California with a lot of tech and research industries going on. If I'm to stay in this location for now, EMF mitigation absolutely has to be brought to the next level, but I'm still in a brainstorming way to do it smartly, effectively, without wasting $$$. I'm determined to find a way.

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-16T01:03:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-quantum-health-model.8760/post-100797>

> [seanb4 said:](/goto/post?id=100783)
>
> Like the idea of visualising some of these concepts but I'm not sure I understand the diagram. Are the black arrows meant to be things that negatively effect becoming optimal?

Sean

They're inputs - things that can help you get to optimal - think nutrition = epipaleo or it could kick you out of optimal think food = wheat. Or think of a flu virus, or bacterial infection - that could easily knock you out - impacting your gut health and causing a stress response.

EMF does the same thing. not all inputs are good, not all inputs are bad - but they can cause stress upon you. Think about viruses - and go back and read Brain gut series. Viruses can help us evolve or they can kill us - and our gut is the mechanism by which we assimilate them everything is connected.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-16T04:43:02+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-16T04:51:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-quantum-health-model.8760/post-100804>

I hope you all listen to the Nov webinar.......i found it simple to do because we focused on one small aspect that is foundational to a quantum cell........The quantum cell takes the parts of nature.......you all learned in EE 4 and it adds in the protein back bone.......from these four fundamental things, acting in unison, or coherently, we get life.

This life.......is designed to be a see a saw........this is a synonym for meta stability. It can go up or down based upon what the cell faces in the environment it is put into..........

Randy just explained how the Quantum physics issue plays its roll of the dice on DNA: He said, "And don't forget, all of these interactions are quantum and therefore probabilistic and **not predictable. it just depends**. So the outcomes may be different--that is, one person may be healthy and another contracts cancer. We are all different because of our quantum nature." That captures the essence of the physics and yet explains why biology will never get it........because they think life is concrete when at its core.......it is quantum and it can never be pinned down ever.......just like an electron.

# Thread: Fermented Vegetables
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-vegetables.8763/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### elvey90 — 2013-11-14T13:05:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-vegetables.8763/post-100697>

Hey All,

A newbie here on the Epi Paleo diet. In Brain Gut 6, Jack mentions to eat fermented vegetables, can anyone provide examples of acceptable fermented vegetables to choose from?
I'm looking at saukreut at the moment but not sure if that's ok to eat.

Cheers!

### Hemming — 2013-11-14T13:49:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-vegetables.8763/post-100698>

Sauerkraut is fine. Kimchii in general.

You can make your own fermented food from whatever you like. Currently I'm fermenting apples with chili, ginger, garlic, cinnamon and liquorice (one flavour in each jar).

### JoeBranca — 2013-11-14T21:03:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-vegetables.8763/post-100722>

Hemming, fermented apples sounds interesting. I'd like to try that while they're still in season. How are you doing it? Just slicing them up and adding salt in a jar?

### Hemming — 2013-11-14T21:09:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fermented-vegetables.8763/post-100724>

I sliced them up and put them in a bowl with salt. I put 1dl of sauerkraut juice in each jar, added the apples with around 1.5l of water.

I haven't tasted the apples yet but the fermentation is on track. The liquid blew out when I opened them the other day. Its not as bad anymore  The liquid tasts like apple cider including the carbonated feeling.

# Thread: Breakthrough in Quantum Computing - Qubit Lasts 39 Minutes at Room Temp
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakthrough-in-quantum-computing-qubit-lasts-39-minutes-at-room-temp.8766/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cinnamon — 2013-11-15T02:28:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/breakthrough-in-quantum-computing-qubit-lasts-39-minutes-at-room-temp.8766/post-100741>

Fascinating article published today on advances in research toward building a silicon based quantum computer.

[http://www.cbc.ca/news/technology/qubit-record-moves-quantum-computing-forward-1.2426986](http://www.cbc.ca/news/technology/qubit-record-moves-quantum-computing-forward-1.2426986)

Excerpt:
**

"Researchers are trying to develop quantum computers because they have the potential for exponentially greater computing power than conventional computers. That is because conventional computers encode data as "bits," each of which is in one of two possible states, "0" or "1," while quantum computers encode data as "qubits" that can each be in multiple states simultaneously. That would allow them to perform multiple calculations as the same time.

The phenomenon of being in a "0" and "1" state simultaneously, known as "superposition," is possible due to the strange laws of quantum physics that apply only to very small particles such as atoms that are used to create qubits."​

**
As you may have guessed, qubits work better in the freezing cold....


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Concerns about coconut oil and DHT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/concerns-about-coconut-oil-and-dht.8767/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2013-11-15T03:12:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/concerns-about-coconut-oil-and-dht.8767/post-100742>

I understand that coconut oil is a very healthy substance with it's saturated fats, ketogenic properties and antimicrobial properties. I'm really interested in beginning to get a couple of tablespoons of it throughout the day, however, I have a concern. The only thing keeping me from consuming any is a study I read a while back discussing the inhibitory properties of lauric acid (from coconut oil) on 5-alpha reductase, the enzyme that turns testosterone into the more active Dihydrotestosterone. I'm 16 so I know my hormonal system is quite different from the majority of the people on this forum. I don't want to mess with my hormonal levels at this age as I'm concerned about permanent effects resulting from hormonal imbalances. I'm hesitant to consume a substance which may lower my dht levels. Does anyone have any knowledge on this topic? Will taking coconut oil decrease the dht by a significant amount, or do the positive effects outweigh the slight hormonal effects? Thanks!

### caroline — 2013-11-15T03:23:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/concerns-about-coconut-oil-and-dht.8767/post-100744>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/concerns-about-coconut-oil-and-dht.8767/post-100757>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/concerns-about-coconut-oil-and-dht.8767/post-100780>

Yes, interesting. Does anyone know what time of year this experiment was run?

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-11-15T23:25:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/concerns-about-coconut-oil-and-dht.8767/post-100795>

### Huck — 2013-11-15T13:39:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-physics-proves-that-there-is-an-afterlife.8769/post-100771>

-   Robert Lanza claims the theory of biocentrism says death is an illusion
-   He said life creates the universe, and not the other way round
-   This means space and time don't exist in the linear fashion we think it does
-   He uses the famous double-split experiment to illustrate his point
-   And if space and time aren't linear, then death can't exist in 'any real sense' either

> He continued: 'Life is an adventure that transcends our ordinary linear way of thinking. When we die, we do so not in the random billiard-ball-matrix but in the inescapable-life-matrix.'

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencet...ics-proves-IS-afterlife-claims-scientist.html](http://www.dailymail.co.uk/sciencetech/article-2503370/Quantum-physics-proves-IS-afterlife-claims-scientist.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-15T15:56:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-physics-proves-that-there-is-an-afterlife.8769/post-100773>

### nonchalant — 2013-11-15T17:08:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quantum-physics-proves-that-there-is-an-afterlife.8769/post-100775>

I believe our primal kitty almost died Monday night. We saw that she had killed and eaten half of a rat that Saturday. It may have been poisoned, but I'm not sure why it would take so long to make her sick. To our relief, she was better Tuesday morning, and on Thursday she was completely back to normal. Good thing we perceived her as alive Tuesday morning.

This is so bizarre.

### nonchalant — 2013-11-17T03:22:41+0200

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sick-of-tired.8773/post-100786>

Welcome Danny! Energy is the key to it all.

### caroline — 2013-11-15T22:57:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sick-of-tired.8773/post-100789>

Hi Danny and welcome!

Please go to the home page and follow the easy start guides. Just jump in and start with the leptin R/X using the epi- paleo template.

It would be great for you to start a journal in the journal section - go to the top of this page and click on forum and go down 5 categories to journals.

A journal really helps with commitment and Dr. Kruse likes journals so he can get some context. Please tell us more! Do you have labs that you can post? there is a section for labs and there are many here who can hack them for you - or at least give it a crack!

Do you have Dr. K.'s book? available at amazon - on kindle and paper. It is a great, easy and quick overview of all of this

Please listen to the podcasts - they are listed on the home page. Huge source of information and easy to grasp.

There is a new bootcamp starting with Dr. Tim Jackson .... Heal your hormones - he gives a lot of detail and is very easy to understand. We already have had week one last week - but if you purchase the bootcamp you will get that one too.
Check out the home page.

If you become a gold member you have access to the whole library of webinars and the live Q&A that follows a few days later. You can view them at any time you want and as many times as you want. you certainly don't have to - we will support you in any way we can.

There is a lot here to take in .......just keep reading and asking questions!

### RickD — 2013-11-16T04:00:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/myopulse-therapy.8774/post-100799>

Anyone here try myopulse therapy for soft tissue injuries? Been messing with some achilles tendonosis and decided to try a new Chiropractor in town. After checking me out he recommended I give myopulse therapy a try. I agreed and let his tech hook me up. Unlike most therapy techniques I felt absolutely nothing during the treatment and was thinking this was a waste of time and money, but walking back to the office afterwards my achilles felt better than it had it weeks. Same thing after a second treatment. So far I'm impressed.

Researching it bit it seems to come right out Becker's work. Small amounts of currents in the microamp range are applied to the body at frequencies generally below 10hz giving the injured cells the energy they need to heal themselves. The link below provides more background

[http://www.healthydoctors.com/acusc...ope and myopulse explantion.htm#\_Toc457224424](http://www.healthydoctors.com/acuscope%20and%20myopulse/acuscope%20and%20myopulse%20explantion.htm#_Toc457224424)

### caroline — 2013-11-16T05:17:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/myopulse-therapy.8774/post-100807>

Rick - that sounds really interesting .... I hope we will hear more.

### RickD — 2013-11-16T05:22:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/myopulse-therapy.8774/post-100809>

If it continues along the trend I've seen so far I might end up buying one and hooking myself up 24/7

### caroline — 2013-11-16T06:27:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/myopulse-therapy.8774/post-100816>

so - it isn't too expensive? or are you independently wealthy??? What about EMF's?

### RickD — 2013-11-16T07:33:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/myopulse-therapy.8774/post-100821>

I was trying and it appears failing to be funny. Machine is probably 8-10k to buy and input loaded with EMF, although what it outputs is acceptable to our bodies from what I read. Per use treatment cost is $59

### caroline — 2013-11-16T05:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-9-quantum-sleep.8775/post-100810>

How is everyone doing with this latest blog?

I feel like a lot more is becoming clearer for me ..... I really do like the brain pain \[most of the time!\]

Maybe this is what quantum tunnelling is all about..... a direct route from JK - to us!

I do go off the rails now and again ... I promise to do better ...

### Josh — 2014-10-04T03:20:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-9-quantum-sleep.8775/post-142745>

**"The pineal gland pays attention to light, dark, magnetic fields and to gravity." EE9**

Where does the gravity come from? Inside from protein tubules and water or outside from the interaction of larger things? Or is it the center of the vortex in the 3rd ventricle?

I am thinking that the better the redox and electron flow with higher magnetic fields, the less susceptible to outside gravitational forces and the stronger the endogenous ones will be from microtubules and the 3rd ventricle vortex. This would explain why certain people are more sensitive to exogenous changes in gravity and would be diagnostic for their redox, etc..

### Josh — 2014-10-04T03:21:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-9-quantum-sleep.8775/post-142746>

[http://www.cell.com/current-biology/pdf/S0960-9822(08)00865-8.pdf](http://www.cell.com/current-biology/pdf/S0960-9822\(08\)00865-8.pdf)

> Collectively the daily, seasonal, lunar and tidal geophysical
> cycles regulate much of the temporal biology of life on
> Earth. The increasing isolation of human societies from
> these geophysical cycles, as a result of improved living
> conditions, high-quality nutrition and 24/7 working practices,
> have led many to believe that human biology functions
> independently of them. Yet recent studies have highlighted
> the dominant role that our circadian clock plays in
> the organisation of 24 hour patterns of behaviour and
> physiology. Preferred wake and sleep times are to a large
> extent driven by an endogenous temporal program that
> uses sunlight as an entraining cue. The alarm clock can
> drive human activity rhythms but has little direct effect
> on our endogenous 24 hour physiology. In many situations,
> our biology and our society appear to be in serious opposition,
> and the damaging consequences to our health
> under these circumstances are increasingly recognised.
> The seasons dominate the lives of non-equatorial species,
> and until recently, they also had a marked influence on
> much of human biology. Despite human isolation from seasonal
> changes in temperature, food and photoperiod in the
> industrialised nations, the seasons still appear to have
> a small, but significant, impact upon when individuals are
> born and many aspects of health. The seasonal changes
> that modulate our biology, and how these factors might interact
> with the social and metabolic status of the individual
> to drive seasonal effects, are still poorly understood. Lunar
> cycles had, and continue to have, an influence upon human
> culture, though despite a persistent belief that our mental
> health and other behaviours are modulated by the phase of
> the moon, there is no solid evidence that human biology is
> in any way regulated by the lunar cycle

### Josh — 2014-10-04T17:34:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ee-9-quantum-sleep.8775/post-142780>

[http://www.collective-evolution.com...didnt-sleep-like-you-are-were-doing-it-wrong/](http://www.collective-evolution.com/2014/10/02/your-ancestors-didnt-sleep-like-you-are-were-doing-it-wrong/)

# Thread: Thread for non Geeks....... and those who love non Geeks!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 39

### caroline — 2013-11-17T10:10:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100886>

I often have trouble with all the science..... anyone else? I want to get much, much better at this.

If JK is going thru all the trouble to lay all this out for us - I want to give it my best shot!

If you have something you don't understand ..... let's talk and see if we can figure it out together....unless I am a party of one????

Randy has given a really good explanation of transition metals in another thread....

C/T#7 is a great back to basics blog - in my opinion ..... what do y'all think?

Any ideas on how we can nail this????

### prAna303 — 2013-11-17T10:56:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100893>

For me the science is quite easy to understand BUT i can not spread the word in these terms. The geeky words is not for me to use, i can not talk that language and if i should try, i would look and behave like a fool i feel. So for me i take the science, go outside, look at the ocean, the mountains, the snowfall and connect the dots. When i get back i can speak the ideas in MY words so i can stand for those thoughts. When i get confused i always ask myself this:

How would i, we humans do IF we did not have all this weird things. How would i act if i was as wild as an animal?

### Inger — 2013-11-17T11:04:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100895>

Yeah.. I am like Peter too. I understand it because I feel/experience it in the real world. That is enough for me.  and I look up stuff and read about it if I get confused. I do not have a geek mind either. Must be because I grew up on potatoes. But I am not too sorry because of it, because I really enjoy life here and now. you do not need a geek brain to do that

I would say.. do not get stuck if something seems to complicated in some blogs, just read further and take the whole picture and live it. You will know

### SeaHorse — 2013-11-17T13:43:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100897>

Audrey....I've been meaning to ask you this for awhile and now you've opened the door....what exactly aren't you understanding? Because I see you changing your life and trying to live all the protocols and find your own way in the world.... what worries you if you can't repeat the exact steps of protein unfolding, or the methylation cycle or interesting behaviors of electrons and photons.

Because that knowledge is out there (or here, actually) doesn't mean you need to be able to memorize it. Understand it yes, in terms of how you are living your life....but it seems to me it is causing you a lot of stress which is not the point. And one thing we did learn very clearly and simply is if cortisol is off then a lot of other things are off....I don't feel that we have to know every element of the science to know what we need to do...

On the other hand, if this is a passion for you to know all this stuff at the same level that Dr. K writes about it, then "fill your boots" as they say in Newfoundland, but why stress about it? We all spend too much time on our computers reading the site as it is.....I'm not trying to be argumentative....You sound stressed to me....many of us are not going to be able to write out the science, but I can tell somebody why I am doing all the things I'm doing and you, of all of us who post seem to be on a mission to spread the word and it sounds like you do an amazing job.

Also, information overload is a killer when it comes to inspiration....there has to be a balance....."knowing better" also means knowing when information isn't the point and action and change is....something Dr. K has talked about a lot...an idea without execution etc. etc. So....that's my little loving poke in your side today Audrey....you're the best!!!

### Huck — 2013-11-17T13:46:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100898>

For about a year, I really made an effort to understand the blog posts. But I'm afraid my brain hasn't healed enough yet to understand the detail, so now I just skim the posts looking for the big picture idea and the specific ideas that are in bold. I can't hold enough information in my short term memory to understand complicated subjects for which I don't have an adequate knowledge base. Understanding the detail doesn't necessarily speed up the healing, so I don't sweat the details.

I keep waiting for the Google translator to add Krusean.

### Hemming — 2013-11-17T14:10:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100899>

I think Jack really wants to spread the message but his blogs get way too technical for the majority of the readers which scares away many. I listen a lot more to the podcasts than the recent blogs. Some of the earlier blogs were easier to understand. If you don't have an education in biology/medicine etc. you're screwed in some of them. I'm not saying that's right or wrong but more that there is a theoretical and practical side of this. I understand when people say its all about light, water and magnetism but how exactly do I live by those rules if I can't decipher it from a blog post?

### prAna303 — 2013-11-17T16:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100903>

It is like a perfect game of flirting... You get a balance between action, thoughts and connection. The thing is that when you get stuck on some parts, it will take some action from you to get more information by yourself, you begin to act and not react.

So i think Jack is doing a magic game of flirting with us, and he does it really good.

### Inger — 2013-11-17T16:40:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100905>

Yes, the flirting thing is it! I am totally hooked since the very beginning... haha...

I think Jack is the flirting master

Hemming, I am very un-geeky but I just look up terms I do not understand if I feel I need it to get the picture he is painting. I even use google translator at times because my English is not the best... I think it is possible for anyone... but sure there must be a will to learn. Maybe... we must first feel the spirit.. and then we get hooked... like Peter describes it with the flirting thing. Then nothing is difficult anymore, because you are in love. Anyone been in deep love know the wings and magic it gives

Maybe it is all about love in the end

### caroline — 2013-11-18T01:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100924>

Thankyou everyone for all your profound thoughts on this. All of you challenge me in so many ways and I thank you for that.

Initially the reason I started this thread is because I worry that newbies are embarrassed to ask questions that they perceive as stupid .... I know I did - and still do sometimes.

Jack has said if we don't ask questions - he doesn't know what we don't know ... and I appreciate that.

He told me once - long ago - about a dopey question .... "it isn't dopey - the dopey thing is that you waited so long to ask".

I always try to remember that and pass that along to new people. I hate to see them drop of - for whatever reason ....but especially if they are frustrated with the science. Overall - the concepts are simple to implement and then as we keep reading and asking questions we start connecting the dots.

As I have said, I really do appreciate that Dr. Kruse has to write for the science geeks and add in all the cites. If he doesn't he gets ripped to shreds and not taken seriously.

Seahorse - thankyou for your reply.....as you rightly point out - I do seem to be stressed and can't shake it off. I think I need to have some fun!!

There are so many pearls of wisdom in your post that really mean alot to me. I really do know all that but sometimes get a little off track!

All of you are my straight edge.........

I think what bothers me the most is - I can't explain things to people properly ...... using all the correct terminology.
I am going to stop worrying about that and just do what I am doing.....

I still think tho - if there is a concept we aren't grasping ....we should talk it thru. Randy gave such a great explanation for transition metals and, of course, Gretchen is always a help!

### cantweight — 2013-11-18T03:42:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100932>

In the real world i am geekier than most, on this site I am definitely a non-geek. I love reading these blogs and watching the webinars it is so raw and honest. I am so sick of medical bullshit that fully explainable science is refreshing whether I get it or not. No smoke blowing....it is what it is. While I dont get everything, what I have gotten has excited me and made me look at things differently; its given me hope

Something funny....a few months back I had an extra credit assignment for a history class I was taking. The assignment was very simple just share a website that correlated with a chapter in our history book. Our first chapter was all pre history, evolution "Lucy" and "Ardi"...so I shared Jacks site. I thought the bipedalism blog and a few others were a great match.

I got a zero. No points. I contacted the teacher to plead my case and ask why he gave me no credit....he said because no one in the class could possibly understand anything on the site.

So I figure no matter how much I dont get....I'm still getting more than my history professor....lol

### nonchalant — 2013-11-18T04:06:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100934>

I took a few Coursera classes online, but I found that I had problems with some of their initial premises. They would define the problem as pertaining to X, and then go merrily on their way, discussing all the ramifications of X. But I wanted to explore Y. But Y was dismissed early on, perhaps because Y didn't fit all those nifty equations they had cleverly figured out.

And since Jack was speeding things up on his blog, I just didn't have time to spend on elegant theory that didn't necessarily fit my observations.

I thought the bipedalism blog and a few others were a great match.
>
> I got a zero. No points. I contacted the teacher to plead my case and ask why he gave me no credit....he said because no one in the class could possibly understand anything on the site.
>
> So I figure no matter how much I dont get....I'm still getting more than my history professor....lol

I, too, love reading the blogs and then the webinars - they are so raw and honest ......I just have to cut myself some slack sometimes. I do sometimes forget that it isn't possible for me to know everything a neurosurgeon knows and then factor in that Dr. K. is in another dimension altogether..... maybe another planet?????

The 'degree of slowness' of your Ferrari is directionally proportional to the intensity of memory in your brain. The better the hormones the better the memory. The degree of speed is directionally proportional to the intensity of forgetting. The more your circadian cycles are off the less time you have. When you follow QED, you travel "on light". But not at the same speed at sunlight. Everything in the world is about sex except sex. The reason they cars and hormones are bed fellows is because sex and speed is about power. Night

Education is the knowledge is the only thing that increases your wellness quotient. Why do I get "feisty" at times? If it does not.........it's on you.

### nicld — 2013-11-18T19:51:38+0200

I think I am going to take reading and learning from the blogs the same way I did in college and I got my two degrees.

Each one will be copied into a word document and any words I do not get, I will look up and put their meaning in the document. Then, hopefully at the end of each blog I will try to write myself a summary of what I think it means. I need to get this stuff, my life and my families well being depends on it.

I may have started with one of the hardest series (Quantum Biology) but if I do it a little at a time, some might sink in. I have chemisty and two semesters of Physics in college but I think that all of that learning was left in the classroom right after the final exam. I just do not use it enough to remember it. ### nicld — 2013-11-18T21:07:10+0200

Oh, I don’t think I will “get” quantum mechanics but I at least need to understand the words and the basic meaning of them so I can get the basic jist of it.

For me, I need each one of the terms broken down so that it makes sense to me, kinda like a glossary of terms. I am a very written word learner more than a pure visual or oral word learner. I used to take notes from a lecture then rewrite them and color code them so they made sense to me. That’s how my crazy brain works and one can never go wrong with learning.

### caroline — 2013-11-19T00:39:08+0200

I think we all intuitively know this ... don't we?

### caroline — 2013-11-19T00:58:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-non-geeks-and-those-who-love-non-geeks.8780/post-100987>

Yesterday, on a coach to Sydney, There was a really huge obese young woman. She fully took up the whole compartment/space between her seat and the one in front. She had her own seat right back and, sad to say, she looked like a beached whale splayed out.

Instead of feeling indignant about her plight and wondering why she doesn't help herself ....... I felt so sorry that she has been failed by her doctor, family, friends, us et all.

But, after reading this thread ....... It is her personal responsibility to somehow help herself ...... that is what all of us are doing ....that is what Dr. K. did.

If she isn't sick and tired of being sick and tired - what can anyone do.......

### Inger — 2013-11-19T14:43:27+0200

### caroline — 2013-11-17T10:24:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-shirt.8781/post-100887>

In one of JK's last podcasts - he talks about wearing a compression shirt. That surprised me that he still does that ......Does anyone have a take on that? I understand it compresses the fat cells - is there a detail we have missed? For me - that part of the podcast was really breaking up so I might have missed something critical!

Usually Jack prefers no clothes....... but I guess one can't make rounds starkers!

I have worn a compression like tank top for the past 18 months....I wonder if this is part of the reason my shape has changed dramatically? I just do it because I got used to wearing one and now love it - a lot better than undies ....sorry TMI ....

The only drawback .... if someone needed to undress me in a hurry......

### prAna303 — 2013-11-17T10:50:46+0200

Could it be something like this?

When you have been in a bad EMF place you must take some extreme actions to lower the entropy in the unit called you. Cold water AND pressure simulates the great depth of the oceans where life is strong and energy filled. So simply said, the shirts helps keeping the energy inside you like a closed system, stopping you to loose energy to the world around us.

### Inger — 2013-11-17T10:56:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/compression-shirt.8781/post-100892>

Audrey...lol you are priceless. I know why I love you  (my secret tho..)

The compression shirt is because it makes less blood flow at the skin surface which makes it cool faster and stay colder as we do CT. The purpose with CT is to get the skin temp down. Not the core temp. That is how I have understood it.

I like Peters theory too  kind of could think about it like this, the blood get "pressured" into us from the surface = core temp stays warm better

# Thread: How much water do you drink a day?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-11-17T16:41:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-100906>

I drink about a litre (bottled, spring) I would say. I have roughly <100g protein, 40g carbs, the rest fat. The protein should require me to consume more water but the fat metabolises into water when it breaks down (hydrogen + oxygen) so would this offset the extra water for protein?

I am wandering if this is enough and what other people drink.

I am quite thirsty often but only in my mouth if that makes sense. My stomach reacts badly to anything (bloating/distension) so I am never thirsty in my stomach (if that makes sense). An example of this was I dry fasted for 7 days and never really felt truly like I wanted water, my mouth did, but overall I didn't. When I did become pretty damn dehydrated I drank some water, but after a few sips I had to force the rest down and my stomach bloated instantly, I could feel the water slushing around in my stomach 24hrs later.
I know this probably happens to everyone when dehydrated but it happens to me every time I eat or drink but just to a lesser extent.

Anyway I have been trying to get around this by taking a mouthful of water roughly every 10-15 mins and 2 hrs away from food. But I am now wondering if I am constantly mildly dehydrated and that is contributing to my symptoms (fatigue, dizziness, palpitations, bloating, excema, cold extremities, sunken eyes, constipation, etc).

So how much water do you drink in a day and how much do you get from food?

### Inger — 2013-11-17T16:57:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-100907>

You get way too little Sean. I drink maybe 3 liters/day. It varies a bit as I do not count it really. I eat mostly raw so my food is moist and juicy mostly, I probably get some water from food too.
If you are around EMF and drink only 1 liter it is pretty bad I guess

### seanb4 — 2013-11-17T17:23:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-100909>

I might struggle getting to 3 litres, whilst keeping stomach symptoms somewhat under control, but I will definitely try to push up to 2 litres per day over the next week and note the effects.

Anyone else?

### seanb4 — 2013-11-17T17:31:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-100910>

Also how many times per day do you pee?

### seanb4 — 2013-11-19T11:01:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-101008>

I'd say I pee usually 3-4 times a day. But with extra water more like 10

### Inger — 2013-11-19T13:30:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-101010>

I never count...lol But I guess around 5 times maybe? It varies... sometimes I go to pee even if I do not feel like I have too, before going out etc. I pee in the morning after raising, and in the evening before going to bed and a couple of times in between
I do not need to pee in the night anymore even if I drink pretty much in the evening (except when I have my period.. then I sometimes have to, IDK why)

### seanb4 — 2013-11-19T20:01:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-101037>

Thanks inger.

If im peeing a lot more when drinking extra water does this mean the water is not beong used?

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-19T20:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-101040>

I drink anywhere btwn 2-4 32oz jugs of RO water/day....

### seanb4 — 2013-11-22T17:34:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-much-water-do-you-drink-a-day.8783/post-101369>

Okay so the general consensus is between 2-4 litres.

Over the last couple of days I've upped my water intake to 2 litres. Whilst bloating has increased slightly throughout the day it goes back to usual levels by morning, I think the bloating is causing water retention which is relieved somewhat by the extra water intake.
My fatigue, energy, and digestion seemed to have improved slightly leading me to believe i was slightly dehydrated before.

Anybody have answers for my other questions in this thread?

# Thread: Hello Everyone

**Subforum:** Meet and Greet

### Butters — 2013-11-18T12:24:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.8787/post-100947>

Hello Everyone,

I'm a 23-year-old student from Germany and have been linked to the Paleo/Low-Carb community for 2 years now.

One week ago I stumbled over the blog and since then I had so many aha-moments, it's unbelievable.

I'm studying sports science, so the weight is not my problem, but i'm very interested in the whole CT- and Performance-Thing and longevity. Plus seasonal-eating makes perfect sense to me.

I don't want to feel cold in winter anymore, be sick or weak now and in future and make the best out of my genes. So I start right now.

Greetings form the country of bread and potatoes

### sooperb — 2013-11-18T14:39:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.8787/post-100949>

Hello Butters, I imagine you're going to be blown over by all the stuff in here. I have been around a couple of months and there is just so much to take in that I am never going to get through it. It is great to be among like minded people who don't laugh at the way you choose to live or mutter under their breath that you're strange or just plain mad  and who have been there and tried "it" and are willing to feed back on the good and the bad. I hope you stick around and tell us about your experiences.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-18T15:00:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.8787/post-100951>

welcome.............Bread butter and beer.........not good.

### nonchalant — 2013-11-18T16:04:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.8787/post-100957>

Welcome Butters! It is so wonderful to be able to embrace the cold. I never liked being cold, but I have found that I adapted to CT fairly rapidly. I used to fear getting cold, even if it wasn't freezing outside. Like I knew I would never be able to rewarm myself. I feel real freedom now.

### Butters — 2013-11-18T16:54:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-everyone.8787/post-100960>

> welcome.............Bread butter and beer.........not good.

Forgot the beer...not a huge fan of it but I eat pure butter instead. It drives my friends crazy, when I do this

> Welcome Butters! It is so wonderful to be able to embrace the cold. I never liked being cold, but I have found that I adapted to CT fairly rapidly. I used to fear getting cold, even if it wasn't freezing outside. Like I knew I would never be able to rewarm myself. I feel real freedom now.

I'm excited where this journey leads to. The whole thing is like a big giant playground. First thing I noticed after one week using only cold water for showers, and a little face-donking, is that my skin quality improved drastically. I have had unclear skin since puberty, but now there light. It's amazing. Paleo alone could not solve this problem.

I hope it won't be so difficult to cold adapt with 10-12% bodyfat. Is there special info on the blog for lean/athletic persons?
I'm sorry if my english isn't so good.

# Thread: Where do you get your Seafood (Aussies)?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### elvey90 — 2013-11-18T12:34:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100948>

Hey All,

This is more for Australians (I'm from Melbourne), and was wondering where do you get your seafood(s) from?
I find that to get wild caught you have to go to faraway places (aka dandenong markets) and in terms of costs even farmed grown fish are pretty expensive too (yea I'm on a budget).

I'm also currently getting grass fed beef from Aldi and/or a local butcher, I'm working towards removing fowl/poultry altogether from my diet and incorporate purely grass fed beef, some seasonal veggies (kale, cabbage, broccoli, bok choy & sauerkraut), plenty of seaweed (found a authentic Japanese grocer to get these), free range eggs, and health fats (coconut milk, avocado, coconut oil, ghee), and of course seafood.

So if anyone has any suggestions on where to get affordable seafood or even share what you do so that I can brainstorm some ideas, would be very much appreciated

Cheers!

### caroline — 2013-11-19T00:01:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100981>

Hi Elvey. I get GF beef from Aldi too. What about Lamb? I often get a leg of lamb and throw it in the crockpot overnite and BAB is ready and waiting for me!

Jack has always said "Just do the best you can" sardines are great - in olive oil and canned oysters, mackerel mussels etc. etc.

You can get fish heads etc. at a fish market very cheap and make stock. I get flash frozen sushi grade tuna - in 4oz pieces from a fish market where I am. I just let it thaw slightly and then slice very thinly for sashimi - I roll it in nori etc.

Shrimp \[prawns\] are really easy to get. You must have loads of wonderful fish markets in Melbourne.....

I also get small frozen fish from a chinese store and make a MHS

Pork is great too........If you listen to Jacks last podcasts he talk about this. Also bacon from Aldi - 1Kg for $5.00

### PaulG — 2013-11-19T00:03:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100982>

Yes, I am finding seafood is way more expensive than the UK.

We used to have wild caught Salmon steaks twice a week but not anymore.

However, I try to eat three or four times per week New Zealand green lipped mussels that sell frozen from IGA for $6.20 per kg. They have a very good nutritional profile, high in omega-3, iodine, iron, vitamin a, c, zinc, magnesium - look it up. I also bought a steamer and they take 10mins to cook.

I have also read that the nutritional value is highly degraded from freezing and cooking so supplementing with powder version may be the way to go although Dr. Kruse advocates whole foods over supplements.

Also is it possible for you to take up fishing as a hobby - thats my next move, if you get into sea fishing then you are combining earthing with photoelectric effect just stay out of the sun between 11am and 3pm to avoid the extreme UV.

### caroline — 2013-11-19T00:07:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100984>

Great ideas Paul! Someone - I think maybe Dali ...if I am remembering right ...... does C/T in the ocean and then throws a line in and catches his dinner! and then cooks at the beach.......great multi tasking!!!

Mussels are great and inexpensive. Did you see the seafood stew in the recipe section?

Duck is excellent too - Aldi has Dick now.....it is frozen. I don't worry too much about frozen. It is flash frozen and, in my opinion, much better than the fresh fish hanging around in a supermarket counter.

### elvey90 — 2013-11-19T01:22:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100988>

Thanks for all the suggestions, with purchasing beef and eggs etc it feels easy for me but with seafood totally new with this!
As of right now with my schedule I won't be able to take up fishing and undertake CT in the water (though that sounds like a really awesome idea and actually have considered moving down near the beach areas).

Just a quick question with Aldi brand bacon, aren't they filled with nitrate?

With regards to to frozen seafood, so from the readings, I'm getting the consensus that if you can't get wild caught seafood, it's better to get farmed, frozen, canned etc seafood which is better than getting no seafood at all. I guess I should also supplement with iodine and selenium to combat the mercury levels as well. Am I heading in the right direction?

Will definitely check out IGA and grab some mussels!

Cheers!

### Sammy — 2013-11-19T01:49:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100989>

You can get lots of seafood at Vic market. I normally buy sardines ($6.50/kg), they also sell salmon heads for $1-2 each. Lots of meat and cartilaginous bones. I use it in broth. You can get a kilo of frozen mussels (no shell) for $14. I buy half a dozen of oysters a week for $6. I once asked my butcher and he told me all beef in australia is grassfed. I am not sure whether this is true or not.

### Sammy — 2013-11-19T01:58:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100990>

I read somewhere that the sun has to be 50 degrees or higher in order for the uvb rays to penetrate the atmosphere. ( uvb is required for vit b synthesis). Below 50 degrees and most of the uvb is reflected, and only the UVAs get in. At our latitude... This would correspond to around 11-3... Yet this is the time Sunsmart/ cancer council tells us to stay out of the Sun. Does anyone know anything about this?

### caroline — 2013-11-19T02:04:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-100993>

as far as I can see - all bacon has nitrates- some naturally occurring I think.

Don't drive yourself crazy .... Dr. K. has said that food is only 10% ...... you need to worry about the conditions of your existence much more! non native EMF?

Water is super important for us here in OZ......we need the energy payload from imported water from the N/Hemisphere

What about lamb liver and hearts? excellent nutrient package and cheap!

Have you read the thread in the cave about Aussies and the donut hole?

### Gracelind — 2013-11-19T05:26:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-101000>

In the U.S. bacon that claims to be cured "naturally" without sodium nitrate has usually been cured with celery salt--that has a very high concentration of nitrate.

Vital Choice from Alaska does ship to Australia, though I imagine shipping is pricy. I met the owners at a Weston Price Conference and the products we tasted were quite good. [http://www.vitalchoice.com](http://www.vitalchoice.com)

Fish such as salmon that is frozen immediately after catch may have more nutrients preserved than "fresh" fish that has taken several days to arrive at market. Same with frozen veggies and fruits. [http://www.vitalchoice.com/shop/pc/viewContent.asp?idpage=2#Flash-Frozen](http://www.vitalchoice.com/shop/pc/viewContent.asp?idpage=2#Flash-Frozen)

You have to be careful with canned fish--be sure it's packed by a canner with exceptional standards of hygiene that uses the best parts of the fish.

Bon appetit!

[http://www.vitalchoice.com/shop/pc/viewContent.asp?idpage=2#Flash-Frozen](http://www.vitalchoice.com/shop/pc/viewContent.asp?idpage=2#Flash-Frozen)
>

### nonchalant — 2013-11-19T16:04:47+0200

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-21T23:38:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-101278>

I live within the melbourne metro region, and there isn't any issue finding decent quality Fish and Shellfish. Just yesterday I went over to my usual market and picked up this guy:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" } [](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/photo1-jpg.179/)

Pretty damn fresh NZ salmon harvested and shipped immediately. This one was about 5.3kg, for $15/kg, or $79 for the whole thing which is cheap enough for some great salmon. I immediately chopped it up, sliced up some sashimi, and seared some trimmed off parts of the belly for a quick snack.

In general, I tend to buy whole fish. Like yesterday, I picked up a pike for $4/kg, some cuttlefish for $6/kg, and a couple of jack mackerels at $4/kg. The yield (edible portion by weight) on most of these fish are 70-80% in my experience, which still puts them under $10/kg if you only count the flesh (but I use the heads too!) Sometimes I pick up the prawns too, and in general, you can get pretty decent Queensland prawns at $15/kg (frozen and cooked is just fine). This was all at Preston market, which isn't the best, but is plenty good for my needs.

So yeah, for seafood, making it affordable requires that you're willing to dive into the markets and get your hands dirty.

\---

For offal, again, I know a couple of butchers at the market. Personally, I just look for the quality of organ meats. As you know, they say that a stressed animal will never have a healthy liver, and so if you see spots, uneven colour, weird textures, etc ... I wouldn't buy the liver. That said, most butchers here tend to have decent liver, and I get mine from this stall at the market as well for $2/kg.

For meats, I tend to take Dave Asprey's advice and eat leaner cuts of meat when I'm not sure of the source (toxins usually get stored in the fat). So for beef, stuff like oyster blade, gravy beef (shins), certain cuts of chuck, rump, etc ... I don't usually get lots of pork, mainly because I've had skin issues before from fatty cuts of pork, and usually just stick to loin cuts and bones (bitter melon and pork soup is comfort food for me). Lamb in general doesn't seem to hurt me much, and I just get the stuff from the markets as well, usually $10/kg of lamb leg or loin chops.

\---

I rarely eat poultry nowadays, but the markets also give you easy access to pastured chicken, duck, and turkey on the cheap.

\---

I should also add that homebrand unsalted butter from woolies is usually from New Zealand, which usually means grass fed as well. (the salted one is from Australia though!)

### Kiera2 — 2013-11-22T05:51:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-101308>

elvey90, try making your own ghee. It's so easy, and a much cheaper option.
There are a lot of tutorials online, here's one I just found, and it looks good. [http://www.everydaymaven.com/2013/how-to-make-ghee/](http://www.everydaymaven.com/2013/how-to-make-ghee/)
I don't skim, I just stir a lot until the 'bits' have settled. Be careful not to burn them!

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-22T07:07:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-101312>

> [elvey90 said:](/goto/post?id=101310)
>
> Good suggestion, was contemplating that but honestly where I'm at now, I have little patience for this kind of stuff haha, plus the convenience of having ghee right there and then. Yes something to work on but eventually it would be good to know how to make your own ghee.
>
> Cheers!

Agreed with coriander on making your own ghee. I do it too since butter seems to give me a problematic reaction, and usually melt down 10 sticks of butter at a time, getting about 2kg of ghee 40 minutes later. So yes, it is a substantial upfront investment (which you should never rush by turning up the fire, lest you burn the butter protein), but after that I usually have enough ghee to last 3-4 weeks.

You're right about so-called "pastured" chicken, they're raised on a farm and are free-roaming birds, but you won't really know if they truly are fed on just seeds, nuts and grubs. The only time whereby I saw a vendor ever advertise that was at a farmer's market, and the chicken wasn't cheap!

Good find regarding the pork, and hope that you find some success with all of the food sourcing.

### Kiera2 — 2013-11-22T09:07:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-do-you-get-your-seafood-aussies.8788/post-101323>

'Pastured' chicken doesn't mean they are pasture fed, it means they are free to roam in a pasture. Like at Taranaki Farm: [http://www.taranakifarm.com.au/produce/](http://www.taranakifarm.com.au/produce/) and Polyface Farm: [http://www.polyfacefarms.com/](http://www.polyfacefarms.com/)

### HoneyChild — 2013-11-18T17:36:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-in-is-it-my-field-or-what.8790/post-100964>

I did a basic blood test on Friday (cd22) and got the results today. I'm concerned about quite a few things, I know I should really do more testing but I was hoping that this test would at least give me an idea if my field is a major problem or not.

Basically I'm 31, have no libido and intercourse is painful. I get swollen toes from time to time and this summer developed xanthelasma around my eyes (basically little orange blobs under my skin). This is supposed to be related to oxLDL and heart disease!

I started leptin reset this time last year along with cold showers and occasional cold baths. I've always been naturally slim however I ended up losing more weight than I expected and now I think I'm underweight. Whenever I do cold baths I seem to lose even more which scares me and I stop doing them.

I also started having problems with waking up in the night hot and thirsty but this appears to have stopped since I cut back on the fruit about a month ago.

I do my best to keep technology to a minimum, I work from home, don't have wifi or cordless phones. My magnetic field at night is very low and I have a magnet sitting on the floor under my bed. I wear UVEX glasses in the evening.

The only supplements I take are maca and magnesium spray on my feet.

The problems with where I live are: live couple of flights up, city population about 1.2 million, nearest cell phone masts are about 1/4 mile away. We're desperate to move however I'm struggling to find the idea home away from other houses.

I have some other tests I did earlier in the year and will post them soon.

You talk about your LDL not converting. So you need to look at four things - 1)your vitamin A cycle - go back and re-read hormones 102. & 2) you need to figure out if your making Reverse T3 instead of Free T3. if you're making Reverse T3 it will compete and prohibit any Free T3 your body's making (you've got to find out what's happening and fix this). 3) T4 is converted in the Gut to T3. you've got some gut issues going on that's impacting your thyroid. 4) Problems with your thyroid can directly impact your cortisol, telling your body that it's stressed b/c you don't have enough Free T3 to help Vitamin A to convert LDL to your Sex hormones.

you're in a perpetual do-loop. I'd start there. you can supplement pregnenolone - to help move your forward -but if you don't know whats driving your pregneneelone steal you won't fix the issue...

just my 2 cents...

### QuantumRadical — 2013-11-21T04:48:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101206>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=101180)
>
> I love, love, love this webinar! I haven't gotten all the way thru yet.
>
> This is what I was missing .... basic quantum cell theory.
>

> It seems to me that a lot of these terms are just something scientists latched onto for their NYT crossword puzzle.
>
> To me - Fractal sounds random too .... I thought I understood the meaning but when I looked it up ...I just go - holey cow!

Audrey, fractals are easy. Different phenomena showing remarkably similar patterns. Whirlpool, tornado, spinning galaxy, for example, are completely different phenomena but kind of look the same. They are exhibiting a fractal pattern. Look at a tree's leaf. It often resembles the tree--that is, it has veins that look like tree branches. The tree at is smallest element to its largest have the same pattern (not identical). Fractal!

### yewwei.tan — 2013-11-21T05:12:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101207>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=101180)
>
> I love, love, love this webinar! I haven't gotten all the way thru yet.
>
> This is what I was missing .... basic quantum cell theory.
>
> I don't understand negative entropy ..... if entropy is randomness .......?
>
> I don't really quite understand Fractal either.......does anyone have something other than what wikipedia says?
> It seems to me that a lot of these terms are just something scientists latched onto for their NYT crossword puzzle.
>
> To me - Fractal sounds random too .... I thought I understood the meaning but when I looked it up ...I just go - holey cow!

Regarding Negative Entropy -- entropy means "disorder" or "randomness", and negative entropy therefore means "more ordered" or "less random". Scientists like to use particular terminology , so don't let that trip you up!

Regarding fractals -- an example is the Sierpinski triangle [http://www.oftenpaper.net/sierpinski.htm](http://www.oftenpaper.net/sierpinski.htm) or the Koch Snowflake [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch\_snowflake](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake)

Nature uses fractals; a tree looks like its branches, and the branches on those branches look like more branches. Interestingly, our the capillaries that send blood to all your muscles look just like tree branches. Why? Perhaps it's because similar fractal patterns that a tree can use efficiently gather sunlight apply just as well to a blood vessel trying to send precious blood to hard-working muscles.

This allows nature to use very simple rules to create amazing detail; a tree branch "knows" how to grow itself using it's "parent's" pattern. It's purely a matter of efficiency! But we need a formal language for describing them of course, and hence the mathematical notion of fractal dimensions.

Sidenote: Nassim Nicholas Taleb loves to talk about fractals, how they apply to everyday life (giving credit to the guy who discovered them, Benoit Mandelbrot, who very recently passed away). Taleb's books are a great read, and his most recent 'Antifragile' is probably a long but approachable read.

When a unit (lifeform) do itÂ´s living, walking, talking, living a life in the environment that surround it that unit increase the entropy. When you increase entropy you quickly fast forward to a state of maximum entropy and that is death in different forms. The trip to that place can happend fast and when you reach some kind of level you slowly die more and more. You can be classed as dead as unit but the decay is slow from that point. Think how you felt when you did that crash when it was so warm, the you is 37C and the surroundings was 34C? Not much difference between life and death.

So life is about taking in negative forms of entropy.

When you take a whole day of living, think about what draws energy from you, not just the living you but also the factors from outside you. This is why training and eating less is a good way to kill your life. When you train, you can not think the ordinary way anymore. ### nonchalant — 2013-11-21T20:06:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-november-webinar.8803/post-101270>

I think negative entropy describes what I observed in late summer. All summer long I was happy that I could tolerate the heat so well. Similar to what I experienced last summer, just a bit better. Thanks CT! Then one day I was drenched in sweat. Dripping into my eyes. I was miserable. What was different? I was barefoot...

Then I realized it was a cloudy day. It was Texas hot, but not nearly as hot as the previous day, which was sunny. Without the sunshine, I didn't have the negative entropy, or at least not enough of it, to counteract all the entropy-producing heat!

I would have liked to explore this more, but darn it, the temperatures soon started to get mild. I still find it a bit difficult to believe that a hot sunny day is more tolerable than a hot cloudy day. I'll get to the bottom of this... in about half a year...

# Thread: Need some guidance! (anxiety causing PN)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-guidance-anxiety-causing-pn.8808/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 10

### Frankie L — 2013-11-20T03:52:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-guidance-anxiety-causing-pn.8808/post-101077>

Hi everyone!
I hope someone can help me with his issue. Here's a bit about me...

I am a 40 yr old athletic muscular woman. I work out 3-4 times a week. Eat plenty of greens and no gluten. I am (was) a vegetarian (4 Yrs) and eat low carb diet and and stay away from **red meat and chicken** and NO processed food., I eat fish. I don't drink alcohol or coffee or any other of the no no drinks. **I just started to eat red meat November 19!!!!**

Since January of this year, I've developed moderate anxiety to the point that my the tops of my feet are constantly numb and part of my hands too slightly. I developed anxiety from worrying about my lymphoma coming back, I had it 7 years ago and I am in remission.

I've had numerous blood tests and diagnostic testing that says the cancer is gone - ok I get that but my sympathetic response is not "getting it"....I have permanent numbness and I would like to reverse this. I have no pain, I can live with this but I would like to fix it. I don't believe is RX for anxiety, so when I need a little calmness I take St. John Wort. - that's it because the anxiety is tolerable. I can go weeks without feeling anxious then one day I wake up and boom, there's the SR kicking in for no reason - I think great, those goes my feet vibrating away. My feet and hands vibrate like I'm holding a cell phone! OMG it is really freaky!! What is the cause of this?

I just started taking B12, liquid magnesium, R-ALA and ACL and 2 tablespoons of Coconut oil and a probitoic. I just started to take sea vegetables (liquid iodine), barley grass and beta 3D glucan (for immune boost), Co q10 and selenium.

I understand that PN is caused by myelin deterioration, but my adrenals are going non stop although I am not mentally anxious. My body wants to go into SR first thing in the morning and it feels like I had 3 cups of coffee!! When I go to bed I can feel my hands tremor!!!!!

What would you suggest to return feeling in my feet and hands?
Is Paleo the solution?
Any thoughts would greatly be appreciated!

One other question, what is the vibration in my feet caused by? Is this the nerves firing up or the blood circulating away from the peripherals?

Thank you

Frankie L

### sooperb — 2013-11-23T19:13:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-guidance-anxiety-causing-pn.8808/post-101478>

Hi there. As someone who has endured anxiety attacks for years I sympathise with your plight. I, too, would class myself as someone who isn't a worrier or a stress head yet I have been plagued. I think there is a subconscious response that provokes an anxiety response to stimuli which kicks your adrenals into action and before you know it you are experiencing a panic/anxiety response. I have lost count of the number of different "symptoms" my body has experienced but I can relate to the numbness and the vibration. My anxiety seems to kick in mostly at night, I have palpitations and sweating and many times I had the vibration. The first time I awoke and felt it, I thought it must be my washing machine spinning, it was just that sort feeling, in the centre of my chest and down to my stomach area, I have seen it described as a cellphone type of buzzing. It was some time before I realised it was me and not the bed shaking! It added to my anxiety of course. I have experienced tingling around my jawline, I have lain in bed shaking as though I had a fever and I have had a buzzing sensation in my foot. Numbness and tingling in my labia at the start of a panic attack, the list is endless.

These days nearly all of those things have gone and I credit my earthing sheet with bringing about the change. I still get palps but these tend to be triggered by either alcohol (I'm not a slop head, 1-2 glasses can set them off) MSG, sugar overload or a combination. Knowing the cause is most of the battle and makes it easier to eliminate the effect. I sleep on the sheet every night, it is earthed actually out of the bedroom window and into the earth and I take it with me if I am away from home for more than one night. It might be worth a look for you too.

### caroline — 2013-11-23T21:27:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-guidance-anxiety-causing-pn.8808/post-101487>

I think MSG is huge and it is very hard, if not impossible, to avoid unless you only eat what you yourself make. I made some bone broth the other day and I added in a tiny bit of stock powder \[a brand I have used for years\] and I had a terrible night.

I am not willing to sacrifice a night's sleep for anything if I can help it!

### sooperb — 2013-11-23T22:14:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-guidance-anxiety-causing-pn.8808/post-101496>

I was aghast to find MSG in Bisto and stock cubes. Both seem to have removed it now but I stopped using either and chose organic. I am very careful, we cook pretty much everything from fresh or we check labels if we have to. Eating out is a lot trickier, I haven't dared have a Chinese takeaway for years, they use so much MSG. I have asked in the Indian restaurant if they use it but the proprietor, who we know quite well, half shrugged and half laughed so I am none the wiser. If he thinks I'm not happy he will say they don't use it just as he told me very seriously he would make sure there was less salt in my food. There wasn't of course.

I've watched Asian chefs cooking on TV programmes, they use a huge amount of salt, probably a throw back to when there wasn't much refrigeration. I'm not against salt per se but I'd stake money on it that they aren't using best additive free sea salt lol.

# Thread: In Light of the November Webinar, What about Alfred Pischinger?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2013-11-20T04:04:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/post-101081>

After listening to the November webinar, I am left wondering where Alfred Pischinger and the modern Humoral/Extracellular Matrix school fits in. They identified that the human organism is open thermodynamically and demonstrated quantam effects experimentally I believe. The recent reprint of his work by North Atlantic in 2007 covers most of his significant work. I do not pretend to be an authority and have resisted physics my whole life, yet I get it and I bought Feyneman's QED today. Strangely we had 5 books in our country house in Maine and one of them was "Surely You're joking". I read it every summer, but failed to catch the fire.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-20T05:44:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/post-101083>

Alfred Pischinger was an extension of Claude Bernard original theories. Bernard believed the "environment within", while referring to the extra-cellular fluid environment, and its physiological capacity to ensure protective stability for the tissues and organs of multicellular living organisms. These theories were all forebears of the idea of a morphogenic field without any concept of how it worked or was induced. Gilbert Ling did all the heavy lifting. And when I get to the Quantum Puzzle blog........you'll see how much he really did. You might be shocked to know that after Feynman won his Nobel he took a year off to focus on biology. He always knew QED scaled to biology but he never connect the dots. The reason was he did not read Frolich's work because Frolich left the partical world of the Standard Model and went to the emergent field of physics and began to use coherence and its scales. He was further ahead than any partical theorist. Feynman was a math guy......not a biology guy. The math of biology was not yet invented in Feyman's hey day.........Fractal geometry of Mandelbrot.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-20T05:52:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/in-light-of-the-november-webinar-what-about-alfred-pischinger.8809/post-101084>

Since Mandelbrot this field is now attracting many bright physicists........and my faith is in them and not biology or medicine to fix this mess. Today we now have links from QED built and developed and proved by Feynman to fractal geometry. There are deep connections between quantum and fractal dynamics that are just beginning to be explored. In 1996, Michael Berry at Bristol University in Britain discovered to his surprise that quantum systems exhibit fractal properties. Four years later in 2000, several physicists in Poland constructed fractal solutions of the SchrÃ¶dinger wave equation that are valid for a large class of quantum problems. This stunned me when I read it. It means everything is fractal, the cell, its reactions, , the vortex in the brain, and even time is fractal at its core. Even more fundamentally, French astrophysicist Laurent Nottale showed convincingly that the geometry of space-time becomes fractal when Einsteinâ€™s principle of relativity is extended to scale transformations. When you can make Einstein fractal it is part of the great design.

### Josh — 2013-11-20T04:19:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-101082>

It seems as though PQQ requires other B vitamins as well as d Ribose and Ubiquinol to be present in adequate levels to function. What is the recommended synergy for its use?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-06T21:37:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102618>

Promise Iw ill get to this..............but I just do not have enough out on the blog about redox........Manganese is a big deal and yes it is a transition metal.

### Josh — 2013-12-07T19:58:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102740>

For what it is worth, I muscle tested this and found it to be relevant. Resonant testing using machines such as radionics or EAV based or with the body can be a perilous exercise as it is a challenge to create the proper challenge and then to interpret the data. The people I trust most verify all findings with either kinesiology or an EAV device that tests a single factor or set of factors in a circuit with the subject organism, human or other. The devices that automatically test many factors and circuits and produce reports are always biased by the experience of the people that build and program them. They can produce very useful data, but for me one must always understand the limitation of the programming bias. I use the body because it is more sensitive in my experience and does not need to be plugged in. This being said, I had tried the PQQ in combination with about 100 other single nutrients and formulas, but not manganese. At times it would improve a subject's resonance when combined with ubiquinol, B12 and occasionally d Ribose or Magnesium Glycinate. I am always surprised when it works out this way and many people have questioned such results as being too unreliable as they are subject to the bias of the tester. I have been surprised often enough to know that my bias does not mean much if I am testing properly. Being able to find evidence of effects such as these forces me to go back and try to learn the science to the point where I have a plausible physiological explanation for what I observe/test.

### Josh — 2013-12-08T01:25:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102762>

I took a look at the literature on Manganese and its relationship to PQQ. I am always amazed at the continuing assumption that trace mineral levels are adequate in the environment and food chain and that they are uniformly absorbed and used by people. If we remove those two assumptions which is a reasonable thing to do at this point in history, one would have some real issues without adequate Manganese and or the ability to assimilate and utilize it. Of course there is not much money or interest in studying a mineral that can not be made into a patented drug with high revenue potential so there is not much research. In the FDA we trust...transition element it will be subject to all of the EMF effects that would tend to leave an individual under stress either deficient in it and or unable to utilize what was in his or her system reliably. Without this, several important parts of human physiology are no longer assured to function well. Among other things it appears would be the loss of the enzyme function responsible for PQQ utilization? Curioser and curioser....I wonder if there is a study somewhere showing that while trace amounts are required for normal function, it requires high doses to restore a deficiency condition? I found one study that found this to be the case with Molybdenum. The recesses of my brain where redox chemistry may still live are beginning to take notice...

> Function:
> Manganese is a mineral element that is both nutritionally esse
> ntial and has the potential to be very toxic. This fact is
> further complicated by the small range of dosage for clinical
> benefit and toxicity with serious consequences. Manganese
> is an important factor in many critical biochemical proce
> sses including antioxidant functi
> on. The principle antioxidant
> enzyme within our mitochondria (energy) is superoxide
> dismutase and the enzymes requires manganese for optimal
> performance. Manganese is also required fo
> r normal skeletal development and cartilage synthesis. Wound healing is also
> impacted by manganese, as the synthesis
> of collagen in skin cells is dependent
> on the presence of adequate manganese.
> Manganese is also important functioning as a co-factor in th
> e metabolism of carbohydrates, amino acids and cholesterol.
> Manganese is considered anti-osteoporotic and anti-arthritic.
> Immunologic Effects of Symptoms:
> Reduced T-lymphocyte response to challenge
> and impaired ability to fight infection.
> Deficiency Symptoms:
> Deficiency in manganese is rare, but it is now estimated that
> up to 20% of the population may be deficient in manganese
> caused by improper diet and eating ha
> bits. Deficiency in manganese may lead to
> various health problems which could
> include bone malformation, eye and hearing problems, increa
> sed cholesterol, hypertension, infertility, cardiovascular
> issues, memory loss, hearing loss, muscle cramping and tremor
> s. Other deficiency symptoms may include ataxia, fainting
> and carbohydrate intolerance (diabetes). Manganese de
> ficiency has also been linked to myasthenia gravis.
> Manganese Toxicity: Symptoms of manganese toxicity
> mimic those of Parkinson’s disease with permanent
> neurological damage. It may also precipitate hypertension in
> patients over 40, and significant rises in manganese are
> found in patients with hepatitis, cirrhosi
> s and in dialysis patients and victims of
> heart attack. Early signs of toxicity inclu
> de
> loss of appetite, impaired memory, and mask-like facial e
> xpressions. Excess manganese w
> ill reduce iron absorption.

### Josh — 2013-12-08T05:25:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/essential-cofactors-for-pqq.8810/post-102791>

Thank you the reminder on Spectracell. The most interesting stuff that I have found is the recent research on SLC30A10 which among other things suggests how much is not known about the functions of Manganese. Digging through some other research, I found one interesting study that had a weak, but still interesting correlation between lower fat and or vegetarian diets and Manganese deficiency in spite of sufficient quantities in the diet suggesting lower bioavailability in some food sources. The study also showed that it took 90 days of supplementation to increase levels. I wonder if the trend towards higher iron levels in many people are created competitive inhibition, at least at a functional level with Manganese?
