# Thread: Responses to Ray Peat's Conclusions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 45
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2013-11-22T06:16:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-101309>

If any of you are familiar with Dr. Ray Peat, you probably know that most of his conclusions are in direct opposition with the conclusions of Dr. Kruse. Many of these things are hard for me to refute so I'm hoping the intelligent people of this forum will be able to address some of the key differences between the two and why Peat's conclusions are so different from Jack's. I have a few of my own ideas but they are by no means developed or complete
1\. Ray Peat says ketosis/fat oxidation is bad. He says it releases stress hormones, kills thyroid function, stresses all the cells of the body, and releases free fatty acids which damage mitochondria, promote inflammation and reduce cellular respiration.
2\. A high simple sugar diet is ideal. Sugar metabolizes easily, produces protective CO2, helps produce thyroid hormones, fuels the brain, reduces stress hormones. He essentially considers glucose to be the antistress hormone.
3\. All PUFAs are toxic in all amounts. PUFAs, including dha and EPA from fish, destroy mitochondrial function, cause oxidative stress by creating lipid peroxidation products, cause cancer, damage the brain, damage reproductive tissues, reduce cellular respiration, suppress the immune system, and more. Fatty fish, nuts, all sources of PUFAs should be avoided.

Essentially, stay out of ketosis, burn mostly sugar and avoid all sources of PUFAs, including fatty fish. Here are a few of his articles if you want to read his research in order to better understand his position.
[http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fats-functions-malfunctions.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fats-functions-malfunctions.shtml)
[http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/unsaturated-oils.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/unsaturated-oils.shtml)
[http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fats-degeneration3.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fats-degeneration3.shtml)
[http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/salt.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/salt.shtml)
[http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/unsuitablefats.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/unsuitablefats.shtml)
[http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fishoil.shtml](http://raypeat.com/articles/articles/fishoil.shtml)

I really don't want to give up my PUFAs from seafood, but if anyone is able to convince me that DHA is poisoning my brain, cells, and mitochondria it would be Peat.

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-22T15:29:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-101345>

QiGuy

Watch this video from Dr. Cunnane about DHA [http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8uroLRUsEEQ](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8uroLRUsEEQ)

### bio-fractal-soul-self — 2015-03-01T08:31:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-156969>

Dr. Kruse is right on the money with most of everything he says; especially everything in the CT and BG series, but that being said, anyone who hasn't thoroughly read Dr. Peat's work, particularly his older work and his books, is doing a great disservice to themselves. You don't have to accept every last thing someone says. For instance, I think epi-paleo ketosis works for me, and has the most direct and indirect (and observational and anecdotal) evidence behind it, as opposed to any other diet, really. _Especially in our modern nnEMF+blue light rich environment.
_
But, Dr. Peat has been speaking about things like nnEMF, static magnetic treatment devices, magnetized super cooled water, solar radiation and how solar and lunar cycles and changes alter human behavior and brain physiology, since about 1970. That's about 45 years now. He also went to Russia to study with some of the best Russian neurologists, neuro-anatomists/physiologists, gerontologists, sociologists, etc. and he wrote about their research on brain encephalization and things of that sort in his books. Furthermore, everyone here is aware of Gilbert Ling. Want to know something interesting? Dr. Peat and Gilbert Ling have been in personal contact for decades, Ling has openly stated that Dr. Peat's work on 'the organism' and cellular energetics is fantastic, and lastly, **Gilbert Ling and Dr. Peat are going to be in a documentary together** soon, along with the likes of Mae Wan Ho. So, he's really no quack. His older work is just better, and he seems to have had a lot of favorable experiences with things he experiments with personally, so he probably has some biases.... though everyone does.

_Also, “If cancers were werewolves, the cancer industry’s search for more refined killing technologies might be going in the right direction. But the genetic doctrine of cancer’s origin is just as mythical as werewolves and vampires.”
\-Ray Peat, PhD

What's not to love about that, lol?
_
I hope my statements don't give off any false notions; I'm a firm believer in everything we all discuss here, and not one of those "Kruse vs. Peat" people - I like to study everyone and gather as much as possible, and Dr. Kruse is currently - by far - ahead of most everyone there is, but that doesn't mean when it comes to people with seemingly "opposing" ideas that we have to throw the baby out with the bathwater.

### NeilBB — 2015-03-01T18:41:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-156983>

I'm not sure about Peat specifically, but I know that even the greatest and most experienced thinkers become susceptible to over-generalizations of principles and false-conclusion drawing due to incomplete information or the inability or unwillingness to control for all the variables. They also become invested, both emotionally and economically over time in specific ideas and therefore become less amenable to change when presented with novel info that conflicts with their prior conclusions.

Translation: "You can't teach an old dog new tricks." Some truth to that, unfortunately. It's how we seem to be built...

I agree strongly, don't ever throw out the baby with the bathwater. The most important thinkers in history have always been those who have been able to distinguish the babies from the old bathwaters, and build upon that base. It is essential for progression of knowledge...

-

Wisdom is like a turning wheel in this case.............the more you ask in curiosity, the faster the destination appears if you follow the path of the wheel.........Few do this consistently, because it is a deficiency in thinking. -   People who fail in this application get results, and their life suffers from these results...........or you do because you followed them with out discernment.

-   Dogma of experts, slows the wheel or progress in any field.........and when you are aware of this.......you can plan for it in how you study any topic. And let others decide who is right.........The mark of an educated mind is to consider something you really don't believe and then decide the course of action.

### Jack Kruse — 2015-03-01T20:00:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-157012>

"Your ignorance cannot become dead by accumulating knowledge. You can accumulate all the
knowledge available in the world, you can become an Encyclopedia Britannica, but that won’t help.
You can become a walking encyclopedia, but your ignorance will not be dead through that. Rather,
on the contrary, your ignorance will become hidden, secret; it will move to the deep recesses of the
heart. So on the surface you will be knowledgeable and deep down you will remain ignorant. This is
what has happened, and all the universities go on helping this.
Your ignorance is never dead; it is alive, working. And just on the surface you are decorated, you
are a painted being. Your knowledge is painted just on the surface and deep down you remain
ignorant. The knowledge, real knowledge, can happen only when the ignorance is dead. Before
that, knowledge will remain information – borrowed, not yours, not authentic – it has not happened
to you. It is not a lived experience, but only words, verbal, scriptural."
Vedanta: Seven Steps to Samadhi-Osho

Osho

> is a mode of life, not a question of information. It is a way of life, a wrong way of life, that creates
> ignorance. It is not just a question of memory, of how much you know, or how much you don’t know
> – that is not the point."
>
> Osho
>
> Via Josh Ferber........very appropriate for this thread. There is a deep lesson here. I'm not interested in being liked........just correct. And my curiosity always is my best critic.

And we need people like you now more than ever. To give us absolute power over our health!

### Brett Jacques ND — 2015-03-03T17:20:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-157302>

This thread is a good one as it allows us to further refine the message of science and philosophy. How many times have we chased an idea, a scientific concept etc. down a rabbit hole only to come up empty and unfulfilled? I still have this happen. We are structuring our base of knowledge to interpret the context of everything to adapt to today. Love it!

### Tracy Guild — 2017-05-21T18:35:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-214380>

No guru is ever 100% right. If they were, they would never change and grow and therefore would not be able to manage a changing world. I am less discouraged by the differences of opinion and more encouraged by the cross over agreements. Peat is a genius and I have learned so much reading his work on hormones, thyroid, liver and cells. I find so much value in it and validation of Kruse's work regarding biophysics. Peat is understated, and I think most people make generalizations from his work without reading the bulk and really listening to or understanding his meaning. They are in total agreement regarding sun, circadian biology, the ever important relationship between energy and water and emf. Peat advocates eating oysters and shellfish, organ meats. The main difference is the discussion about carbohydrates/fats and Kruse often says this depends on your n=1, so I don't at all see why these perspectives cannot exist in the same discussion. In several years time, Kruse helped me to get rid of arthritis, improve my vision and sleep better. Peat has recently helped me to overcome estrogen dominance and improve my thyroid function. I am keeping both. Love to both and to everyone trying to get their healthiest. Read all the things.

### Cpt.Tired — 2017-05-24T05:01:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-214544>

Tracy I love your positive attitude about this emerging science!

### Cpt.Tired — 2017-05-24T05:09:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/responses-to-ray-peats-conclusions.8834/post-214545>

> [NeilBB said:](/goto/post?id=156983)
>
> I'm not sure about Peat specifically, but I know that even the greatest and most experienced thinkers become susceptible to over-generalizations of principles and false-conclusion drawing due to incomplete information or the inability or unwillingness to control for all the variables. They also become invested, both emotionally and economically over time in specific ideas and therefore become less amenable to change when presented with novel info that conflicts with their prior conclusions.
>
> Translation: "You can't teach an old dog new tricks." Some truth to that, unfortunately. It's how we seem to be built...
>
> I agree strongly, don't ever throw out the baby with the bathwater. The most important thinkers in history have always been those who have been able to distinguish the babies from the old bathwaters, and build upon that base. It is essential for progression of knowledge...

### Raiken3712 — 2013-11-22T07:33:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insurance-deadline-tommorrow-need-help.8835/post-101313>

I can't get insurance at Walgreens after November 22 and I think thats my best option.

I have no clue what to get.

I wish there were an easy way to link all my options so people could give me ideas and thoughts....

My choices are Aetna, BCBS, UnitedHealthcare Bronze, Bronze Plus, Silver, Gold and Platinum

Bronze has 2,750 Deductible while Platinum has none the costs are much higher though with it.

Also Platinum seems to need In Network providers and the places I want to go like

[http://paleophysiciansnetwork.com/doctors/TX/Irving/maximal-health](http://paleophysiciansnetwork.com/doctors/TX/Irving/maximal-health)

are not In Network.

I've heard sometimes you don't have to meet the deductible for certain things, but I'm not sure what...I really have no clue.

The price for Bronze plans is an average of 15 dollars every two weeks with the subsidy they give us.

The platinum which I'm sure is a horrible option for me is 126 dollars.

Should I just pick the cheapest to avoid the Obamacare tax?

Also for Dental I have Bronze at 1,000 annual maximum Deductible is 100...sounds low I don't understand this at all...nothing like the medical here.

Bronze price is around 4.00 per period.

Platinum is around 50 cents....with credits and no deductible or annual maximum...that actually sounds good...I have to missing something.

Aetna, UnitedHealthcare, Metlife, and DeltaCare USA Platinum are the choices.

Vision also doesn't sound that bad Bronze is from 1.00 per period to Platinum which is 3.60.

Bronze is Exams covered in full. Frame discount and lenses discount.

Gold is 10.00 copay for exams 200 frame allowance 10 dollar copay for lenses no platinum option.

Choices are VSP, Metlife, UnitedHealthcare

I should have done this sooner...I know, but if anyone has any input I really need it.

Thanks

I haven't wrangled in my junk eating habits...but I haven't had anything major happen healthwise..

I am constantly tired, my knees hurt from inflammation and a bicycle accident that never healed probably, and my lower back hurts occasionally.

I think I might be able to deal with most of my issues by starting to eat better and exercise, but I have to sign up for insurance because of Obamacare so I want to make the right choices.

# Thread: Frankie L's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frankie-ls-journal.8839/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Frankie L — 2013-11-22T16:56:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frankie-ls-journal.8839/post-101359>

Well here goes...this is my first time really journalling. So I will provide some brief medical and personal history so we have a context of what I am dealing with. Here is my profile:

Athletic Muscular female
40 years old
Tall, Medium Build
I work out 2-4 times per week at he gym - cardio and weight training
No issues with energy.

**Diet**

Vegetarian for 5 years until November 19, 2012 **Had grass feed red meat!**
Started to eat salmon of March 2013 about 1-2 times a week
Don't eat PUFA oils
Lots of olive and flax seed oil
Still ate eggs while a vegetarian about 2 - 3 per day.
Eliminated Dairy accept for organic 2% cottage cheese and flax oil combined
Don't drink pop, coffee, juice only herbal tea (no caffiene)
Only eat Ezekiel Bread and don't eat any carbs with gluten
May occassional have a cookie or some dessert
Don't drink alcohol
Drink plenty of home-made smoothies such has kale, spinach and swiss chard
Drink Reverse Osmosis water with added trace minerals (I do this)
Don't eat fruit accept for raspberries and blueberries
Eat lentils and chickpeas NO RICE
Drink almond Milk
Eat alot of avacados
Eat 4 tablespoons of Organic coconut oil a day
**Just started to eat read meat as a start to PAELO**

**Medical History**

Cancer:
2006 Diagnosed with Non-Hodgkins Lymphoma Folicular B cell
Told it was incurable
Did 8 weeks of CVPR and 2 Years of Rituxan
Tumour came back in 2010
Did 5 weeks of back-to-back radiation-tumour disappeared
Decided to take matters in my own hands and go natural DID A TON RESEARCH OVER A FEW YEARS
Tumour came back in my neck
Changed diet and started to take supplements to change my chemistry
All tumours are gone and I am in complete remission
I will never ever do chemo or radiation again knowing there are other safer alternatives

Anxiety:
Woke up in January of 2013 worrying incessantly about the lymphoma coming back.
Chronic worry, can't stop, was wondering what the heck happened over night?
This persisted for several months and was at it's peak in March of 2013.
Hands started to tingle and feet
Vibration in legs and feet that would come and go.
Slight tremour in my peripherals espeically in the morning.
Developed numbness in hands and tops of my feet.
Self diagnosed PN.
Stopped worrying about my issues, my body still continues in the "sympathetic state"??
Take St.John's wort if anxiety becomes intolerable.
I feel my adrenal glands are taxed and need a break.

**Daily Supplmentation**

Cancer protocol:
B12, 10,000 iu of D3, Beta 3D glucan, Barely grass, CO Q10 and selenium, vitamin multi-pack

Eyes:
Vision essentials -bilberry and luten (really helps!)

Anxiety and Periperal neuropathy:
Just started with protocol about 2 weeks ago.
Liquid Magnesium, 4 tablespoons of coconut oil, Reservatrol, Krill oil, L-Carnitine, R-Alpha Lipoic Acid, Liquid Iodine drops, Liquid B complex

Overall Health:
Sea vegetables (contains iodine), probiotic,

**Non-Native EMF**
Eliminated cell phone from bedroom and unplugged lamp.
Removed landline phone as well.
Room is dark with no artifical light.
Have 2 alarm clocks but are positioned away from the bed
Cell phone is turned off when I 'm not using it. Never in my pocket.

Ok, can't think of anything else at the moment.

### caroline — 2013-11-23T00:56:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/frankie-ls-journal.8839/post-101409>

Some early thoughts Frankie ......you need to get rid of the alarm clocks. Can you turn off the breaker to your bedroom?

Do you have a smart meter? do your neighbours have a smart meter? Do you use a microwave? It should be unpluged.

Have you considered starting the Leptin reset - using the epi paleo template? C/T will be huge for you.

There are easy start guides on the home page.

You really need to clean up your diet ....ditch the pulses and the ezekial bread and the fruit for now.

just eat simply from the epi paleo template.

Don't hesitate to ask lots of questions ..... there is a ton of stuff here to take on board.

If you can manage - join us for Dr. Tim Jackson's "Heal your hormones" bootcamp. He is giving us an awesome amount of information and he is very easy to understand. Have you listened to his free webinar?

As I said - keep reading and asking questions .... there are lots of great people here to help!

Thanks for starting a journal and sharing all that with us - have a look at other's journals when you have time .....may people are stuggeling with all kinds of stuff and they could benefit from your experience too.

That wasn't so hard was it?

### Inger — 2013-11-22T18:00:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-testosterone.8842/post-101373>

Nude tanning in real sunshine, no sunscreen. It must be completely nude, whole body. As much as you can. It is awesome for rising testosterone naturally... IMHO the most effective single thing you can do. Do it in fall and spring too in addition to whole summer.

CT. Icebaths and also just having it colder overall, like mild CT. Also an amazing tool... in combination with minding light cycles/avoiding fake light after sunset and a ketogenic diet.

There are some supps/herbs that are said to raise testosteron if it is low, like Ashwagandha, Boron, tribulus-terrestris... etc. but IDK if they work. I would go for the two above. I bet earthing and being in the nature are amazing too! And yes some heavy lifting once in a while should help too....

### Huck — 2013-11-26T13:35:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-testosterone.8842/post-101671>

Understand that this is my interpretation of what I have learned here, backed up by my experience (n=1).

You can try to supplement your way to better health, but without regular labs and the knowledge to interpret them, it is likely to be a waste of money. Some things, like vitamin d, are easy to supplement and easy to test, at least in US. Other things, like testosterone, are more challenging. I had serious problems when, with my doctor, we had tried to raise testosterone through supplements. But I was able to raise my testosterone just by getting lots of sun, without the problems I had with supplements.

The long term (18-24 months) approach is to follow Jack's protocols and get your "supplements" from natural foods. What many of us have seen is small changes that add up to big changes over time. The next 24 months will happen whether you follow Jack's prescriptions or not, so why not follow them and see what happens?

You need electrons and photons. Spend lots of time in the sun and ground whenever you can. Drink spring water and eat organic food. Minimize emf. I think you and I have the same problem with cold. I can't stand to be immersed in cold water, but spot icing isn't a problem. Maybe that is what you can start with.

I wonder if a trip to Stonehenge would help? At a minimum, it would help you focus on possibilities!

I check my magnessium level using Spectracell micronutrient test.

Magnesium threonate ------gets to brain & spinal cord ///morning during lighted hours
Magnesium orotate (big one) key for ATP synthesis, (from cardiovascular surgery literature)----------- lighted hours, --------------200-400 mgs a day------
Magnesium Malate------gets to skeletal muscles and cardiac muscles 800mg/bed time

.............

**fermented cod liver oil blend 750mg x2 daily**

Same goes for sources of LA and ALA.

> Oestradiol - 75 (norm 50-218)
> Prolactin - 289 (norm 40-360)

> SHBG - 19.12 (norm 14-48)
> Testosterone - 7.3 (norm 8-29)
>
> I also had a MRI on my pituitary and my clinician says that there is no pituitary adenoma but the gland itself is rather bulky with patchy enhancement
> The consultant want to put me on Tostran Gel 3 gm daily
>
> I have also recently had high levels of c-reactive protein which I am having another blood test this week to check my levels again
>
> \---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
>
>
>
> Tostran 2% Gel. One gram of gel contains 20 mg testosterone.
> [http://www.drugs.com/uk/pdf/leaflet/171307.pdf](http://www.drugs.com/uk/pdf/leaflet/171307.pdf)
> \----------------
> Being on this board, first reflex is to examine your lifestyle and environment and adjust it the best you can to follow natures laws as explained to us by dr Kruse.
> Second line of defense is to use help of medical advisers that have a mind set in tune with dr Kruse' view. Dr Kruse arranged for us booth-camp with dr Tom Jackson, it is not to late to join it.
> Third line would be to use all this what we have learned here, to evaluate advice that we are getting from our local medical advisers.
> \-----------------
> You may have pituitary problems, but in the mean time your LH @ FSH are highish, but your testicles are not responding as expected.
> Tostran Gel 3 gm daily=60mg testosterone on the skin looks like good starting point,
> but
> should add HCG-shot=250iuEOD to prevent testicular and scrotum atrophy.
>
> 6 weeks to three months latter is good time to do more thorough testing. (I can expand on your goals then, plus, within week or so dr Tim Jackson will be talking about this subject).
>
> You may want to check what I test here:
> [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6427-PG-E2-ratio&p=81508#post81508)
> \-------------------
>
> OTOH keep eye on your very high HSCRP. You should make attempt to figure out where it is coming from, then you may have a better chance for relief.

>
> I am not a doctor.
>
>
>
> ..

The endo has told me when i go back in january he can look at injections.

One point i would like to make is that i used to get up every morning and take the dogs for a 5-7 mile walk everyday. Could walking to excess be why my testosterone is low now
as i feel extremley tired walking the dogs 2 miles now?

I will look at the tom jackson bootcamp shortly.

The other types may have value but I will consider it secondary (plus, loading on some of them will loosen up stool, sometimes too much.

I use results of those analysis and am still in the process of massaging my data, using spreadsheet, to get as close as I can to what dr Kane is doing.

Many people (you and me are in this group), were eating fish oils and krill oils and possibly other oils and tons of oily nuts.
When I learned about what dr Kruse proposes, I dropped all that supplementation and loaded on seafood.
Now I think that it was mistake.
There need to be a phase, I still do not know how long, where we should abstain from eating seafood, just to give our body time to process all past excesses of EPA & DHA that we got by eating fish oils and krill oils and the other oils.

### nonchalant — 2013-12-08T17:13:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-testosterone.8842/post-102861>

Usually a chelated magnesium will have two numbers. The mg of the total dose, mg and chelate combined, and also the mg of just the magnesium alone, the lesser amount. You can usually find that on the nutritional info panel, with the %DV.

# Thread: Conference: Transcriptional Regulation in Development
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/conference-transcriptional-regulation-in-development.8844/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2013-11-22T22:40:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/conference-transcriptional-regulation-in-development.8844/post-101397>

Hmmm . . . Don't see anything here about QED.
Plus, it's disconcerting if it's a novel event to "bring together researchers working in developmental biology with those studying the specific molecular and cellular mechanisms of transcriptional regulation."

Back in my more-naive days and before this journey on the medical self-education I never really wanted to have, I used to believe that scientific research was coherent and inclusive.

**Conference: Transcriptional Regulation in Development**
[http://www.cell-symposia-transcriptional-regulation.com/](http://www.cell-symposia-transcriptional-regulation.com/)

_It is clear that cell fate decisions and developmental processes both drive and are driven by changes in transcriptional regulation. Over the years, insights from developmental biology have fueled advances in our understanding of transcription and chromatin while studies of transcription mechanisms have provided powerful tools and concepts for understanding some of the molecular events underlying developmental biology. Our understanding of key concepts such as regulation by non-coding RNAs, histone modifications, pluripotency, differentiation, and epigenetics would likely be rather limited without the ongoing dialogue between these disciplines.

For this symposium, we aim to continue to bring together researchers working in developmental biology with those studying the specific molecular and cellular mechanisms of transcriptional regulation. We hope that this meeting will provide a forum for scientists to challenge assumptions about whether a seemingly important detail of enzymology is in fact biologically relevant and to discuss approaches to identifying molecular basis for interesting but poorly understood biological phenomena._

No QED mentioned here, either:

[http://www.cell-symposia-humanevolution.com/index.html](http://www.cell-symposia-humanevolution.com/index.html)

**Cell Symposium: Evolution of Modern Humans - From Bones to Genomes**
March 16 - 18, 2014, Hotel MeliÃ¡, Sitges, Spain

# Thread: Optimal/Protocol for Social Eating
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-protocol-for-social-eating.8847/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### elvey90 — 2013-11-23T03:53:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-protocol-for-social-eating.8847/post-101428>

Hey All,

On Dave Asprey's page he has a protocol on what to do if you 'accidentally' eat crappy foods (can't find the link someone please find it  ).
Was wondering what everyone does when they eat non Epi-Paleo foods when they go out on social gatherings, please feel free to share.

Just to make it easier to categorize, here's a list:

1) What you do before heading out
2) What you do during the social event
3) What you do after the social event

Please keep in mind that this is what you do if there isn't any or limited Epi-Paleo foods at the event.

What I personally do:

1) Before heading out:
\- If I don't feel like eating at all, I'll have a high protein and fat meal about 1hr before I have to head out, that curbs any hunger.
\- If I don't have time, I have a 1-2 cups of coconut milk mixed with raw/organic cacao powder and if I have Maca powder I add that in too.

2) During the social event:
\- Try and find Epi-Paleo foods
\- I used to bring a lemon, cut in half and squeeze into a cup then drink it without water before I eat, might get some funny looks
\- I don't eat at all and drink tea or water

3) After the social event:
\- If I ate I would drink 2 glasses of water with 1x lemon with manuka honey
\- Take Activated Charcoal (mentioned on Dave Asprey's site), as soon as the meal is consumed.

It'll be good to grab if anyone wants to share their protocol if you have one

### caroline — 2013-11-23T06:18:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-protocol-for-social-eating.8847/post-101440>

I always eat something before going out, even if only a BPC. It is really hard to make informed picks if you are really hungry.

A dinner party is hard....I still eat before hand and, if necessary, just move food around on a plate. Now I just bring something I can eat and share.

Anyway. - it isn't the end of the world.....people are more important than food. Just do what you need to do (eat) and move on.

But - for me - I can't tolerate gluten very well and definitely can't do MSG ..... And people get that.

Always call a restaurant the day before and explain your situation. A lot of people eat paleo these days .....

At my tiny farmers market - I asked for chicken liver and they told me it always sells out first because there are so many paleo people. I was pretty surprised!

We should never be afraid to ask for what we need .....that is just plain dumb!

The one thing I do find ...friends worry that I eat too much protein. They don't get the high fat, medium protein, low carb thing. But having said that - they think everyone needs some sugar......

When I was in central Asia travelling - I was asking for butter for my tea and everyone thought that was pretty cool. in the long run it was the vodka that got me...LOLl

### Inger — 2013-11-23T09:41:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-protocol-for-social-eating.8847/post-101455>

Ignore the food and put your interest and focus on people. Easy  No one will feel bad because everyone likes to be recognized

+

I bring my own food
or eat before I go
or pick up something from a shop on the way to eat
or eat only the stuff that is acceptable for me (you will mostly know before the event what kind of food there will be)

I never eat bad stuff. The only things I cheat with on such occasions are; HWC, raw cheeses, salad or some veggies, berries, nuts, cooked seafood and meat (I eat raw at home)

Help cleaning up, serving, etc. I always say I can be the waitress...lol Or look for the children when their parents are eating. There is always stuff/work to do on parties!

This is actually not an issue at all when we stop focusing so much on food and focus more on person = love

### prAna303 — 2013-11-23T09:58:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-protocol-for-social-eating.8847/post-101458>

> 1) What you do before heading out

I do nothing special, often the event is at night so food is not a real problem for me. Often i eat my last meal quite early which do not disturb my way of living. My days now, because it is winter here, eating is about 1000 grams of seafood / fatty fish, up to 500 grams of lard, some eggs and water from a stream in the mountains.

> 2) What you do during the social event

For me food has always been about fixing my offset, this i learned during the last eight years. So for me food does not mean anything at a party, i do not have feelings for it, so i do not look at that corner. For me the event is always about connecting, this is what i really feed off, human vibrations. When you connect with others, food and the cravings for it goes away. This is getting tougher and tougher today because of all this iPhone, smartphone updating illness that struck the world, but i always manage to attract a crowd that will resonate.

> 3) After the social event:

Depends, if it was a meaningful event, i suck up on the feelings, let them feed me. If it was a EMF hell, i go out in the wild and sleep outside. If it is summer i simply go freediving, to make the offset in me.

# Thread: Please help - having a real snafu calculating bun/creatinine ratio.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-having-a-real-snafu-calculating-bun-creatinine-ratio.8850/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Horse — 2013-11-23T07:08:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-having-a-real-snafu-calculating-bun-creatinine-ratio.8850/post-101443>

Would someone kindly help me out with this one thing? My Bun (Urea) is 6.7mmol/L (Ref 2.5 - 9.0) and my Creatinine is 55 umol/L.(Ref 40 - 100) I understand I need to have both readings in the same units so I converted the umols to mmols (won't put conversion here as I don't wish to pre-empt). I then thought it was a simple matter of dividing the Bun by the Creatinine to get the ratio; but I come up with ridiculous numbers!!

If someone is able to help; could you please spell out the steps of the calculation as well as the answer (ie the ratio) please? I'd be **so grateful.**Thank you.

### Horse — 2013-11-23T08:53:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-having-a-real-snafu-calculating-bun-creatinine-ratio.8850/post-101452>

PS. After 2 days of pulling my hair out with this; I've just realised that the reference range for Bun/Creatinine ratio (10-20:1) is different to the Urea/Creatinine ratio (as this ratio is in SI Units and is 40-100:1). Therefore JK's ideal of the ratio needing to be under 10 applies to the former. A ratio of under 40 would seem to be the ideal for the latter? I understand the actual calculation method (formula to arrive at the ratio) would be the same; but the ratios themselves don't correlate due to the fact that SI values are proportionately greater resulting in a higher comparative ratio. I hope this makes sense; but I would still appreciate the help please. I simply had not realised there'd be a complication with the ratio if using different units to the US. BTW I realised all of this from finally finding [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BUN-to-creatinine\_ratio](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BUN-to-creatinine_ratio) THANKS AGAIN!

### HoneyChild — 2013-11-23T12:19:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-having-a-real-snafu-calculating-bun-creatinine-ratio.8850/post-101466>

I used these calculators for my own results and hope that I did the correct thing...

[http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Creatinine.php](http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Creatinine.php)
[http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Urea\_nitrogen.php](http://www.endmemo.com/medical/unitconvert/Urea_nitrogen.php)

6.7mmol/L = 18.77 mg/dL
55 umol/L = 0.62 mg/dL

18.77 divided by 0.62 = 30.27 = seriously dehydrated!!

### Horse — 2013-11-24T00:13:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/please-help-having-a-real-snafu-calculating-bun-creatinine-ratio.8850/post-101504>

Thank you so much for that HoneyChild - so greatly appreciated. However, I have to confess my head is still done in on the matter. I'd posted further information quite close on the heels of my first query above; and I got a message that it had to be checked by a moderator before being posted but it never showed up. I can't understand why. It would have been great for you to see that further information before you replied...

Basically, the further information was that I'd just found in Wikipedia a sentence which really set the cat among the pigeons. It would be worth you taking a look. [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BUN-to-creatinine\_ratio](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BUN-to-creatinine_ratio) Basically, the sticking point is the second sentence of the first paragraph (containing the bolded words) - " The units of creatinine are also different (μmol/L), **and this value is termed the urea-to-creatinine ratio.** The ratio may be used to determine the cause of acute kidney injury or dehydration." So if this is correct, then converting the creatinine (in my case 55 umol/L) to mg/dL and then using that in the equation would be totally invalid. Wikipedia seems to be emphatically saying that my creatinine value of 55 IS ACTUALLY THE RATIO!!!?? (The range for the ratio in SI units is 40 - 100:1 whereas the range for the ratio in US Standard Units is 10 - 20:1). I understand that JK is saying the ideal would be for it to be under 10 in Standard Units. After having pulled my hair out for days only to come across this info has really confused me no end. What I'm saying is that your methodology seems to be spot on; but if Wikipedia is correct; we're seriously thwarting/deranging the result. I hope this makes sense.

On the other hand, if my ratio is indeed 30.27 which you kindly calculated for me, it's so terribly dismaying as I've been drinking around 3 litres of pure filtered water a day for years. I understand that it's the quality of the water which is the critical factor but it's dismaying nonetheless.

I really do hope this makes some sense to you (and anyone else seeing this) and that this post shows up successfully!

# Thread: Muscle Repair success
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-success.8852/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 19
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2013-11-23T16:16:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-success.8852/post-101473>

SO Last week I tore my calf muscle. quite severely, initially thought I tore my achilles tendon but didn't I've been pushing all of JAck's protocols hard. Yesterday I went into the chiropractor - to get adjusted and told her what happened. When she examined my calf - she was surprised how much healing had already occurred. in just 10 days from the injury.

She could tell where the tear happened - but she was surprised at how quickly things are repairing. She's never seen anyone heal from a tear as sever as I had - I basically shredded the both calf muscles; she said with all previous patients they needed crutches/canes for 3-4 weeks before being able to walk w/o assistance. She asked me what else was I doing besides Icing/CTing (She calls me her Ice client b/c of all the CT I do) she attributed the fast healing to CT, diet, and hydration.

she told me I needed to bottle it and sell what every I was doing - I told her its all out there on JAck's website. She thinks I'm already at 85% healed in only 10 days. I'm going to continue limiting my leg workouts for the next month but get back in the grove for leg work in Jan but I'll keep doing all of JAck's protocols.

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-11-23T18:31:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/muscle-repair-success.8852/post-101477>

Great job on your rehab!! Of course we know that Jack's protocalls work. Your recovery is a great example.

As I read your post, the first thought I had was ... where all the things you do correctly, why did the injury happen? .... Most likely your field (maybe more incentive to make the move your want?)

Finally, I suggest making sure your calves are totally healed before resuming workouts. Likely going to be some scar tissue there. You might want to look into the Graston Technique. I recently started Graston through a chiropractor. In a short period of time, it has broken up scar tissue that massage therapy didn't impact. [http://grastontechnique.com/LocateaProvider.html](http://grastontechnique.com/LocateaProvider.html) .... Google 'Graston Technique' and you can find some good videos that show how it works.

### Shijin13 — 2013-11-23T20:33:49+0200

I recently started Graston through a chiropractor. In a short period of time, it has broken up scar tissue that massage therapy didn't impact. [http://grastontechnique.com/LocateaProvider.html](http://grastontechnique.com/LocateaProvider.html) .... Google 'Graston Technique' and you can find some good videos that show how it works.

THanks ATL Paleo - I was warming up to work out when I tore my calf muscle. The only difference btwn my normal workouts and the one when I injured myself - was I didn't CT before I tore it. I usually CT then Work out.... but I didn't have time that day.

I'll check out the grastontechnique and check w/ my chiro...

### Inger — 2013-11-23T21:09:52+0200

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-11-23T21:34:39+0200

# Thread: a few questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions.8853/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Power98dan — 2013-11-23T17:44:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions.8853/post-101476>

Hello all

I started reading about Dr Kruse and Leptin reset on the MDA monster thread. Not sure how I got there Read not all post but quite a few, then of course I was led here and I have again read a lot. I think I have the basics down, 50plus grams of protein for breakfast, less then 30 grams of carbs if you have more then 30 lbs to lose, no snacking, try and wait 4-5 hrs between meals, spread your carbs thru out the day, don't be afraid of good fats. I should also state I am around 90 lbs over weight, very familiar with paleo and have ate this way off and on for a couple of years. I also have tried Bullet Proof diet with BPC and IF. It worked but... My problem always leads back to sustainability, thus I think if I can get thru the reset, i can sustain this way of eating vs BPC everyday (although I do like BPC).

So far no issues, sometimes a little light headed or carb flu symptoms, but not many really (any sups to aide this if necessary?) I am fortunate I don't really have any issues other then fat. I guess one more issue would be poor sleep, some of this is caused by Rotor cuff injury more on that later, but some is just poor sleep

Thus some questions

1\. I assume if I still want BPC in the am with MCT oil or butter or CO this is OK as long as I eat my protein and don't snack?
2\. I assume I could eat a BPC for lunch, could this be in place of protein if this got me thru to dinner?
3\. Leptin sensitivity, I am still a little confused at how I can tell if my Leptin has been reset. I get the no cravings, after a very solid week of eating this way I have no cravings, but the sweating issue I am not sure if I am suppose to sweat more or less or how I am suppose to tell, I stopped exercising as the Dr suggest, thus how can I tell?
4 Sometimes I don't want any carbs period, but sometimes i do want a piece of fruit, i know fruit is not the best choice during reset, but if carbs are still less then 30 is this ok?

Been lurking for about a week, so far a pound a day weight loss

Side question back in May I lifted the trash the wrong way, and I tore a rotor cuff, went to chiropractor he agrees with my diagnoses and said it could take a long time to heal, and if it did not you may need an operation. It has been 6 months and it is not really getting much better. I don't want to get operation if at all possible. It feels better if I ice it, or apply DSMO, or if I take motrin or such, but I can't live on motrin.

Is there any supplements or anything at all I can eat or do that might help?

Final question, so much information on the website, would like to start reading some of the blogs and even maybe a webinar, again no side issues with health, most interested in weight loss and sleep any suggestions what blog post, webinars or podcast to start with?

Thanks in advance for your answers.

Dan

### caroline — 2013-11-23T21:58:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions.8853/post-101491>

Hi Dan and welcome! That is a lot of questions! yikes!

firstly - please start a journal .... it gives Dr. Kruse context and is great for commitment. Please tell us about yourself....
To find the journal section ... go to the top of this page and click on forum and then go down 5 categories and you will see journals. just start a new thread. I will watch for you!

Please read the easy start guides on the home page to make sure you get the details correct. It is pretty simple really.

so.....you can, of course, have a BPC WITH your BAB - it helps to get your fat/protein up. If that is what you have for lunch - fine. Make sure you eat dinner at least 4 hours before bed..and definitely no snacking!

I am not sure where you got the spreading the carbs thing from .... Only eat fruit in long lite cycles - when it is grown - the same for any carbs really. For me - I finally figured out fruit is just a sugar craving - so it is easier not to have any.

For your BAB you might have to even eat more protein and fat than 50 gr. I did and I am not a big woman. The idea is to eat a big enough BAB to knock out cravings... and it absolutely works!!!

Firstly - just make sure you are doing the Lepin RX using an epi paleo template correctly - there is a webinar.
Drink only spring water or R/O water...

Jack's podcast are great for getting quick information in an easy to understand format ... they are listed on the home page.
There is also a free webinar by Dr. Tim Jackson. If you can swing it - get the bootcamp ...it has been outstanding. Dr. Jackson is very easy to understand and he is covering a lot of ground in great detail
.

Do you have Dr. K's book? The Epi- Paleo RX: The prescription for Disease Reversal and Optimal health
it is available on Amazon in paper or kindle. It explains all of this in simpler terms and there is a great section on labs.

You definitely need Cold thermogenesis C/T ...for your shoulder ... there is an easy start guide and a webinar.

Please read a post today by Shiijin about this very thing.

Please don't worry about being Leptin sensitive yet .... you have other bigger fish to fry!

Your number one priority has to be to control the conditions of your existence .... what is your EMF exposure? at home? at work?

Get as much sunshine and grounding as possible....

Did I answer all your questions???? just keep reading and asking questions......there is a lot here to take in

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-23T23:31:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-few-questions.8853/post-101502>

1\. Leptin Rx
2\. CT protocol......
3\. Limit blue light at night.
4\. eat lots of pastured pork and eggs for the first 4-6 weeks add seafood at night.
5\. 1 to 1.5 gallons of non fluoridated water or spring water............

Dont ask.........I read all your stuff......that is what you need to consider. No booze or wine either......when you rise sun in your eyes. not behind any glass. you need to eat the sun (hint at something that is coming on the blog.

# Thread: Question about sleep.....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-sleep.8857/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nicld — 2013-11-24T04:03:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-sleep.8857/post-101513>

When my thyroid was off, I was waking up about 2:30 am and had a hard time getting back to sleep. The past couple days it has been getting better but I want to figure out why to stop it from happening again. I am really liking sleep again.

From some of the webinars I have learned that growth hormone is released between 2-5am but only in stages 3-4 of sleep. This in turned lets leptin work which in turn tell cortisol levels to start rising telling one to wake up.

I do wear blue blocker glasses at night and have my last meal 3-4 hours before bed.

My cortisol levels are good and I am waiting for the salivary melatonin tests. I think my thyroid is better now and I am on Pg cream.

So, what is going on hormonally that wakes my up at this time and keeps me up? I am having a hard time figuring that out. Still have some more webinars to listen to and them listen too again.

If anybody has any insights that they can share, I would appreciate it.

### caroline — 2013-11-24T04:51:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-sleep.8857/post-101516>

Just a thought .... I do way better if I eat earlier - like around 4:30 or 5:00pm. Much after 5 and I notice a difference. It may not be possible to eat that early I realise.

### Ken23 — 2013-11-24T05:18:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dandruff-hair-loss-and-vit-a.8858/post-101518>

I've seen Dr. K write about the connection between vit A and vit D cycles when talking about dandruff. I am suffering from dandruff and hair loss currently so I've been thinking about what could help solve this, and remembered something Ray Peat wrote about concerning the topic of dandruff. He said that whenever he increased his metabolic rate he noticed significant dry skin, dry eyes and dandruff and he found that high doses of vit A took care of this every time.

My question is....Since eating so much saturated fat in winter months and having such a high metabolic rate during this time, could it be possible that we need that much extra vit A during the winter months as compared to summer?

It's hard to eat that much beef liver. Could a fermented cod liver oil supplement in the winter possibly be a good solution?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dandruff-hair-loss-and-vit-a.8858/post-101520>

### Grizz — 2013-12-07T21:12:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dandruff-hair-loss-and-vit-a.8858/post-102743>

The cure for excessive dandruff is easy.

Simply mix 3 or 4 drops of Lugols 5% iodine into a little water ( about 1 ounce of water ) and wet the scalp. Severe dandruff may need this treatment repeated for a few days. Dandruff instantly disappears for several months. Repeat procedure as needed to keep dandruff away. I find that I need this treatment 3 to 4 times a year.

This is far less expensive and more effective than dandruff shampoos. Also more natural. Every cell in the human body needs iodine, including our skin cells. This is an amazing treatment for dandruff, since I have been suffering extreme dandruff my entire life, and this treatment is the most effective I've ever tried, and I've tried everything. Amazingly, this treatment has also reversed my hair loss.

Grizz

# Thread: Mutagenesis might be worse than GMO food
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mutagenesis-might-be-worse-than-gmo-food.8865/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2013-11-25T03:05:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mutagenesis-might-be-worse-than-gmo-food.8865/post-101569>

[http://www.businessweek.com/article...t-crop-developers-in-global-seed-market#r=rss](http://www.businessweek.com/articles/2013-11-21/monsanto-vs-dot-mutant-crop-developers-in-global-seed-market#r=rss)

Last July, when Monsanto (MON) withdrew its applications to sell genetically modified biotech seeds in the European Union, the move opened the way for competitors to challenge Monsantoâ€™s market share.

As opposition to genetically modified crops has spread across Europe and the world, leading chemical companies including BASF (BASFY) and DuPont (DD) have turned to mutagenesisâ€”a technique that mimics the sunâ€™s irradiation of plantsâ€”to create herbicide-resistant crops. The process, which faces almost no regulation, creates opportunities for companies to grab a bigger share of the $34 billion global commercial seed market. But some scientists say mutant crops are more likely to pose health risks than genetically modified ones.

Mutagenesis isnâ€™t new: Breeders have relied on it for decades to produce thousands of varieties of lettuce, oats, rice, and other crops. BASF today licenses its technologies to 40 of the worldâ€™s biggest seed companies, including DuPont and Switzerlandâ€™s Syngenta (SYENF), which in turn sell high volumes of mutant breeds, ranging from wheat to sunflowers, in markets that reject genetically engineered seeds.

The EU has approved planting only one of Monsantoâ€™s genetically modified crop varieties in two decades, prompting its decision to withdraw eight pending GMO requests last summer. Itâ€™s not the only company confronting roadblocks with GMOs in Europe. BASF last year decided to move its plant science division, dedicated to engineered crops, to the U.S. from Germany. Even in the U.S., bills pending in 26 state legislatures and before Congress would, if passed, require labels on genetically modified foods. No such disclosures are required on ingredients derived from mutant crops.

All breeding techniques can create plants with increased levels of naturally occurring toxins or with proteins known to cause allergic reactions. Reports from the National Academy of Sciences, representing the consensus of experts in the field, say the risk of creating unintended health effects is greater from mutagenesis than any other technique, including genetic modification. Mutagenesis deletes and rearranges hundreds or thousands of genes randomly, spawning mutations that are less precise than GMOs. The academy has warned that regulating genetically modified crops while giving a pass to mutant products isnâ€™t scientifically justified.

The risks associated with mutagenesis, the academy also says, are small relative to the incidence of food-borne illnesses such as salmonella. BASF maintains the crops are safe for consumers and the environment. â€œThis has been a technique used for many decades without issue, without concern,â€￾ Bryant says.

In addition to the regulatory-free environment they operate in, mutant crops are also gaining in popularity because theyâ€™re cheaper to produce. Monsanto spends anywhere from $150 million to $200 million to launch a single genetically engineered product. Japan, by comparison, invested $69 million from 1959 to 2001 on mutant breeds that yielded $62 billion worth of products over that period, according to data from the United Nationsâ€™ Nuclear Techniques in Food and Agriculture program.

â€œThese difficulties in getting a GMO to the market, we donâ€™t have it in mutation breeding,â€￾ says Pierre Lagoda, who heads up the UN program. Thatâ€™s spurred even more interest in the mutant varieties, he says. In 2013 alone, Lagodaâ€™s program has received requests to help irradiate a record 31 plant species ranging from sugar beets from Poland to potatoes from Kenya.

â€œThe current regulations are a huge incentive to go back and do things the old way,â€￾ including mutagenesis, says Wayne Parrott, a professor of crop science at the University of Georgia in Athens. Monsanto has also used mutation breeding, says the companyâ€™s chief technology officer, Robb Fraley.

Industry experts say breeding and biotechnology are starting to converge. Over the past five years, breeders have increasingly used molecular markers and sequenced genomes of corn and other crops to improve crossbreeding, making conventional breeding more like genetic engineering. â€œThere is not a black line between biotechnology and nonbiotechnology,â€￾ says Paul Schickler, president of DuPontâ€™s Pioneer seed unit. â€œItâ€™s a continuum.â€￾

# Thread: The Paleo fallacy of the land based food chain
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-paleo-fallacy-of-the-land-based-food-chain.8866/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-25T03:25:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-paleo-fallacy-of-the-land-based-food-chain.8866/post-101578>

Synthesis of arachidonic acid (20:4ω6) and docosahexaenoic acid (22:6ω3) from their precursors, linoleic acid (18:2ω6) and α-linolenic acid (18:3ω3), respectively. Plant seeds are rich in linoleic acid and green leaves in α-linolenic acid. Meat, eggs, and human milk are rich in arachidonic acid, while fish, shellfish, eggs, and small mammals are rich in docosahexaenoic acid. Docosahexaenoic acid is the precursor to neuroprotectins while arachidonic acid is the precursor to eicosanoids and leukotrienes which, on balance, are commonly associated with inflammatory processes. Conversion of linoleic acid and α-linolenic acid downstream is very slow. Synthesis of docosahexaenoic acid involves peroxisomes.
Natureâ€™s preference for DHA in the brain is strikingly demonstrated in large, vegetarian land mammals. The omega-3 (ω3) DPA is the dominant ω3 PUFA found in non-neural tissues and is thus abundantly available in the body, much more so than DHA (Crawford et al., 1969). However, neural membranes even in these large mammals still specifically conserved DHA. During evolution of the land mammals, this retention of DHA composition was associated with economy in brain size relative to the body, with a logarithmic reduction as they evolved larger bodies (Crawford et al., 1993). The structure of the brain is 60% lipid. Neural cells have a particularly high membrane content of DHA. In different mammalian species, the content of both arachidonic acid (20:4 ω6, AA) and DHA does not vary: it is brain size that varies (Crawford et al., 1976, 1993; Figs.2.1 and 2.3). DHA is rapidly and selectively incorporated in neural membranes and is concentrated at synaptic signaling sites (Suzuki et al., 1997). It is the most unsaturated of cell membrane fatty acids (Jump, 2002). Some DHA can be synthesized from alpha (α)-linolenic acid, but the process is highly ratelimited (Sprecher, 1993, Sprecher et al., 1999; Plourde and Cunnane, 2007) and, moreover,α-linolenic acid is oxidized at a very rapid rate of 60% in 24h compared with only5% for DHA (Leyton et al., 1987; Freemantle et al., 2006). In 1972, Crawford and Sinclair first published evidence that DHA itself was an independent determinant of brain growth and evolution (Broadhurst et al., 2002). DHAdeficiency studies in rodents (Sinclair and Crawford, 1972; Benolken et al., 1973; Galliand Socini, 1983; Weisinger et al., 1999; Catalan et al., 2002), chickens (Budowski et al., 1987), and primates (Fiennes et al., 1973; Neuringer et al., 1986), and visual and cognitivetrials in human infants (Carlson and Werkman, 1996; Martinez and Vasquez, 1998; Birchet al., 2000) support the concept that DHA is essential to brain development and function. Moreover, in collaboration with Professor Pierre Budowski at the Hebrew University of Jerusalem, we described competition existing between ω6 and ω3 PUFA and showed thattheir balance is critical for brain development and structural integrity (Budowski and Crawford, 1985).

BOOM!!

Human Brain Evolution, 2010: Cunanne and Crawford.

> Human Brain Evolution, 2010: Cunanne and Crawford.

Two questions:
1; **how to get rid of excess EPA**? I used to eat fish & krill oils. For the last almost two years I know better, but my EPA is continuously overwhelming my Omega3's
my EPA=348 instead of desirable 57

2; Specifically, how to calculate Omega6/Omega3 ratio?
Assumed ideal test results= all analytes are exactly in mid range of desirable range of values, I calculated
TotalOmega6/TotalOmega3 =11.9
and
AA/DHA=7.2
none of those ratios are close to 4.

there is a misinformation going on that results in people eating (Omega3) supplements:
fish oils, krill oils, EPA, DHA (which is generally, most often, wrong, unless supported by blood analysis)

**Quote from Ed Kane**
#1---excess of any kind of omega3 will suppress Omega6, specially AA, Arachidonic Acid (which is critically important)
#2---excess of Omega6 WILL NOT suppress omega3

Results below are based on LabCorp/Mayo Clinic analysis

Fatty Acid Profile, Comprehensive (C8-C26), Serum

# Thread: DNA--Not Always Destiny
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-not-always-destiny.8870/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2013-11-25T06:55:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-not-always-destiny.8870/post-101586>

This gives new meaning to the warning in genealogy that you never know who you might find hanging from the family tree.

[http://blogs.smithsonianmag.com/sci...scientist-who-discovered-he-was-a-psychopath/](http://blogs.smithsonianmag.com/science/2013/11/the-neuroscientist-who-discovered-he-was-a-psychopath/)

_Why has Fallon been able to temper his behavior, while other people with similar genetics and brain turn violent and end up in prison? Fallon was once a self-proclaimed genetic determinist, but his views on the influence of genes on behavior have evolved. He now believes that his childhood helped prevent him from heading down a scarier path.

â€œI was loved, and that protected me,â€￾ he says. Partly as a result of a series of miscarriages that preceded his birth, he was given an especially heavy amount of attention from his parents, and he thinks that played a key role._

While MTHFR isn't named, it can be associated with multiple miscarriages.

His DNA apparently determined the architecture of his brain and yet his epigenetics could overcome even that.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-11-25T15:28:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dna-not-always-destiny.8870/post-101604>

bingo DNA is the first draft of your book.......epigenetics is the editor of the final draft.

# Thread: Eating after CT for muscle building?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-after-ct-for-muscle-building.8871/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Butters — 2013-11-25T10:51:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-after-ct-for-muscle-building.8871/post-101591>

I'm at the beginning of CT (2 weeks now) but I experience very intense shivering during my bath (showers not).

Would it be an advantage to eat dinner after my CT-sessions in the evening to support muscle building through shivering? My intuition says it makes sense, but what tells the science? Does it vary for different body compositions?

### caroline — 2013-11-25T23:32:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-after-ct-for-muscle-building.8871/post-101640>

Dr. K. has always told us to eat high fat and drink 32 oz of ice cold water before C/T.........

He has said recently that at first, if he was shivering too bad, he would get out and have more fat and then get back in a little later.

everyone who is doing C/T consistently is finding that they are much stronger etc....

Have you looked at the C/T webinar?

### Butters — 2013-11-25T23:44:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-after-ct-for-muscle-building.8871/post-101642>

Yeah, I read about eating fat and protein before, but in Ben Greenfield's podcast he mentioned that lean persons can skip the high fat meal. I'm under 14% bodyfat and wonder if it's normal to shiver a lot more then.

I don't want spend money on these webinars because I have a small budget, so I didn't see it.

### caroline — 2013-11-26T00:29:24+0200

### Danco3636 — 2013-11-26T13:28:37+0200

> Yeah, I read about eating fat and protein before, but in Ben Greenfield's podcast he mentioned that lean persons can skip the high fat meal. I'm under 14% bodyfat and wonder if it's normal to shiver a lot more then.

I wounder about the same thing.... I am lean and seem to either shiver a lot more and or is difficult to warm up past CT. Say a 30 min bath in 50F or less and I am cold for hours afterwards. While I CT I seem to be fine but it is afterwards that gets me.....
Also with winter setting in, doing CT a bit colder and having more of a steady cold stimulus...(not wearing a jacket when going out) my hunger and cravings has gone up.... a lot in the evening....

### Danco3636 — 2013-11-26T13:41:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-after-ct-for-muscle-building.8871/post-101672>

One more thing.... Found this in my notes from Ray Cronise's site:

_"You don’t need to go cooler than 55 and for the time periods you are doing, 60F is low enough. the entire idea idea is NOT to shiver. Shiver burns glycogen, not fat (see part 3 of muscling your metabolism). The reason you aren’t shivering is that the body is adapting and keeping up with the heat. Belive me that sitting in 60F water is pulling Calories from your body. Even at 80F you are in the range of 240 kcals/hour. So you don’t want to shiver, you want to adapt.
What is most important is to not eat for at least 4 hours after – if you can time it and better not to have eaten before."_

Like to here others thoughts.....

### Hemming — 2013-11-26T15:02:29+0200

Shiver burns glycogen, not fat (see part 3 of muscling your metabolism). The reason you aren’t shivering is that the body is adapting and keeping up with the heat. Belive me that sitting in 60F water is pulling Calories from your body. Even at 80F you are in the range of 240 kcals/hour. So you don’t want to shiver, you want to adapt.
> What is most important is to not eat for at least 4 hours after – if you can time it and better not to have eaten before."_

My only problem with Ray is his obsession of reducing calorie intake. As an anorexic, I can assure you that calorie restriction will not help you tolerate cold.

### Butters — 2013-11-27T10:46:18+0200

### QuantumRadical — 2013-11-27T17:03:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eating-after-ct-for-muscle-building.8871/post-101731>

> I don't have the money to buy so much ice for step 2 of the CT-protocol. Should I still buy a compression shirt and take baths with cold tap water?

If I recall correctly, from the CT webinar a while back, shivering is just another positive aspect of the CT protocol. I CT until I get to mild-to-moderate shivering, then I get out. Recovery takes me a while--anywhere from 30 min to a couple of hours. My feet are always the last to warm up.

# Thread: HoneyChild's Musings
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honeychilds-musings.8878/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 111
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2013-11-25T13:26:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honeychilds-musings.8878/post-101599>

Basically I'm 31, have no libido and intercourse is painful. I get swollen toes from time to time and this summer developed xanthelasma around my eyes (basically little orange blobs under my skin). This is supposed to be related to oxLDL and heart disease!

I started leptin reset this time last year along with cold showers and occasional cold baths. I've always been naturally slim however I ended up losing more weight than I expected and now I think I'm underweight. Whenever I do cold baths I seem to lose even more which scares me and I stop doing them.

I also started having problems with waking up in the night hot and thirsty but this appears to have stopped since I cut back on the fruit about a month ago.

I do my best to keep technology to a minimum, I work from home, don't have wifi or cordless phones. My magnetic field at night is very low and I have a magnet sitting on the floor under my bed. I wear UVEX glasses in the evening.

The only supplements I take are maca and magnesium spray on my feet.

The problems with where I live are: live couple of flights up, city population about 1.2 million, nearest cell phone masts are about 1/4 mile away. We're desperate to move however I'm struggling to find the ideal home away from other houses.

Link to my test results and brainstorming...

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?8790-Labs-in-Is-it-my-field-or-what](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?8790-Labs-in-Is-it-my-field-or-what)

### HoneyChild — 2013-11-25T13:31:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honeychilds-musings.8878/post-101601>

It looks like H. Pylori has been linked to xanthelasma, elevated cholesterol and raynaud's...

**An uncommon lesion: gastric xanthelasma**

Xanthelasmas were found to be associated with chronic gastritis, gastrointestinal anastomoses, intestinal metaplasia, and H. pylori infection.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16245231](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16245231)

> Infections
>
> Another cause of high cholesterol profile is infection. Multiple studies have shown associations between bacterial infections like Chlamydia pneumoniae and H. pylori, which is the bacterium causes duodenal ulcers, and viral infections like herpes and cytomegalovirus and elevated lipids. (6) For example, H. pylori leads to elevated levels of total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, lipoprotein (a), ApoB or LDL particle number, and triglyceride concentrations as well as decreased levels of HDL. (7)
>
> Several mechanisms have been proposed to explain the association between infections and elevated blood lipids. Some evidence suggests that viral and bacterial infections directly alter the lipid metabolism of infected cells, and other evidence suggests that lipids increase as a result of the body’s attempt to fight off infection. Other evidence suggests that LDL has antimicrobial properties and is directly involved in inactivating microbial pathogens. This has been confirmed by studies showing that mice with defective LDL receptors—and thus very high levels of LDL—are protected against infection by gram-negative bacteria like H. pylori. (8)

[http://chriskresser.com/what-causes-elevated-ldl-particle-number](http://chriskresser.com/what-causes-elevated-ldl-particle-number)

**Helicobacter pylori eradication ameliorates primary Raynaud's phenomenon.**

The study shows that H. pylori eradication causes a significant decrease in clinical attacks of Raynaud's disease. The reduction of vasoactive substances determined by the eradication of the bacterium may be the pathogenetic mechanism underlying the phenomenon.
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9724144](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/9724144)

### Inger — 2013-12-01T10:00:56+0200

On the way home I stopped off at a little park and put my bare feet on the fallen leaves for half an hour and looked at the sun. I then made a smoothie using one of the sardines and I have to say it wasn't exactly the most pleasant of experiences but I did manage to drink it all. Use the heads for smoothie and make something nice from the rest? That is how I do. That way you will not have to drink large amounts of the yuckiness.. and you get a nice tasting dish to enjoy after you get the smoothie down! Also, to pour the smoothie through a sieve helps big (you might need to help and push with a spoon or it will not go through... at least if it is thicker). That way it feels like a rich fatty chocolate when you drink it with closed nose. I always close my nose. I taste nothing. I imagine it is chocolate! And with nose still closed I rinse my mouth and put something nice tasting in it and only then I stop holding my nose.
Makes the whole thing not yucky at all. And you get the very best part of the fish raw  eyes, brain...

Think about it like your medicine. Your hormone mood booster! That is what I do. I just had pieces of raw heart and liver with pieces of raw beef fat for breakfast. Nothing else. Just plain, fingerfood. It was yummy! And how it makes me feel... priceless!

Never think something is not possible, there is always a way

### HoneyChild — 2013-12-01T18:07:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honeychilds-musings.8878/post-101965>

The problem is that I absolutely detest the taste of sardines, so I have no idea how to "enjoy" them. I hoped that drinking them as a smoothie would be the answer.

I love smoked fish like mackerel and herring but they don't come with the heads, so I'm not really sure what to do. I thought of buying some flat fish like plaice or dab and using their heads for a smoothie. I'm sure that would work too.

I noticed that the sardine smoothies I had yesterday were surprisingly filling and I didn't end up eating dinner until 7pm which is very late for me in the winter. I simply forgot about food. I guess it's the electrons

I'm also relieved that the smoothie didn't make me vomit, I've vomited so many times this year it's not funny. I think sometimes it's because of the raw meat and fish I've eaten. My body needs more time to adjust to this new way of eating it seems. I ate raw lamb chops a few weeks ago and woke in the middle of the night to be sick and was ill all day. So I'm now a bit more cautious about raw stuff, even although I have no problems with actually eating it.

Raw lamb heart is absolutely lovely and tastes like steak to me, although for some reason I hate it when it's cooked. I'm going to see if my local butcher has some heart tomorrow.

### HoneyChild — 2013-12-14T16:32:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/honeychilds-musings.8878/post-103401>

In October I started studying for a degree in health science. There are 7 books in this module and at the end of each book we have to do an online test plus there are a few written assessments too. The first online test I did I rushed it a bit and only got 63% however with the other 2 I printed the questions off and took my time. I ended up getting 92% and 100%

I really only need to get at least 40% to get into the final exam and it's the result I get in the exam that really matters. So hopefully I can continue to do as well as I have been so far.

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

This is quite a big deal for me really. I've never considered myself an academic type. I remember in primary school I never did well at all. For some subjects we'd be split up into bottom, middle and top groups and I was invariably in the bottom group. I remember thinking it was strange that all the youngest kids in the class were in the bottom group but all the oldest kids were in the top. It didn't seem fair to me and I was the youngest. One of my friends was 13 months older than me!

I'd also come home with report cards saying things like "can barely cope with the most basic of work." No matter how hard I tried it made no difference, it was very demoralising. By the time I got into secondary school I felt there wasn't much point in trying, "I have two options, either I can try hard and get nowhere or I can not bother and still get nowhere." I choose the second option.

So I suppose the point in all this is that we can can change, nothing is set in stone.
