Thanks again Misty and Kyle, and of course, Dr. Jackson.

So much to learn and take on board ...... so little time!

We have Dr.Kruse hurting our head on a daily basis .......and now we have Dr. Tim giving us massive amounts of information that we need so badly if we are going to optimise this journey.

Dr. K. and Dr. Tim ...... what a great combination!

How lucky we are....

### Gracelind — 2013-12-04T08:31:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heal-your-hormones-webinar-3.8962/post-102254>

Adding my thanks and appreciation to Caroline's.

So valuable to learn how the various hormone systems work and interact.

I appreciate Dr Tim's connecting this info to the SNPs for enzymes in the Methylation Cycle. Adding this to our analyses from Genetic Genie and Sterling's App is invaluable.

Lots of actionable pieces to put together.

### caroline — 2013-12-04T08:43:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heal-your-hormones-webinar-3.8962/post-102256>

I have to move my butt and imput my 23&me ....is it easy to do? My computer skills are so limited....

This bootcamp is an amazing boatload of information and resources .....I will need many listens for all this to stick ...

Dr. Tim and Dr. Kruse really compliment each other beautifully. This bootcamp just adds so much to what Dr. K. has been teaching us .....really expands what we are learning in such a positive way.

I am a happy girl ..

### Gracelind — 2013-12-04T09:18:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heal-your-hormones-webinar-3.8962/post-102261>

Happy is good!

It takes a couple of steps but isn't hard to do. Log in to 23 and me then click on your name upper right. In drop-down menu click on Browse Raw Data. Upper right click on Download. Answer your security question. Save the zip file to your computer.

Then go to [http://geneticgenie.com](http://geneticgenie.com) and scroll down to Methylation Analysis. Click on the link. On the next page locate your Genome file from 23 in the Browse to upload file box. You'll get a chart with your Methylation SNPs and suggestions of how to address specific mutations.

At [http://mthfrsupport.com](http://mthfrsupport.com) click on Sterling's App on the top menu bar, then Order Report. It does cost $20.00 USD and uses Paypal. The analysis is much more comprehensive--more SNPs-- but doesn't include suggestions for supplements or discussion of the SNPs. It's necessary to do a search for the SNPs not on the Genetic Genie list to figure out what they are.

Have fun!

### caroline — 2013-12-04T22:12:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heal-your-hormones-webinar-3.8962/post-102325>

Thanks Grace........

# Thread: Cure for a cough--sleep on a glacier?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cough-sleep-on-a-glacier.8963/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2013-12-04T08:54:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cough-sleep-on-a-glacier.8963/post-102258>

John Muir must have been in exceptional physical condition to cover the difficult territory that he did. He's known for such things as climbing to the top of a tall pine to ride it's wild tossing in the winds of storms. The man was clearly cold-adapted! What caught my eye is his recovery from a long-term respiratory condition while walking and sleeping on a glacier. Also, his comment on his physical strength in lugging his heavy sled over the broken icy terrain.

From Travels in Alaska by John Muir Chapter XVIII
My Sled-Trip On the Muir Glacier [http://www.sierraclub.org/john\_muir\_exhibit/writings/travels\_in\_alaska/chapter\_18.aspx](http://www.sierraclub.org/john_muir_exhibit/writings/travels_in_alaska/chapter_18.aspx)

_I slept well last night in the middle of the icy sea \[on the Muir glacier\]. The wind was cold but my sleeping-bag enabled me to lie neither warm nor intolerably cold. **My three-months cough is gone. Strange that with such work and exposure one should know nothing of sore throats and of what are called colds. My heavy, thick-soled shoes, resoled just before starting on the trip six days ago, are about worn out and my feet have been wet every night. But no harm comes of it, nothing but good.**

I succeeded in getting a warm breakfast in bed. I reached over the edge of my sled, got hold of a small cedar stick that I had been carrying, whittled a lot of thin shavings from it, stored them on my breast, then set fire to a piece of paper in a shallow tin can, added a pinch of shavings, held the cup of water that always stood at my bedside over the tiny blaze with one hand, and fed the fire by adding little pinches of shavings until the water boiled, then pulling my bread sack within reach, made a good warm breakfast, cooked and eaten in bed.

Thus refreshed, I surveyed the wilderness of crevassed, hummocky ice and concluded to try to drag my little sled a mile or two farther, then, finding encouragement, persevered, getting it across innumerable crevasses and streams and around several lakes and over and through the midst of hummocks, and at length reached the western shore between five and six o'clock this evening, extremely fatigued. **This I consider a hard job well done, crossing so wildly broken a glacier, fifteen miles of it from Snow Dome Mountain, in two days with a sled weighing altogether not less than a hundred pounds.**_

### caroline — 2013-12-04T23:38:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cough-sleep-on-a-glacier.8963/post-102343>

another book I have to get...... that gives me goosebumps!

# Thread: Can we talk feet?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cantweight — 2013-12-04T20:41:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102312>

I have 3 flat footed kids. I raised them as shoeless as possible and they all have feet that look like Fred Flintstone's.

Although a lot of what i have read says the flat footed-ness is a good thing....my kids complain of sore feet far too often. I am going to take them to see someone, but I am not sure of the best course of action.

Do I let them get fitted for orthodics and what ever other medical crap is advised? Is there a better route? Sports med? PT?

Id appreciate any suggestions. Thanks!

### nicld — 2013-12-04T21:34:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102319>

Hmmm, I really have nothing except when I was having foot issue I used heel that pain shoe insoles and they helped tremendously. ([www.heelthatpain.com](http://www.heelthatpain.com)). My oldest DD has been complaining about her feet so I got her a pair of them but she really has not used them. At least I did get her to stop wearing those cheap crappy flip flops (she's 22 now).

Do they hurt when barefoot or just when they have to wear shoes? Maybe the difference between the two is causing the foot to have to change shape so much that the muscles don't know what to conform to.

### prAna303 — 2013-12-04T21:39:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102320>

Since i work with gait and posture i will say that this is a complex matter.

Many questions and if you ask the regular guy who works in this field today they say orthotics and insoles. For me this is a easy way to make money and force someone into problems further down the road, especially with kids.

\- How old are they
\- Any other illness? accidents?
\- How is their gait?
\- Do they lack 3D perspective? bumps into things...

I would say, look for someone who is asking many questions, the one who is finding the small things not just looking at the foot. A shoulder that is hurt by a car crash can be fatal to gait.

The only way i would be able to give advice is to meet them in person or nex best, a clip where they walk around, first in shoes and then barefeet.

This is a good place to look for ideas:

[http://thegaitguys.tumblr.com/](http://thegaitguys.tumblr.com/)

### cantweight — 2013-12-04T23:32:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102340>

Peter that is exactly what i was thinking but I dont know where to look....maybe there are primal podiatrists?!?

If all that I have read about the flat foot being a positive i dont want some old school doc to force their feet to conform to something they shouldnt. I will attempt a walking clip....would really appreciate your insight

Nicole it is most adversely affected by their cleats which are very hard soled. But the pain is there off and on regardless of what they do at times. i wonder if it may be nerve?

### nonchalant — 2013-12-05T02:45:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102363>

My daughters feet transformed in high school when she got into dance. They were quite flat before, which surprised me because my arches are high. Now she has a nice arch. I was surprised feet could change so much in an older child.

### prAna303 — 2013-12-05T09:46:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102393>

> My daughters feet transformed in high school when she got into dance. They were quite flat before, which surprised me because my arches are high. Now she has a nice arch. I was surprised feet could change so much in an older child.

I see this alot, when i look at the dancers foot i see often pure art but it can have a price. Since dance is about turn outs most of the time (classical ballet) many get into trouble around 40 years of age. The biggest thing is that when you dance you get muscles that works in a natural way... Look at many dancers and they have very defined muscles in their lowers AND butt. These muscles helps to lift the arch but also helps to create balance. This is why there is no universal solution to problems with feets, the only clue for me is that you can find errors at other parts of the body.

Remember also what Jack said many times, when someone loose energy to the environment the most delicate parts gets into trouble and moving in time and space is just one of these subjects, if you ask ME.

### prAna303 — 2013-12-05T09:55:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102394>

> Peter that is exactly what i was thinking but I dont know where to look....maybe there are primal podiatrists?!?

\- I talked to many pros in this matter and i am sorry to say that many do not look under the rocks... I have no education, no diploma but i do have passion for motion. That together with my knowledge in semiconducting makes me look at a person in a different view. Today all they say is that people are broken but they will never ever say a word of how they personally think about the reason why someone is ill.

> If all that I have read about the flat foot being a positive i dont want some old school doc to force their feet to conform to something they shouldnt. I will attempt a walking clip....would really appreciate your insight

\- Flat feet can be optimal IF you live in a flat landscape, many are today. The arch makes lines in the body either work good in nature or bad in man made world. I say that way to flat is something that gives a clue about muscle balance. The big BUT is always, how old are the kids because, change with insole in early years can make big, BIG errors down the road. One thing i always see today is that many have lost their sense of foot, not feeling the room (3D).

\- A clip could give me something to tell about the person, better than nothing. I need to see the whole person, not just feet. If you manage to shoot something, give me a PM and i send you my mailadress.

### cantweight — 2013-12-05T16:30:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102402>

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=102394)
>
> \- I talked to many pros in this matter and i am sorry to say that many do not look under the rocks... I have no education, no diploma but i do have passion for motion. That together with my knowledge in semiconducting makes me look at a person in a different view. Today all they say is that people are broken but they will never ever say a word of how they personally think about the reason why someone is ill.
>
>
>
> \- Flat feet can be optimal IF you live in a flat landscape, many are today. The arch makes lines in the body either work good in nature or bad in man made world. I say that way to flat is something that gives a clue about muscle balance. The big BUT is always, how old are the kids because, change with insole in early years can make big, BIG errors down the road. One thing i always see today is that many have lost their sense of foot, not feeling the room (3D).
>
> \- A clip could give me something to tell about the person, better than nothing. I need to see the whole person, not just feet. If you manage to shoot something, give me a PM and i send you my mailadress.

It is a tricky and complex issue. The kids are 8, 11, and 13. They had poor health (strep,lyme, fatigue, gut dysbiosis) early on and massive EMF exposure, SAD until 2009. My gut reaction to their flat feet after reading jacks blogs was that the arches had flattened in a desperate attempt to absorb more electrons. But the kids were always without shoes. I had read it was better to learn to walk barefoot and I was very ill myself so the process of putting shoes on 3 toddlers (which is much like wrestling alligators) did not appeal to me.

My 8 yr old son only complains from soccer cleats...I may just need to invest in a better quality shoe for him. His gait depends on where he is neurologically which is improving dramatically over the last couple months so we will have to wait for the dust to settle there.

My 11 yr old son is my biggest concern. He seems to cave inward, triangular shaped ankles touch medially, he also experiences occasional hip pain. Has had issues previously with arthritis and MCTD. He runs in a very stomping manner. His walking gait seems normal.

My 13 yr old daughter has a very wide forefoot and very narrow heel. I believe her pain is from muscles developing in her foot due to running and sports involvement where she had zero in prior years due to illness. She has a healthy gait and her running coach just changed her from a toe runner to a proper strike. She also has a history of bartonella which is known to cause foot pain.

I am just going to approach this like any other med issue and find a doc that thinks beyond the norm. Thank you for confirming that I wasnt over thinking this!

### prAna303 — 2013-12-05T16:56:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102405>

> My 8 yr old son only complains from soccer cleats...I may just need to invest in a better quality shoe for him. His gait depends on where he is neurologically which is improving dramatically over the last couple months so we will have to wait for the dust to settle there.

\- This i very normal, soccer is one of many sports that makes your gait cycle alter. Often you shoot more with one foot and the other one is a stance foot. Try to see if you feel any difference. In this age, many things happens with your motion, same as your 11 years old. If you look at shoes, remember that soccer shoes are flat, for a big reason. If you put a heel lift in that shoe you put strain on your lowers, it has to be flat, so do not use insoles in them. One other things is that when you get shoes, get some that are very bendy in the toebox. Take the shoe and bend it and see if it bends easily around the part where the heads of the toes would be. If it is not flexible, it is like walkig in a skiboot.

> My 11 yr old son is my biggest concern. He seems to cave inward, triangular shaped ankles touch medially, he also experiences occasional hip pain. Has had issues previously with arthritis and MCTD. He runs in a very stomping manner. His walking gait seems normal.

\- Did he crawl alot before walk? This is also something i see alot. Sound like a lack of dynamic power together with some lost sensibility in the foot. Alot of barefoot in nature but same rules as above with shoes. Does your kids use the upper when moving? In my part the world many do not have any arm swing. Here we do not want to look like we own the world and make ourself smaller when only walking in the lowers. When you switch to run in this case you get a stomping and humping motion.

> My 13 yr old daughter has a very wide forefoot and very narrow heel. I believe her pain is from muscles developing in her foot due to running and sports involvement where she had zero in prior years due to illness. She has a healthy gait and her running coach just changed her from a toe runner to a proper strike. She also has a history of bartonella which is known to cause foot pain.

\- Tricky one, together with some of the clues from the others, i would let others instruct her in running... For me "proper stride" is a compensation for the shoe, aka heelstrider. This you could get a million versions of but my take is if you run barefeet in the woods, you would not drop on your heels. Does she have a full flexibility in the hips? Often in girls i see that they have trouble to compensate the fact that they are girls. Here we also have the biggest thing like the others... Arm swing, girls are often experts in the noble art of NOT do swing, is it social? is it fashion? i do not now...

I would say that seek some help "live" but always listen if someone is looking beyond the trouble that is visible today. Their history is nothing unique today so the ones that try to help you and them must also look at new ways. Kids are kids and i think many doctors today put them in horror because they give them a lesson and not HOPE.

Please feel free to ask more questions...

### Shijin13 — 2013-12-05T17:14:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-we-talk-feet.8969/post-102407>

> [prAna303 said:](/goto/post?id=102393)
>
> I see this alot, when i look at the dancers foot i see often pure art but it can have a price. Since dance is about turn outs most of the time (classical ballet) many get into trouble around 40 years of age. The biggest thing is that when you dance you get muscles that works in a natural way... Look at many dancers and they have very defined muscles in their lowers AND butt. These muscles helps to lift the arch but also helps to create balance. This is why there is no universal solution to problems with feets, the only clue for me is that you can find errors at other parts of the body.
>
> Remember also what Jack said many times, when someone loose energy to the environment the most delicate parts gets into trouble and moving in time and space is just one of these subjects, if you ask ME.

Chaos has flat feet. However I've noticed changes in her feet the longer she's taking gymnastics. She's in gymnastics to hone her skills for balance and coordination - and she enjoys it and I find her applying what she's learned to her outside play....

Would the mode of transmission be through the solar bosons, via the magnetosphere, riding the heliopause?[http://news.yahoo.com/calm-solar-cycle-prompts-questions-impact-earth-213912384.html](http://news.yahoo.com/calm-solar-cycle-prompts-questions-impact-earth-213912384.html) You laugh.......but did you know solar bosons are part of the Electromagnetic force also has infinite range to act upon matter. A mathematician would say that an electromagnetic wave travels from Andromeda to your eye, and he would show you many equations to prove it true. A quantum biologist, like myself, says that it does even more than that..... that it also extends the power and information in Andromeda's light directly to your eye and brain to make some sense of it. Today, you can see Andromeda with your naked eye. It is 2.5 million light years away. When its light started heading your way to your eye, human beings did not even exist. Yet, you are here now to see that light. And this light can change your your biology right now. This is by definition action at a spooky distance, showing you the power of the electromagentic force. It is, in fact, infinite.

Start realizing that you got a lot more to think about........those photons from andromeda also are likely in you food too now...........connecting you to that galaxy in another way you might have not realized.

# Thread: Woried about O3/O6 ratio?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woried-about-o3-o6-ratio.8972/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 20

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-05T04:39:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woried-about-o3-o6-ratio.8972/post-102373>

Balance can be had with nuts! If inflammation is low you should consider the power of the nut. Tiz the season of the nut. Many recent scientific studies have appeared over the past two decades, beginning with four smaller studies between 1992 and 2002 (Adventist Health Study, Iowa Womenâ€™s Health Study, Nurses Health Study, and Physicianâ€™s Health Study) and ending with two landmark studies published this year (the 2013 PREDIMED Study and the recent 2013 Harvard NEJM report). When this research is viewed as a whole, the evidence is now clear that we should all go Crazy for Nuts when we conquer inflammation.

The most impressive of these studies was the 2013 PREDIMED study, where a very Epi-paleo like Mediterranean diet was combined with 3 servings of mixed nuts per week. Here they reported a 39% reduction in all cause mortality. The effects lowered cancer atherosclerosis, and heart disease in break out data.

So the question is do you use nuts? Jansz asked this question recently in ask Jack and went into no detail........well maybe this will interest you a bit more.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-05T04:45:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woried-about-o3-o6-ratio.8972/post-102374>

So what are nuts biggest effect? Since we are in the EE series guess what? Nuts help limit mTOR like metformin. They have polyphenols that do some amazing epigenetic gymnastics. The strongest effects of polyphenols are not as anti oxidants but are via their epigenetic effects. This has to do with gene expression on the electrification of DNA. BOOM

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-05T04:57:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woried-about-o3-o6-ratio.8972/post-102378>

You think the latest 'paleo crazy' of resistant starch is big? Well instead of eating a processed man made dietary fiber like this why not eat nuts. Did you know nuts have huge ability to make hydroxybutyrate? This fat is manufactured in your gut by bacteria to cause an epigenetic alteration of your DNA and RNA. Hydroxybutyrate is a very powerful epigenetic compound that inhibits the deacetylation of histone protein's. Hydroxybuturate is a broad spectrum HDAC inhibitor that inhibits most HDACs except the class II and class II HDACs. HDAC inhibitors can also be classified by their molecular structure. In this classification system, hydroxybutyrate is classified as a “fatty acid type” HDAC Inhibitor and is the most effective “fatty acid inhibitor” in this molecular class. It is a "baby Maxwell demon".

### JanSz — 2013-12-05T06:12:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woried-about-o3-o6-ratio.8972/post-102385>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=102373)
>
> Balance can be had with nuts! If inflammation is low you should consider the power of the nut. Tiz the season of the nut. Many recent scientific studies have appeared over the past two decades, beginning with four smaller studies between 1992 and 2002 (Adventist Health Study, Iowa Women’s Health Study, Nurses Health Study, and Physician’s Health Study) and ending with two landmark studies published this year (the 2013 PREDIMED Study and the recent 2013 Harvard NEJM report). When this research is viewed as a whole, the evidence is now clear that we should all go Crazy for Nuts when we conquer inflammation.
>
> The most impressive of these studies was the 2013 PREDIMED study, where a very Epi-paleo like Mediterranean diet was combined with 3 servings of mixed nuts per week. Here they reported a 39% reduction in all cause mortality. The effects lowered cancer atherosclerosis, and heart disease in break out data.
>
> So the question is do you use nuts? Jansz asked this question recently in ask Jack and went into no detail........well maybe this will interest you a bit more.

Each answer brings more questions.

Definition of O6/O3 still eludes me.

For now I think:
O6=LA+GLA+DGLA+AA+DTA

O3=ALA+EPA+DPA+DHA

I also think that before one starts worrying about his O6/O3 ratio, he/she first should figure out where are the individual components. Each componnent's level affects many other components in this set.
There can be large number of solutions that have ideal O6/O3 values. But, ideal O6/O3 ratio value is healthy only if components are also within their sweet values.

It may make sense to calculate O6/O3ratio when individual components are within some defined range.
But, it may not make sense to calculate O6/O3 ratio when individual components are out of some defined bounds.

What I do not know is what units are used when calculating individual fatty acids and then recommending some O6/O3 ratio.
Different units will bring different recommendations.
\------------------------------------------------
For example, when discussing P/E ratio (progesterone/estrogens) ratio, I have seen Dr. John Lee advocating progesterone to estrogen ratio between 200 and 300 to 1. But for that progesterone & estrogens are expressed in molar weights.
Calculating P/E one would use units pMol/L (Mol/volume). If need be he would first convert from common laboratory used ranges of pg/ml (weight/volume)

I have seen dr Kruse advocating P/E of 500:1, but I do not know what units would be used when aiming for that ratio.
\------------------------------------------------
Back to fatty acids.
I do not know what units are used when recommendation is made that O6/O3 should be in vicinity of 4:1.

I have seen some Fatty Acids Analysis done for dr Patricia Kane.
They are shown in her book page 231 The Body Bio Detoxx Book 2009 ed
Over there units are not posted.

I have made my fatty acids analysis at LabCorp (actually done at Mayo linic), the results were posted in (Mol/volume) units, nmol/mL

I have figured two O6/O3 ratios, dr Kane and Mayo.
In both I assumed that each component value is equal to average of their range value.

dr Kane O6/O3--->=2.8
Mayo Clinic O6/O3---->11.9

That is huge difference.

I am looking for some interest (within members of this board) to clarify definition of what is said when someone is recommending healthy O6/O3 ratio.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-05T17:37:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/woried-about-o3-o6-ratio.8972/post-102414>

roasted nuts are an oxidized PUFA.......not good. And JanSZ.......you keep making the error that DHA = all other sources of O3!!! It does not. DHA is not metabolized like DPA is. The only difference in the 2 is one double bond at position 6. But that one small quantum difference tells our mitochondria to beta oxidize DPA and not DHA and this is fed into FADH2 in cytochrome two..........You must begin to realize that the details matter. I have given you them.......and you are mixing a lot of other ideas together in your own mind. Get it right for you and move forward. All 0mega 3's are not the same.......DHA is special........and O6's can be great or bad........what makes the choice? back ground inflammation. this is where paleo fails many people.......they tell you O6's are bad all the time. Just Read Sisson or Wolf's books. then read my Fish oil post from 3 yrs ago. I was showing you then they had it dead wrong and I was doing it nicely.......when they began to use Social media to shoot me........I took the gloves off. They look for lap dogs to repeat their message to make $$$$.....see Jimmy Moore. Peskin and Kane are more right on O6's than Wolf and his crew.........but even Kane does not have it totally dialed in. Mike Crwaford has shown in many studies DHA is conserved for the brain.........so this is why it is special. And what it does to M1 and M2 macrohages are the switch that makes the 4:1 O6/O3 balance the rocket fuel for the brain
i want you all to get it right..........because that mistake can hurt you in the long run.

Did you know nuts have huge ability to make hydroxybutyrate? This fat is manufactured in your gut by bacteria to cause an epigenetic alteration of your DNA and RNA. Hydroxybutyrate is a very powerful epigenetic compound that inhibits the deacetylation of histone protein's. Hydroxybuturate is a broad spectrum HDAC inhibitor that inhibits most HDACs except the class II and class II HDACs. HDAC inhibitors can also be classified by their molecular structure. In this classification system, hydroxybutyrate is classified as a “fatty acid type” HDAC Inhibitor and is the most effective “fatty acid inhibitor” in this molecular class. It is a "baby Maxwell demon".

A photonic Maxwell's Demon:

[https://phys.org/news/2016-02-physicists-photonic-maxwell-demon.html](https://phys.org/news/2016-02-physicists-photonic-maxwell-demon.html)

### Da-mo — 2017-08-10T00:46:45+0300

> So omega 6/3 this ratio should be omega 6/3= 4 to 1 ? Not omega 3/6= 4 to 1. Is it possible to have this ratio and if so would it be ideal. My gut is saying it is very unlikely to get this ratio, but it would be ideal. What about omega 9. I read Dr. K's posts, I either need non geek version or more details in non geek if possible.
>
> Thank you

Yes Omega 6 = 4, Omega 3 = 1. But it also depends which tissues you are considering . . . .

> Skin makes up 4% of our Body Weight and has 1000 to 1 ratio of O6 to O3 normally.

> Skeletal muscle makes up 50% of our Body Weight and sets its O6/3 ratio at a 6 to 1.

> Our internal organs, make up 9% of our BW have the lowest ratios at 4 to 1.

> Adipose tissue sits at 22 to 1 ratio and makes up 15-35% of our total BW depending upon how fat or thin we are.

> The brain makes up 2-3% of our body weight (BW) but has a 100 to 1 ratio of _**06 to 03 within it normally**_.

I'm thinking this should read 1 to 100 as it doesnt follow the trend or fit with the rest of the text since the brain should have the highest metabolic rate = most DHA. Perhaps @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) could clear this up for us and make a correction to the blog if necessary.

> The trend is the lower the metabolic rate of the tissue the lower the amount of DHA is present.

From Brain Gut 13 [https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-13-where-optimal-meets-all-world-performance/](https://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-13-where-optimal-meets-all-world-performance/)

> **NON GEEKS:** Here is biochemical foundational proof that performance is found in the [Epi-paleo Rx](http://www.amazon.com/Epi-paleo-Rx-Prescription-Reversal-ebook/dp/B00BIUAZUQ) and not in offal and bacon.
>
>
>
> Another interesting factor is the requirement that each organ of the body has for omega 6 and 3 fatty acids. I decided to look into this issue more several years ago when I was researching how to reengineer my own health best. I had always been taught to believe that the brain had a larger DHA omega 3 component than an omega 6 level and it turned out I was dead wrong. The brain makes up 2-3% of our body weight (BW) but has a 100 to 1 ratio of _**06 to 03 within it normally**_. It appears the 0mega 6 to omega 3 ratios and their relationship to phosphotidyl choline and phosphotidyl serine ratios are **most critical** for cell membrane signaling of environmental signals to our brain. The amount of these moities in the brain is totally tied to the biochemistry of calcium efflux and calmodulin function. These function is completely tied to the electromagnetic field of the sun and Earth at the time child forms in utero. The omega 6/3 ratio is critical for protein confirmational bending in the lipid membrane structure after proteins are made in the brain. This single insight made me realize that mammals do something special with their omega 3’s and 0mega 6′s for some environmental reason.
>
> It appears in certain diseases of the brain, the ratio’s gets dramatically altered, and may actually be a good biomarker for us to use diagnosis and prognosis in neurodegenerative disorders and epigenetic diseases. Skin makes up 4% of our Body Weight and has 1000 to 1 ratio of O6 to O3 normally. Skeletal muscle makes up 50% of our Body Weight and sets its O6/3 ratio at a 6 to 1. Our internal organs, make up 9% of our BW have the lowest ratios at 4 to 1. Adipose tissue sits at 22 to 1 ratio and makes up 15-35% of our total BW depending upon how fat or thin we are. The trend is the lower the metabolic rate of the tissue the lower the amount of DHA is present. It appears metabolic rates are highly dependent upon the DHA in the tissue. Why Is that?

This is why older folks need more sunlight exposure and not less as they age. The less sun you get the higher the BP rises and more arteries calcify to cause atherosclerosis. NO vasodilates arterioles in the skin to bring blood closer to the surface. This is true in skin and retina and it lowers BP. Sunlight is a natural calcium channel blocker via its operations with UVA light in AM light. UVA light is what liberates NO with its specific electromagnetic wave power from the arterioles walls.

# Thread: I finally feel like a success story...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 337
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MrPinkies — 2013-12-05T18:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-102418>

Hi friends...

I know, I know.. I went MIA on the forum... Life kinda got busy there for a minute.. but.. I guess... it's time to stop and smell the roses!! (right? well.. not so much in 30 degree winter weather in Michigan..)

So... I've made the decision that my family and I are the ultimate success story..

I am officially down ONE HUNDRED FIFTY FOUR POUNDS!!!!!!!!!! Yep.. 1-5-4... 154.. A little over 110 pounds of that was due to the Leptin Reset. Am I still dreaming?!!? No longer 3XL.. or a 2XL.. or even an XL.. or even a L.. Friends.. I went from a size 26 to a size 10 (and still shrinking!).. I am a MEDIUM!!! It took me a long time to get the hang of things.. but.. I think.. somehow my dear husband finally got through to my thick skull how this all works.. I had a lot of trial and error days.. but.. I never thought I would be under 200 lbs again... My brain still has troubles on occasion with the fact that I am under a size 12 even (which is what I was in High School and when I met my husband I was a size 14).. I've corrected my cholesterol, blood pressure, and the ever dreaded PCOS... I'm on the official count down of 15 lbs... 15lbs and winter in Michigan? No prob, bob, I got this! LOL

My dear husband is down to 135 pounds (from around 180ish).. Still smaller than me, but, I am no longer 100+ pounds heavier than him.. which embarrassed me to no end..

and... of course.. I have to include a section about Autism.. because that is how I operate these days... So.. One of these days, I would LOVE to shake your hand, Dr Kruse.. I hope I don't come off as creepy..

So.. for my husband, the Leptin Reset, did a LOT for his Autism and anxiety issues.. Go read his success story and blog: Autism with a side of bacon..

For me, all of my sensory issues for the most part are 98% GONE!

For my kids.. all I can say is.. pure MIRACLE.. Life has certainly changed in the last year.. My son gained over 2 years worth of language.. His repetitive behavior is 95% of the time- manageable.. and the other 5% is rare and cured with a nap... He is in a general ed classroom with an aide and I can't even begin to explain the tears I had rolling down my face when the teacher was explaining to me that Ryan LOVES to partake in answering questions during class with correct answers.. and that he is starting to ask friends to play with him.. Lizzie is completely caught up, in a preschool class without help, and we are hoping when she turns 6 we can drop the label completely.

Since we have changed every single light bulb in our house.. got rid of our cell phones, iPad, TV, and turned off the wifi.. MY KIDS SLEEP!!!!!!!!!!!!! They get tired and put themselves to bed every single night without a fight at 8/8:30pm. I am not joking.. I almost wish I was.. Not a single friend believes me!!!

It's our goal to get the magnetico booster pads at tax return time.. I CAN'T WAIT!!!!!! I'm also looking forward to skin removal surgery in late June of 2014.

Last... "impossible" is just an opinion..

1\. Have either set of parents come around? nope.. even though they compliment how well things are going for us.. I don't get it, but, at least Chris, the kiddos, and I are doing this together.. and the kids are actually, even the three year old, are having a grasp that this is a good thing for them.. We are talking a LOT about "good choices for my body" type conversations.. and the conversations about "taking care of our temple", our only home... someday.. I will pull out my pictures at 309+ pounds and the weight loss journey I have taken over the last year and a half.. I have lost over 36 inches around my hips... I have lost over 24 inches around my waist.. etc etc etc... I NEVER want ANYONE to ever go through obesity like I did.. I don't wish that on ANYONE.

2\. Am I still dancing? YES! I have some pain in my knees, but, oh well.. I am loving my classes that I am taking.. I'm in classes that the majority are 14 to 18 years old.. very talented girls... me.. in my mid-30s.. keeping up and crushing combinations that I haven't done in 15 years... talk about feeling GOOOOOOOOOOOOOOOD... that is it, my friends.. that is it..

3\. Did Chris tell you to tell me to grow my hair out?!!?!!?? Any payments in hopes that I'd listen to you? LOL just kidding.. yes, I chopped my hair.. and now.. I'm growing it back out!

4\. Miss you greatly! I'll be on the forum more often~ just for you!

5\. I forgot to mention, I'm going back to school in Jan. to finish up my degree~ Human Resource Management. Year and a half to go!

My parents get why we are doing it, but, feel that it's complicated.. Whereas Chris's parents think that if we prayed and believed hard enough, all of our problems would go away- reap what you sow- kind of thing.. I laugh hysterically when they both tell us that it's too complicated and confusing.. Is it REALLY that hard?!?!? For me, anyway, life has been WAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAY simplified.. I make everything from scratch myself.. I still have pancakes and cookies.. just made with different ingredients.. nothing was taken away really, other than, the convince of pre-made things.. but the more I actually put things into practice, I've realized that it really does NOT take that much more time to make things from scratch.. it's a bunch of BS and lies we tell ourselves.. One of the BEST things I have EVER learned through my weight loss journey is to NEVER lie to myself again/being honest to ourselves.. That is exactly why my new favorite quote is "impossible is just an opinion"... It speaks to me on so many different levels..

> If it wasn't for the ketogenic version of the leptin reset, EMF "stuff", and over 100K dollars on various therapies (which were a waste of my money compared to the diet and EMF stuff), my 6 year old son would still be around 2 years old, MAYBE even 3... I don't think many people grasped the success we have had with him over the last year.. It is important to take initiative, you are correct... but praying to God and wishing in hopes to change my son.. The idea of God, etc, doesn't make any sense to me.. besides, it seems selfish to ask God to do such a silly thing in the first place compared to other things in the world that need his attention..

In my opinion the decision of action is God's work behind the veil of quantum mechanics.........

### MrPinkies — 2014-01-07T23:28:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-105020>

So... I want to move... out of Michigan.. but where to go.. where to go.. that is the question... No, it's not because it's a negetive 15 degrees, grey clouds, and nearly a foot n' a half of snow... nope.. nothing to do with that... well.. ok. that's not entirely truthful.. the best days in Michigan is when it is snowing and there's snow everywhere and the sun hits the snow and it literally sparkles!! I love the simple things, I know.. but.. it's SOO SPARKLY!!!!!!!!! like diamonds! Ok, back to the discussion: Through my weight loss journey, I have started to get a REAL understanding about how my body reacts to light cycles and the seasons.. and while.. I am learning to love the cold/snow, these months and months of grey skies are nearly killing me.. D3 has helped out a smidgen this year- WAY better than past years... This is becoming so clear to me (and to Chris too)...

Chris and I used to take a yearly vacation to Tennessee.. what I absolutely miss is that we would go early spring and it would be warm and GREEN on the trees!! Best of all- THE SUN!!!!!!!!!!!! The morning sunrise made me so happy... Not that I would move to TN, but, it's been a place that after so long of a grey winter, it always has lifted my spirits!

If you could move anywhere... where would you go and why?

### fitness@home — 2014-01-07T23:39:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-105022>

Well, I did move out of Michigan and landed in Texas. We love it here.

I truly can relate to your issues with the long grey days. February and March were the worst two months of the year for me when I lived in MI. Even as far back as elementary school I can recall feeling "blah" at that time of year.

If I could live anywhere, it would be near an ocean, gulf, or other large body of water. It would also be in a southern state - would have to do some exploring. Yes - I know Michigan has large bodies of water (which I loved). It's a beautiful state but I did not like the lack of sunlight at certain times of the year.

yours too...

I live in the mountains of North Carolina now. It's plenty gray here in the winter. Grew up in Chicago. I HATED living in Colorado--way, way too sunny. I felt assaulted every time I left the house. The first time it rained (8 weeks after we'd moved there), I went into a tizzy of delight.

Being from Michigan you will not be considered a DAMN Yankee if you move south. Just a Yankee. Midwesterners get off lightly.

I was gung-ho on the chelation thing after the webinar with Bonlie, but then I started researching ... I think you should really get educated on this one before you commit to anything. I have issues methylating and also have a SNP that doesn't detox pesticides well, so detoxing in general (I've recently learned) is a big problem for me. I'm doing a lot to support my liver and heal my gut right now and to jump-start methylation. I also just got a Magnetico about a month ago.

What I'm learning is that your body needs to be able to clear the metals once they get stirred up, or they just move around and can cause a lot of toxic ick. Having good gut health is going to be important. I'm going to do glutathione + Vit C IV and some Meyer's cocktails ... I'm to a point now with healing a bunch of other stuff where I think I'll get a lot of benefit from them. THEN I'm going to look at chelation therapy again, and I'm probably going to do a protocol that has you taking DMSA / ALA every three hours for three days, off for three days. Here are a couple of links to stuff I've been reading up on:

[http://www.dannyroddy.com/main/2010/11/1/mercury-chelation-it-aint-so-easy.html](http://www.dannyroddy.com/main/2010/11/1/mercury-chelation-it-aint-so-easy.html)
[http://www.regardingcaroline.com/andycutler2.html](http://www.regardingcaroline.com/andycutler2.html)

In other news, I think we should have you do a webinar for the Optimal Reset ... maybe we'll pull a panel of a few of you together to share your stories so others can hear what all you did and then the community can ask questions. I think this would be a great way for us to support each other! I'll ping you via Facebook.  Maybe we could have your hubby, Christi and Gretchen, and who else do we nominate? Someone who's gone all-in and had big results to really paint the picture for participants ...

> I've worked with Dr. Tim on my methylation protocol, Hope. I'm taking methyl folate and Super Oxicell, but he also told me a few other things before I started on those to get my body prepped. I don't recall exactly what was for methylation, what was for other things, as we are addressing a LOT of stuff! My chiro also just recommended a methylation supplement and he did a bunch of infrared laser therapy to support liver phase 2 detox ... my poor liver is toast. In my mind, these are all good "starters," but the glutathione / meyers IVs are the big kahunas. I'm stoked about starting them! And the redox stuff I'm doing seems to be uber-important, too ... I just started the Leptin Rx again and added two more weekly sessions of CT (so 4 per week for me).

that is amazing Misty...love it. I have had some issues with folate, even methyl...gives me palpitation so working up to it. love what Jack said....and would explain why it did me no good. so glad i stopped it! wow, infrared laser therapy? ### tellmisty — 2014-02-22T16:15:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-111686>

> [Hope said:](/goto/post?id=111647)
>
> that is amazing Misty...love it. I have had some issues with folate, even methyl...gives me palpitation so working up to it. love what Jack said....and would explain why it did me no good. so glad i stopped it! wow, infrared laser therapy? Sounds cool! Is that the nasal kind? I am so excited for you with CT...I so want to do more with that but for me the focus is moving out of mold, dealing with it and then getting really into it. Which BTW, have you thought about doing your own journal? I love all you have shared...and always love when you share on the calls/webinars. so cool. you are turning into another Gretchen
>
> PS how do you know your liver is toast? 23andme?

Yeah, what you're describing about methyl folate is why Dr Tim didn't have me start right away. You've got to get your body to a point where you can leverage the supplementation.

The laser therapy he did was on acupuncture points, I think. I do have an 810 Vielight, too, that I just started using.

I know my liver is toast because my phase two detox pathways are just shut down. This was showing up in AK muscle testing before I ever started doing lab work. Labs show it, I'm not methylating, I have bad SNPs for detoxing pesticides, etc. When my Chiro started muscle testing, he found issues with the thalamus and liver big time. He worked with me for quite a while. Also tested me for metals... High mercury and another one I can't remember. I'm doing a urine test and just did a blood test for the metals and we'll compare notes.

I wish I were Gretchen... Nowhere close, unfortunately. She has a grasp of the science that I just don't have (her background is amazeballs.) BUT... I think the more you engage with your own story, the more you lean in to Biohacking and testing and retesting and making adjustments and just LEARNING, the better you get at connecting dots in general and seeing similar patterns in other people's journey so you can help them along, too. I've learned a TON from Gretchen and Dr Tim. They offer something so complementary to Dr Kruse's teachings, and I hope people take advantage of the opportunity to learn from them.

I don't think I'll ever do an optimal journal... It's just not aligned with my "flow." But I'm logging my journey for you guys in other ways, like creating these eCourses, knowing the kinds of guests to bring in, the bootcamps to create, and I'm going to submit more guest blogs. And now, I'm spending some time on the forums here and there, but there are times I go weeks without logging in. I appreciate why you're asking me to do that, though, and I have thought about it.  I feel very connected to our community and I'm one of y'all... Trying to crack the code to improve my own health! I've made progress, but there's a hump I'm trying to get over. As I figure it out, I'm taking everyone with me that wants to go!

I'm to a point now with healing a bunch of other stuff where I think I'll get a lot of benefit from them. THEN I'm going to look at chelation therapy again, and I'm probably going to do a protocol that has you taking DMSA / ALA every three hours for three days, off for three days. Every single stinkin' time I try to detox I crash and this last time I crashed HARD! I'm curious now if it could be from metals getting stirred up and not removed - just recirculating in my body. I'll have to do some more digging . . .

Oh, and I love the idea of having members share their success stories

### MrPinkies — 2014-02-22T18:17:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-111700>

Misty-

You're the best! Your posts just put a smile from ear to ear on my face! Throughout my journey, I have learned SO much.. I have much to share.. and I feel like I need to share, especially when it comes to Autism and the success that we have had.. Life is very very very different from two years ago.. Had I known the changes that we were going to experience and go through, I would have had a camera taping all kinds of things.. melt-downs, the frustration, the waking up at 2 and 4 am just because his body needed to move/sensory input, the inability to communicate in need, the sensory over-load or seeking, shower/self care... to the speaking in full sentences, playing with peers, to sleeping through the night, to getting dressed by themselves, fully potty trained.. We were told so many times that our son would amount to nothing.. I think that Lizzie and myself wouldn't qualify under the new definition of Autism now... It would definitely be interesting to have Ryan reevaluated to see where he's at because he is definitely no longer borderline severe, low functioning. He's currently six years old physically.. and probably 1.5 years behind now..

As for my weight loss... if my body ever cooperates and I lose this last 1.2 pounds, we shall party-hardy to celebrate 160 lbs lost!

In thinking about what I've learned here from Kruse, I think we are going to hold off of the DMSA, etc... Chris was rereading a blog or two and realized we can just up our garlic, onions, etc.. We have worked too damn hard to keep our food and anything else we intake clean.. we shall see what happens! Monday can't come soon enough!!!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-23T15:52:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-111797>

The magnetico is not a detox problem........you environmental Ca efflux is. Magnetico helps that. When your redox is in the toilet you feel like a rat in a maze and have no clue where to go. Begin to control your environment. then water..........then food. Food is never the first answer. It is just the easy answer for people because of their beliefs. Fear melts like ice when we allow love's divine light to shine upon our ignorance. The 3 legged stool is that light.......begin to let it shine on your semiconductors. That will help your redox potential faster than any of these thoughts I see BJK77. You must come to grips with the real problem. Hard is hard for us all.

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-24T14:25:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-111915>

> [tellmisty said:](/goto/post?id=111877)
>
> I need to bring someone in to help us all deal with this kind of stuff emotionally and spiritually. It is hard to see people suffer, to be helpless to help them. To surrender, to let go ... I've been mulling this for a coupla weeks.

This is such a difficult process to "teach" because you are in the realm of belief....and as Jk and others have mentioned beliefs are the most challenging aspect of human nature to change. What I see here is often a reflection of all these tangles. The concept of boundaries can help (I believe!). If JK didn't have a sense of higher purpose in his life he would have lost weight, felt great and gone on live his three legged stool life on his own...perhaps with a few family members too. He didn't, so here he is pouring out his experience and learning on a forum which is available to anyone. But if he didn't have boundaries, he wouldn't be able to do it....the emotional cost of creating good things for others can take you away from your own path so easily. (i.e. consults that were helpful for many were turned back on him as a weapon, patterns of bad labs with too much computer time, frustration, etc the benefits of sharing the load <go Misty!>)

This problem takes us right to the deepest part of us...what is our identity and destiny as individual beings and what is our identity and destiny as entangled beings. Having overarching beliefs can really muddy these waters...I speak from my own experience raising our two daughters....I believed that being a good mother was all about putting everybody first...plus my whole family had some pretty extreme health issues that kept us in a kind of crisis/survival mode for years. So, I ended up a "good" mother but lost my health. Are we all further ahead? No.

Since reclaiming my autonomy I am able to model (not teach)what I would like my girls to do as well. Learn what it is that makes them thrive in this world that we live in. At the same time that I'm far more connected to my own self I've been compelled to offer one way for others to learn about themselves (this is the horse work I do). That didn't come because I was wanting to save the world, it came about because I knew I could do it and stay healthy myself..in fact it serves as a constant meditation on this process of maintaining boundaries. And the most amazing part of this is that having more clarity takes you to a much deeper connection with those you do encounter.

Which is just another echo of what Josh wrote above.....having a few people to connect with is a blessing. And my reflection on what Audrey is saying is simply that many people will not wake up in their lives....that is a given. Struggling to do this initial wake up FOR others is a huge waste of energy...supporting and pouring our souls into the process of learning and healing AFTER that point is just a wonderful circular stream of energy.....

### MrPinkies — 2014-04-02T03:21:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-119347>

THIS IS SUCCESS... This picture says it ALL...

Almost exactly a year ago, I was angry with Ryan's OT because she got frustrated (she showed Ryan that she was frustrated) that she worked and worked and worked with Ryan to get him to ride a tricycle.. and he just didn't have the muscles to do it...

This is my son Ryan, 6 1/2 years old with Autism, very very low muscle tone... ON A BIKE!!!!!!!! Pedaling on his own.. WITH his babysitter on the back of the bike! I think about all of the AMAZING things my son has accomplished over the last year.. and somehow.. this one.. this is AWESOME and AMAZING... and just.. I'm FLOORED at all the things he can now do! I am one very proud Mama...

### MrPinkies — 2014-04-27T23:08:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-123598>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=123413)
>
> You're an inspiration, proving just how big a difference the right prescription can make. Do you spread your success on any Autism sites? I'm sure there are many desperate parents who would be interested and motivated by what you've achieved?

Yes, I have made my success known with the Autism community, but, not with great feed back. There's a saying with the Autism community- "if you've met one person with Autism, you've only met one person with Autism".. The reason they say that is that in Autism- there are three main categories- social interaction, communication, and repetitive behavior.. Under each of the three categories are subcategories, with subcategories under those subcategories, etc.. Each subcategory is a very specific skill set, separated by age groups.. Each year you get older, a new set of skills are required of you. Each person with Autism has different gaps in their skills, varied by age.. How many gaps you have determines the severity of the Autism.

Problem: if a parent, for example, ignores the signs and warnings of Autism, and does nothing.. The gaps in the skills gets bigger the longer the child goes without help, the harder it is to catch up... Time never stops, and that's the hard part.. racing the clock and going past their age skill set to actually be officially caught up. The easy read/uncomplicated version: When Ryan was 4, most of his skills were around 1. He gained 2 years by the time he was 5. So.. when he turned 5, most of him was around 3. He gained another 2 years worth of skills in the next year. So.. when he turned 6, most of him was around 5. Does that make sense to you?

Lizzie, we have COMPLETELY caught her up.. We caught her issues EXTREMELY early and did not wait... We had her in therapies by 8 months old.. She was the kid that didn't cry, coo, smile, track people or toys, straight face, no eye contact etc.. She refused the breast, hated to be cuddled.. she'd pick up toys with her wrists because she didn't want to touch with her hands.. She HATED (and still does) water- like baths/showers.. She had a LOT of sensory issues/SPD (sensory processing disorder). she started to coo at 8 months and then went completely silent at 9 months all the way to 13 months old.. However, by the time she turned 3, she was completely caught up and past her age range skill set. She turned 4 this past February and her skills are starting to be age appropriate and are staying age appropriate. We had slight regressions here and there, but, again, we are hoping that we can drop the label when she turns six. She is in preschool, in a class, without help. She tested out of special education services at the low end of the normal range of functioning. I'll take it. We are still working on sensory issues, but, the majority is controlled with the Ketogenic version of the Epi-Paleo Diet/EMF precautions. Magnetico = Lizzie's BFF.. Last, FINALLY made it to the 5th percentile for weight and sleeps through the night!!!

Ryan, on the other hand... not so lucky.. yet lucky... Ryan was our first born... He was a GREAT baby.. around 12 months we started to wonder about him.. He stopped making noises, stopped responding to his name.. absolutely no eye contact.. etc etc etc.. Chris was diagnosed with Aspergers a few weeks before Ryan was diagnosed with classic borderline severe Autism, low functioning.. Not even a week after that, I found out that I was pregnant with Lizzie... It was literally like.. Congrats on your pregnancy.. Yes, your kid has Autism.. good luck to you.. and chances are we will see you in two years for your next kid.. That was it.. no plan.. no guidance.. no nothing... We didn't know what to do, so, we did nothing.. When Ryan turned 3, the majority of him was under 6 months old. I finally put my foot down and did research.. We took out MASSIVE loans and did ABA therapy, speech, ot, pt, etc... and we did make some gains with those therapies.. but it is NOTHING compared to the Ketogenic diet, EMF stuff, magnetico, etc. Like Lizzie, we got Ryan up to the 3rd percentile for weight now and sleeps through the night!! When we went extremely strict with the Keto diet, EMF, spring water, going outside barefoot in sun, orange glasses, etc = MAGICAL things... Ryan turns 7 in September... Our goal is to be around 5 1/2- 6 years old, and we are VERY VERY close!

### Tanya — 2014-04-28T03:09:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-123612>

> [MrPinkies said:](/goto/post?id=119347)
>

>
> Almost exactly a year ago, I was angry with Ryan's OT because she got frustrated (she showed Ryan that she was frustrated) that she worked and worked and worked with Ryan to get him to ride a tricycle.. and he just didn't have the muscles to do it...
>
> This is my son Ryan, 6 1/2 years old with Autism, very very low muscle tone... ON A BIKE!!!!!!!! Pedaling on his own.. WITH his babysitter on the back of the bike! I think about all of the AMAZING things my son has accomplished over the last year.. and somehow.. this one.. this is AWESOME and AMAZING... and just.. I'm FLOORED at all the things he can now do! I am one very proud Mama...

I thought I would mention a wonderful camp "lose the training wheels" that my daughter used to volunteer for a few years during her summer brake. It is designed to help kids with special needs to learn how to ride a bike. They use a specially equipped bikes and with the help of volunteers, after 5 days these children can ride a bike on their own. They have a graduation ceremony during which they all get their certificates - it is hard to describe how happy everyone is on that day, there are so many smiles, hugs and some tears from parents who thought that it would never be possible...
It is available every summer in almost every state. Here is one of them [http://www.pennlive.com/midstate/index.ssf/2012/08/ticket\_to\_ride\_camp\_teaches\_sp.html](http://www.pennlive.com/midstate/index.ssf/2012/08/ticket_to_ride_camp_teaches_sp.html)

Well done, your children sound as though they are well on the way to becoming rounded individuals and that's all down to your love and care

> Hi friends...
>

>
> So... I've made the decision that my family and I are the ultimate success story..
>
> I am officially down ONE HUNDRED FIFTY FOUR POUNDS!!!!!!!!!! Yep.. 1-5-4... 154.. A little over 110 pounds of that was due to the Leptin Reset. Am I still dreaming?!!? No longer 3XL.. or a 2XL.. or even an XL.. or even a L.. Friends.. I went from a size 26 to a size 10 (and still shrinking!).. I am a MEDIUM!!! It took me a long time to get the hang of things.. but.. I think.. somehow my dear husband finally got through to my thick skull how this all works.. I had a lot of trial and error days.. but.. I never thought I would be under 200 lbs again... My brain still has troubles on occasion with the fact that I am under a size 12 even (which is what I was in High School and when I met my husband I was a size 14).. I've corrected my cholesterol, blood pressure, and the ever dreaded PCOS... I'm on the official count down of 15 lbs... 15lbs and winter in Michigan? No prob, bob, I got this! LOL
>
> My dear husband is down to 135 pounds (from around 180ish).. Still smaller than me, but, I am no longer 100+ pounds heavier than him.. which embarrassed me to no end..
>
> and... of course.. I have to include a section about Autism.. because that is how I operate these days... So.. One of these days, I would LOVE to shake your hand, Dr Kruse.. I hope I don't come off as creepy..
>
> So.. for my husband, the Leptin Reset, did a LOT for his Autism and anxiety issues.. Go read his success story and blog: Autism with a side of bacon..
>
> For me, all of my sensory issues for the most part are 98% GONE!
>
> For my kids.. all I can say is.. pure MIRACLE.. Life has certainly changed in the last year.. My son gained over 2 years worth of language.. His repetitive behavior is 95% of the time- manageable.. and the other 5% is rare and cured with a nap... He is in a general ed classroom with an aide and I can't even begin to explain the tears I had rolling down my face when the teacher was explaining to me that Ryan LOVES to partake in answering questions during class with correct answers.. and that he is starting to ask friends to play with him.. Lizzie is completely caught up, in a preschool class without help, and we are hoping when she turns 6 we can drop the label completely.
>
> Since we have changed every single light bulb in our house.. got rid of our cell phones, iPad, TV, and turned off the wifi.. MY KIDS SLEEP!!!!!!!!!!!!! They get tired and put themselves to bed every single night without a fight at 8/8:30pm. I am not joking.. I almost wish I was.. Not a single friend believes me!!!
>
> It's our goal to get the magnetico booster pads at tax return time.. I CAN'T WAIT!!!!!! I'm also looking forward to skin removal surgery in late June of 2014.
>
> Last... "impossible" is just an opinion..

Sorry but Autism cannot be improved and doesn't need to be ,we also don't try to make gay people straight, as there is no need and cannot be done!!!

Please stop posting lies about Autism and diets, it's impossible as the brain was born wired differently to neuro-typicals.

Thanks

I am very happy having Autism! It is who I am and always will be!

### MrPinkies — 2014-04-28T15:58:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-123686>

I am quite heart-broken at this point.. the personal email.. wow..

First.. I stand by what I said.. Therapies like speech, ot, ABA, etc helped some.. but it is NOTHING compared to everything we have done since changing his diet, grounding, emf stuff, etc...

David, I think your mind is very black and white and that the idea of improvement is beyond you..

I LOVE my kids with ALL my heart.. and for you, a complete stranger to me, to tell me other wise, is INSANE to me.. I don't want to change them.. I want them to have skills to be able to cope with this world.. to be able to live on their own.. have their own life without needing assistance.. before we found JK.. I prayed every night to out-live my kids to make sure they would be taken care of.. Do you want to know how many ABA therapists, etc told me that my son would never be able to live on his own when he gets older??!?!? I'm talking about SURVIVAL skills... My son, at 6 years old, is FINALLY been potty trained for 6 months without accidents... You said yourself that you are mildly effected.. obviously, you are living as adult in the world just fine.. but.. a LOT of people with Autism are more severe than you and WILL need help when they get older...

We needed to try... have faith.. and we did.. and had success... we are grateful...

### NeilBB — 2014-04-28T16:27:06+0300

As a physician, I have seen many patients strongly identify with their condition to the point of vigorously defending it and viewing anyone that would reverse their condition as a threat to their person.

Many patients whose seizures have been cured either medically or surgically, go on to develop "non epileptic spells" as a psychological compensation because they so strongly identify with their previous disease and are very uncomfortable with everything that goes with being cured. That is the reality I see all the time.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-28T17:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-123711>

The psychitrist cited earlier in the thread may say what he says. However, scientists at Kings College, London, do not agree.

> Scientists from King’s College London have identified patterns of epigenetic changes involved in autism spectrum disorder (ASD) by studying genetically identical twins who differ in autism traits. ...
>
> ... in 30% of cases, identical twins differ for ASD. Because identical twins share the same genetic code, this suggests non-genetic, or epigenetic, factors may be involved.
>
> Epigenetic changes affect the expression or activity of genes without changing the underlying DNA sequence – they are believed to be one mechanism by which the environment can interact with the genome. Importantly, epigenetic changes are potentially reversible and may therefore provide targets for the development of new therapies.
> ...

[http://www.kcl.ac.uk/iop/news/records/2013/April/Epigenetics-autism.aspx](http://www.kcl.ac.uk/iop/news/records/2013/April/Epigenetics-autism.aspx)

Stay open-minded.

Also, practically speaking, never give up. Some of us are saddled with problems, but there's always some hope, and things can change. What if Helen Keller's family had shut her away and forgotten her?

### sooperb — 2014-04-28T18:43:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-123724>

"Change is difficult and can be a lot of work, and it is sometimes easier to just wear the label as a reason. I see it every day. It is sad, but definitely a common trait."

I couldn't agree more. A lady of my acquaintance was diagnosed with MS in her 20s. She had a successful career, married and had children. Bit by bit her MS has taken over her body until now in her 50s she is wheelchair bound with no standing tolerance and no independent mobility. Despite this, she claims she was told by her consultant that MS is not progressive. She blames the carers for her inability to stand claiming it was their fault for taking away her Sam Hall turner. She has a motability car that she got knowing that she can't get in it so lets her son drive round in it instead and has refused an assessment for an electric wheelchair as she says it will make her look disabled. I have wondered if she prefers to be unable as it gives her a good reason to do nothing for herself and sees a lot of money pour into her account every month in state benefits. She is young still, is intelligent yet seems to prefer being disabled to making an effort to try to and improve on what she has. Of course this is her choice but it doesn't seem to make her happy, she is rude, aggressive and thoroughly disparaging of all the care staff who have to endure her acid tongue and occasional bad language. Why would anyone not want to be the best physically that they can? I'm sure you could easily apply your statement to her, sad as that is.

_Fighting for your health_ improves your relationship with nature.
_Fighting for your diagnosis_ improves your relationship with the social system.
Every potential patient must choose his battle.
The former requires courage, audacity, and independence.
The latter is encouraged by the government, charities, and the medical profession.
Disease-states have been legitimized, institutionalized, and even rewarded by modern society, perhaps because modern society feels guilt for having created most of the diseases in the first place...

### Jack Kruse — 2014-04-29T03:01:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-123794>

Interesting Pam. I agree that is what you saw...........but he has to decide what is right for him. This site is about what is right for our species who has this problem. He has his dogma. I respect it. But.......................
I'm not married to anyone's dogma......just to the science and data that reveals the truth to me about my world and my biology. If he wants to settle for his outcome that is on him. I don't want average. I don't want mediocre. Mediocre is not something to aspire to........it is something to run from. So I think settling for anything on the spectrum is not an option for me. I have a moral duty to post what I think is possible based upon what we know. And what we know says electrons throughout the neocortex is the key.
I believe we should risk more than others think is safe, care more than others think is wise, dream more than others think is practical, expect more than others think is possible. If you don't believe this.........leave. And do not let the door hit you in the ass.

### Christopher — 2014-04-30T16:51:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-finally-feel-like-a-success-story.8975/post-124064>

David, I don’t know if you are still following this or not, but I shall respond anyway. I’m Chris, Kate’s (mrpinkies) husband. I have been professionally diagnosed with both Asperger’s Syndrome, and Social Anxiety Disorder, and I would like to add my perspective.

Before my diagnosis, Kate and I first started to notice the traits of autism in Ryan, our first child, we didn't know what it was. Ryan had some behaviour that seemed to me to be anti-social. I knew I had social anxiety, and I thought that he was modelling his behaviour after my own. I felt guilty. I decided that I should make an effort to be a better role model for my son. I sought therapy for my social anxiety. That therapy lead to my diagnosis of Asperger’s. I was glad for the diagnosis, as it relieved much of the guilt I felt. Ryan’s diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder soon followed.

Kate and I both began our somewhat independent journeys to learn about autism. I was totally preoccupied with learning about my own condition, and at the time, I assumed that Ryan would turn out no different from myself. Everything I was reading about Autism was like looking in a mirror clearly for the first time. It gave me a new understanding of my personality especially my strengths and weakness. Like you, David, I am quite happy with many aspects of my personality that are derived from the Autism. I even went through a phase of thinking that being on the Autism spectrum was superior to being neurotypical.

During that time, Kate was desperately trying to figure out how to handle Ryan’s diagnosis. She was learning that early intervention with therapy was important. And was trying to convince me that we needed to get Ryan into some kind of therapy as soon as possible. I resisted her. I was still on the stage of accepting myself. I was embracing who I was and figured we should also embrace who Ryan was, after all, I turned out ok, so he should as well. We went back and forth on the issue for some time. Despite my stubbornness, Kate was successful in eventually getting Ryan into various therapies.

Eventually, the pride I had in my Asperger’s began to fade. My weaknesses were still with me, even if they weren’t my fault. I was still troubled by anxiety, and depression wich are both related to the Autism. To make matters worse, I was also dealing with some religious confusion at the time which was leading to denial, paranoia, guilt, and apathy. I was in a downward spiral, but I could not fully acknowledge it, nor even begin to understand why.

Around that time, I discovered the paleo diet, which lead me to Jack Kruse. I started with the Leptin Rx. It made me feel great. And the rest is history. By history, I mean, Kate and I both have success stories, and journals on the forum here.

Now I am in a position where I can be honest with myself. There are things I like about my autism, as well as things I don’t like about it. For example, I like my ability to separate myself from society, and acknowledge my individuality. I don’t feel much pressure to conform to the “herd”. I live my own life according to what is logical to me, not by what the people around me are doing. I am intelligent. I am able to learn easily from books on topics that interest me. On the other hand. I need extra time to process communications from others. I tend to take things literally and have difficulty grasping the deeper meaning of what is said to me. This leads to misunderstanding, and unnecessary arguments. I also tend to get caught up on the details of things and lose sight of the bigger picture. Not to mention the anxiety and depression.

Well, ok, lets mention the anxiety and depression. It should be known that anxiety and depression are both strongly linked to autism. Anxiety and depression are caused by both the social difficulties of autism, as well as by the physical state of the brain. Now if there is one thing that bothers me the most about my autism, its the anxiety, and second to that would be depression.

After starting the Leptin Rx, I noticed a drastic improvement in my anxiety. Previous to the Rx, feelings of anxiety, including the dreaded rushes of adrenaline, were and everyday experience for me. Not just in social situations, but when I was alone as well. After the Rx, I only feel rushes of adrenaline in appropriate situations, such as when an angry customer yells at me at work. It used to be especially bad when I would first go to bed. But now, I embrace the darkness and have no trouble at bedtime.

Over time, the lack of anxiety has helped me to explore some philosophical ideas I was previously afraid of. These new ideas, have been very helpful in alleviating my depression. I’m currently re-learning how to enjoy life again, with much success.

Now as far as the social difficulties go, I have had no changes in the way I process communication. But my mind operates more efficiently now.. That means I still require extra time to process incoming communication, but it goes a little quicker now than it did before. Also, I used to have trouble dealing with transitions. For example when I have down time at work, I may end up surfing the web, reading a book, or just sitting in silence. It is hard for me to transition from one of these casual activities to trying to complete a job in a timely fashion. But since the Rx, I find transitions much easier. It helps that the physical sensations of anxiety are gone.

I consider this to be a successful improvement to my life. To further define success, I would say that it has become easier to achieve my goals. One of my main goals is to just simply enjoy my life. I think its fair to say that most, if not all people have the same goal. Autism presents many different obstacles to achieving that goal.

The obstacles my son faces are different, and it would appear more extreme than my own. It has been difficult for me to accept that fact, but it is true. Kate and I both love our son the way he is. We have no desire to change his personality. We only desire to help him overcome any obstacles that block the path toward the enjoyment of life.
