### ATL_Paleo — 2013-12-06T04:41:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/solar-panels-with-grid-tie-in.8980/post-102489>

### SeaHorse — 2013-12-06T14:00:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/solar-panels-with-grid-tie-in.8980/post-102563>

Could be a pro and con list is the first step...hard to get the appropriate information specific to our needs isn't it? Not many solar engineers are going to be familiar with emf stuff...they are all about energy conservation. But maybe this isn't the issue you were asking about??

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/solar-panels-with-grid-tie-in.8980/post-102603>

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bun-creatinine-ratio-and-what-labs-show-it-is-ok-to-exercise.8982/post-102575>

I just listened to the Dec 2012 webinar on weight loss in women and was wondering if someone can help. My wife exercises a lot and eats normal or less than normal but struggles. Jack mentioned you need a certain level of mitochondrial function before you should even do any exercise but it wasn't clear to me exactly how he determines when it is ok. Seems to me leptin reset covers that and broadly you want to be leptin sensitive. My wife has a BUN/Creatinine level of 20 which was discussed briefly. Is this certain evidence that she is LR? Does it means she should avoid all exercise? If not what labs determine if someone is ok to exercise. This would be a hard sell but if needed I need some clinical data to convince her to stop for a while.

A bun/creatine level of over 10 indicates cellular dehydration. as well as bad mitochondrial function.

The only working out I've been doing for the past year & 1/2 has been CT and BW exercises after CT. Due to a time crunch in Nov - I skipped CT and tried to work out - and tore my calf muscle warming up - my Bun/Creatine level is around 20.

The individuals who've had the best results - like Mr. Pinkies have followed the protocols to a T.

labs that tell you its ok to exercise: Lipid panel, Thyroid panel, All hormones, ASI w/Salivary Melatonin. these should show you're not in a stressed state caused by Pregnenenolone steal. Though it should be noted - Misty had perfect labs - but was still having pregnenolone steal that wasn't dx until she did the Genova Dx Compete Hormone Panel.

# Thread: Cryolipolysis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cryolipolysis.8984/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### spalmi — 2013-12-06T17:40:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cryolipolysis.8984/post-102591>

In an interview I heard Jack mention Cryolipolysis. I have been reading about this process and it seems like it may be a completely different process that cold thermogenesis. According to what I read bellow 41 degrees fat cells are triggered to die, which they do immidiately, then an inflammation process takes place to elminate the waste.

### spalmi — 2013-12-11T12:51:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cryolipolysis.8984/post-103127>

I'll add why I suspect it's a different process.

Jack describes the process of cold thermo as decreasing inflammation, Cryolipolysis seems to increase inflammation.
I don't think Jack recommends temperatures cold enough to cause this process to happen.
People argue that using Water and Ice your fat cells don't get called enough for this to happen, they use a technology that pulls your fat away from your body to reduce the conflicting heating of the fad from your own body.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-follow-paul-chek.8987/post-102606>

I found Paul Chek a couple years ago and he reminds me of Dr. Kruse in the sense that he thinks radically different than everyone else. He definitely has a different perspective (coming from a holistic, spiritual point of view and specializing in movement/biomechanics), but he talks about a lot of the same things Jack does.

Paul Chek's book _How to Eat Move and Be Healthy_ talks about the importance of managing EMF and limiting artificial light at night. He probably doesn't have the same basic understanding of natures laws, but I have heard him specifically talk about and recommend Robert Becker's book _The Body Electric_.

I think it would really interesting to hear a conversation between them in a podcast. Anyone else agree?

# Thread: So you're worried about protein levels for longevity or impeding ketosis?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-youre-worried-about-protein-levels-for-longevity-or-impeding-ketosis.8993/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-07T18:21:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-youre-worried-about-protein-levels-for-longevity-or-impeding-ketosis.8993/post-102728>

Many people in the low carb paleo community are dealers in deception with regards to their beliefs on protein. I have had heated discussions with Dr. Rosedale over his view on protein in the remote past and recently with lay person low carber, Jimmy Moore, on some of the nonsense he said while he was in Australia to people who did not have the back ground to understand the nuance. I'd like to share with a blog that I think hammers the point home and puts to rest the misinformation. Now just to show you I am a fair chap.......I am being critical of two people I like here. Both of these guys truly want to help people so I can not hammer them completely. They have no ill intent. But we wont get change if the message is messy and fractionated. Carbs out of season are the real bad guy. But I want to say something about the nuance of things: Just because something isn't a lie does not mean that it isn't deceptive and can not harm someone. Neither of these guys has bad intent. A liar knows that he is a liar, but one who speaks mere "portions of truth" can be the craftsman of destruction without knowing it.............When people are misled worse things can happen.

The take home for my members: PROTEIN IS NOT YOUR ENEMY. This is why the Leptin Rx uses it.

Here is the blog. [http://caloriesproper.com/?p=4052](http://caloriesproper.com/?p=4052)

### Danco3636 — 2013-12-07T19:11:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/so-youre-worried-about-protein-levels-for-longevity-or-impeding-ketosis.8993/post-102736>

Off to do some printing and reading.

# Thread: Lower blood flow to stomach?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### seanb4 — 2013-12-08T12:36:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-102831>

Hi guys,

I have stomach issues from even simple things like plain water. My heart constantly feels like it is beating really hard and it is worse after food/water. I can also constantly see and feel my abdomen pulsating greatly in relation to my heart palpitations.

This could be my stomach causing problems (stress response) and sending more blood to my stomach. Or it could be that my stomach is struggling to get enough blood causing all my symptoms.

So my question is, could taking NSAIDs have caused permanently lower blood flow to my stomach which is causing my symptoms?

Tried googling it and didn't get much help at all.

Any help from you guys massively appreciated.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-14T13:17:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103397>

Shameless bump.

Would a damaged vagus nerve cause lower blood flow. Could this cause symptoms such as feeling sickly in proportion to amount of volume un stomach, feeling food/liquid rise back up through throat, belching long after meals, etc?

### nonchalant — 2013-12-14T17:24:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103405>

Seems to make sense that nerve damage could cause stomach problems, Sean. I've been reading one of Becker's books, and one thing he discovered is that for the animal's leg to regenerate, it needs the nerve to be in the stump. If the blood vessels were all intact, but he removed the nerve back to the backbone, the leg wouldn't regenerate. The Schwann cells provided the energy needed.

### seanb4 — 2013-12-14T19:28:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103411>

Very interesting. Do you know of any tests that could confirm this? Any potential remedies?

### nonchalant — 2013-12-14T19:55:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103414>

Not me, no. Do you know of any issues with your Vagus nerve or other nerves? Perhaps your back, any issues with the T6 or nearby vertebrae?

### seanb4 — 2013-12-14T20:10:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-blood-flow-to-stomach.8999/post-103415>

Not that I know of. The only connection I can make is when I took the NSAIDs 2yrs ago maybe this damaged the vagus nerve. This was when all my problems started. But I have no idea how to confirm this or fix this if that is the case.

# Thread: Lipomas
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### healthman — 2013-12-08T15:21:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102840>

A friend of mine has multiple lipomas, would the epi paleo diet benefit them?
And what is the cause of lipomas? Or what would be an effective alternative treatment?

Thanks

### Inger — 2013-12-08T16:06:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102843>

Healthman, I think rancid PUFAs are one big reason. There might be others but that is what comes into my mind

### healthman — 2013-12-08T16:11:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102844>

Thanks Inger
What are rancid PUFAS?

Cheers

### Inger — 2013-12-08T16:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102846>

Most vegetable oils are rancid to some degree (they all have PUFA = poly unsaturated fatty acids), also EVOO (extra virgin olive oil) is often rancid. It need to be not older than 6 months to be good. Oils in the shop are mostly way older. Most nuts from the shop are rancid too. You need to look for great quality, raw, and as fresh as it gets. Many nuts get rancid fast. Do not eat nuts if they are not great quality. They do more harm than good.

To prevent stuff getting rancid, keep them air tight and cool and dark.... I always keep my nuts in my fridge.

### healthman — 2013-12-08T16:18:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102847>

Thank you again Inger
Apart from avoiding the above, have you any ideas on what else can be done? Grounding, ct etc.

Cheers

### Dali Dula — 2013-12-08T16:45:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102853>

I developed a lipoma on upper left back years 5 years ago which coincided with T2D diagnoses. I had hoped after a few years of vlc/keto diet my lipid metabolism would change and it would resorb. this has not happened Dr. K says if they don't disappear on their own they most likely never will. this leaves surgery as the only option. since they are benign removal is considered cosmetic and not usually covered by insurance. If if doesn't go away cut it out or live with it. On the positive side it has grown no larger and others have not appeared.

### Inger — 2013-12-08T16:59:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102856>

I have one too... I think I got it maybe 10 years ago or so.. back then I was eating a almost vegetarian diet with plenty of PUFAs. Mine has not disappeared either. But it is the same size and no new lipomas have appeared either... so I really cannot give you advice what to do - just do all the good stuff and you will be fine! As Dali says, a lipoma is not dangerous and can easily be cut out/off.

I read somwhere you could put clay on it? Have not tried that myself and I doubt it will make a difference but you can try... also to apply Iodine... or icing..

### HoneyChild — 2013-12-08T18:07:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102870>

Just thinking out loud, maybe iodine applied to the lipomas would work?

[http://curezone.org/forums/am.asp?i=879285](http://curezone.org/forums/am.asp?i=879285)

### Inger — 2013-12-08T18:24:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102871>

From your link too, HoneyChild,

> Friends,
> I have been having lipoma (about 50) over last 8 years. My lumps on stomach and back started to cause pain during sleep due to compression. I saw Dr Andreas tips and I did the following:
> 1\. Mix castor oil with Himalaya Anti stress massage oil (which contains Rubia Cordifolia or Manjistha a powerful lymphatic detoxifier) and apply to lipoma once a day
> 2\. Triphala twice a day
>
> In just 3 days, my lipomas have softened and flattened and reduced in size. The smaller and newer lipomas have shrunk dramatically.
>
> As a nest step, I have ordered Manjistha in tablet form from banyanbotanicals.com (USDA organic) and it is a powerful cleanser. I will take it orally to fasten the detoxification.
>
> This is nothing short of a miracle - because I had made appointment for surgery and tried this as last ditch attempt. Thank God for guiding me.

### Destiny — 2013-12-09T00:35:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lipomas.9004/post-102924>

> [healthman said:](/goto/post?id=102847)
>
> Thank you again Inger
> Apart from avoiding the above, have you any ideas on what else can be done? Grounding, ct etc.
>

I had one on my forearm and it actually disappeared or shrank to almost nothing. I am not sure if it was keto or slimming down or both.

This fat is manufactured in your gut by bacteria to cause an epigenetic alteration of your DNA and RNA. Hydroxybutyrate is a very powerful epigenetic compound that inhibits the deacetylation of histone protein's. Hydroxybuturate is a broad spectrum HDAC inhibitor that inhibits most HDACs except the class II and class II HDACs. HDAC inhibitors can also be classified by their molecular structure. In this classification system, hydroxybutyrate is classified as a “fatty acid type” HDAC Inhibitor and is the most effective “fatty acid inhibitor” in this molecular class. It is a "Baby Maxwell demon" for this aspect of physiology.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/23and-me-shackled-by-our-up-govt.9006/post-102875>

I was that in an email that I got from them. I am so glad that I bought mine last month, like on the 17th but I am still waiting for the results. This is just big government not wanting to let people be responsible for their own health and rely on the government to tell them what the should and should not do.

From the email I got.

"Dear 23andMe Customers,

I'm writing to update you on our conversation with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and how it impacts you.

If you are a customer whose kit was purchased before November 22, 2013, your 23andMe experience will not change. You will be able to access both ancestry and health-related information as you always have.

23andMe has complied with the FDA's directive and stopped offering new consumers access to health-related genetic results while the company moves forward with the agency's regulatory review processes. Be sure to refer to our 23andMe blog for updates.

We stand behind the data we have generated for customers. Our lab partner adheres to strict quality standards that are part of the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments of 1988 - known as CLIA. These are the same standards used in the majority of other health and disease-related tests.

You are among the first people in the world to ever get access to their genomes. You are genetic pioneers. Thank you for your ongoing support and we look forward to continuing to serve you.

Anne Wojcicki
Co-founder and CEO, 23andMe "

# Thread: Scaling Concepts and Information, Food For Thought
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scaling-concepts-and-information-food-for-thought.9011/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2013-12-08T19:37:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scaling-concepts-and-information-food-for-thought.9011/post-102883>

So my relatively straightforward question regarding PQQ and cofactors that Dr. Kruse answered with "Manganese is a big deal and yes it is a transition metal" led me on an interesting journey. I asked myself the question "why would Manganese be a big deal here and what does it do anyway?" A few months ago when I suspected that I was not methylating properly I went on a similar journey with Molybdenum. It started with a sentence in a seminar portfolio that listed "B12, B6, NAC and Molybdenum." I also remembered that the presenter had said that if there are smell issues, then it is probably a Molybdenum deficiency. In the literature on Molybdenum, I came across several interesting facts.

1\. the USDA tested soils from the 1950-80's. Every 10 years, soil levels declined by 50%, every 10 years. Then they just stopped testing....
2\. no one could agree on an RDA, but eventually they came up with the range of something like 10-80mcg/day and it appears that most people get this much in their diet.
3\. then I found a 2004 study where they tested how much Molybdenum it would take to actually raise someone's levels and they found that one needed at least 500mcg/day. They also found that most of the supplemental stuff was excreted in 1 hour (I believe 98% was the number they used)

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14972348](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/14972348)

4 then I found a study from a naturopath in 1991 who described how candida sufferers seemed to have Molybdenum deficiencies which explained why they got so sick because they could not detoxify the aldehydes from the candida waste products. He described how they typically required 300 or more mcg of Molybdenum/day for several months.

[http://www.arthritistrust.org/wp-co.../Molybdenum-for-Candida-albicans-Patients.pdf](http://www.arthritistrust.org/wp-content/uploads/2013/03/Molybdenum-for-Candida-albicans-Patients.pdf)

5\. So based on this research I started taking 2-3mg/day in divided doses and magically I could handle a high fat and moderate protein "primal" diet where before I would get really sick on it after a few weeks.
6\. I have not seen the decline in my need for it after more than 3 months, so when Dr. Kruse started pointing at transition metals and EMF, I thought well what is different now than in 1991 and how would it effect this???
7\. Last night someone suggested that I simply do the Spectracell analysis to see what my micronutrient status is, and while I was grateful for the suggestion, I knew that would not really resolve the issue for me.
8\. Voila! The EMF's are now creating subclinical functional deficiencies in all of us because even though we have enough of the transition element in our system, the EMF's are distorting their function at the atomic level to the point where they do not work as hoped for anymore.
9\. In the case of Managanese for example, it functions better in one valence than another, I believe Mn3+ vs Mn2+ and we know that the electron behavior is radically influenced by EMF's in biology and elsewhere.

So based on this, I would hypothesize that for many of us, we need much higher concentrations of transition trace elements in our diet and system in order to have anything close to reliable function and utilization if our genetics have not already predisposed us to problems with this. This then brings the question of "UL" or generally accepted safe upper limits and toxic levels, etc.. Keeps everyone on their toes...

With regard to Manganese, it is an interesting player in all biochemistry to say the least...As always I appreciated how much we do not know about it as freely admitted by the scientists that have published some of the most recent research, the following was published in 2012:

"Manganese levels in the body are therefore tightly regulated, but the responsible protein(s) remain incompletely known."

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22341971](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22341971)

In the "follow the money world", there is not much money to study unpatentable trace minerals. Note that with Molybdenum, it was found to be effective enough in the treatment of prostate cancer that they actually did make a drug out of it by binding it in a novel way and patenting it. Instead of a few dollars for an amino acid chelate, Oncologists get to charge thousands for a "drug". Nice work if you can get it.

My last piece is to ask myself "what would not work so well in my body if I did not have enough of this?" In the case of Manganese I went to a favorite source text: Roman J. Kutsky's "Handbook of Vitamins, Minerals and Hormones." An older text, but a lot of information about each thing in one well organized place...I discovered that with Manganese, I would have trouble with using some B vitamins, producing acetylcholine and issues with production and metabolism of both thyroid and sex hormones. Pretty major issues and all things that I am working on and have seen evidence of suboptimal function...

Last, but not least, without biologically active Manganese, I would not be able to utilize PQQ with any reliability and thus......

And really last, Spectracell numbers for trace transition elements based on the average levels for the population they use to get their numbers will not give me a reliable indication as to whether I have enough biologically active element in my system to do what I need it to do...

[http://www.spectracell.com/clinicians/products/mnt/](http://www.spectracell.com/clinicians/products/mnt/)

> Micronutrient Testing from SpectraCell Laboratories
>
> SpectraCell Laboratories, Inc. is a CLIA accredited (#45D0710715) clinical laboratory that specializes in micronutrient testing. This patented process resulted from 18 years of research at the University of Texas. **The micronutrient tests measures how micronutrients are actually functioning within your patients’ white blood cells.** These tests allow nutritional assessment of your patients for a broad variety of clinical conditions including arthritis, cancer, cardiovascular risk, diabetes, various immunological disorders, metabolic disorders and micronutrient deficiencies. Also offered by SpectraCell is a specialized profile of homocysteine, lipids and proteins to assess cardiovascular risk.

> SpectraCell’s Tests Are More Advanced Than other Laboratory Tests
>
> Before the introduction of our tests, many diagnosis and risk assessments were based on clinical observation and measurements of static levels of certain nutrients in serum. Static serum levels are not always representative indicators for assessing cell metabolism and utilization.
>
> SpectraCell’s micronutrient testing offers a unique means to scientifically assess the intracellular requirements of micronutrients that play an important role in overall health and wellness of your patients. **Our tests measure the biochemical function** of vitamins, minerals, amino acids and antioxidants, providing a powerful clinical assessment tool for your practice.
>
> Our panels are designed to provide you with the most comprehensive nutritional analysis available. As the only lab that can offer a **truly functional intracellular testing**, SpectraCell also provides you with targeted nutrient repletion recommendations for those vitamins and minerals found to be deficient.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scaling-concepts-and-information-food-for-thought.9011/post-102985>

I looked over the Spectracell methodology and my preliminary assessment is that the presence of the transition metals in the lymphocytes does not tell us how they function in the presence of EMF's. Furthermore, their stress testing "Spectrotox" is an In Vitro not an In Vivo measurement. I would hypothesize that there may well be significant differences in Lymphocyte function inside and outside of the larger organism. Consider a houseplant versus the same plant in a field or forest. Many additional variables that when one considers Quantum effects may significantly impact function and viability. Still, their testing appears to be a very useful measurement and I am sure they are useful clinically. I will consider them for my clients and myself going forward.

# Thread: WHY DOES FBG GO UP WITH AN EPI PALEO TEMPLATE?  physiologic IR
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-fbg-go-up-with-an-epi-paleo-template-physiologic-ir.9015/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-09T03:37:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-fbg-go-up-with-an-epi-paleo-template-physiologic-ir.9015/post-102940>

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2007/10/physiological-insulin-resistance.html](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2007/10/physiological-insulin-resistance.html)

### TheKid — 2013-12-09T12:48:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-fbg-go-up-with-an-epi-paleo-template-physiologic-ir.9015/post-102958>

That post is an oldie but a goodie. I agree with one of the commentor's points that it is probably wrong to call this phenomenon "insulin resistance" because you only actually experience true clinical insulin resistance when presented with an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) or when suddenly exposed to high carbs levels after a period of low carb eating.

I would expect that fully following the Epi Paleo template would avoid all of this because of the seasonal eating. In the dead of winter, you would never encounter a carb load, and would never therefore show any evidence of clinical insulin resistance. And eating seasonal plant foods would first introduce small amounts of carbs in the spring, which would then ramp up into the summer and then peak in the fall with your starchy squashes or tubers and other fruits etc. This would allow you to gently and appropriately adjust throughout the seasons.

It seems to me that the problem, then, is cycling carbs on a weekly or other periodic basis whereby you are shocking the system. You either need to be strict low carb or you need to be really careful about eating in season. And if you are strict low carb, you probably shouldn't come out of that state abruptly. You should come out in the spring so as to follow the normal natural cycle.

### Dali Dula — 2013-12-09T15:38:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-does-fbg-go-up-with-an-epi-paleo-template-physiologic-ir.9015/post-102964>

Jack, back in March of this year I asked this question in Ask Jack:

How is it possible for a T2D/Hashi's to have an increase in average BG from 133 to 145 and yet have A1c decrease from 5.7 to 5.0 during same 90 day period?

You said perhaps spurious lab or RBC turnover. The Tafur article offers another possible explanation, eh? Although my a1c in subsequent labs has been rising (last lab in sept was 6.1) I continue to see average BG higher than a1c would predict. The estimated BG presented on my labs is lower than my estimates from my BG meter. Previous to 3/13 I did not notice this discrepancy. This seems in line with Tafur's observations for long term low carb.
The wild card in all this that even Tafur is not considering is cold adaptation. Brave new world. I will never find a doc who gets this.

### cinnamon — 2013-12-09T18:27:35+0200

Fascinating how a grasshopper can morph into a locust (and back!) given environmental triggers.

excerpt:

"In the most infamous species, Schistocerca gregaria, the desert locust of Africa, the Middle East and Asia, these phase changes (as this morphing process is called) occur when crowding spurs a temporary spike in serotonin levels, which causes changes in gene expression so widespread and powerful they alter not just the hopperâ€™s behaviour but its appearance and form. Legs and wings shrink. Subtle camo colouring turns conspicuously garish. The brain grows to manage the animalâ€™s newly complicated social world, which includes the fact that, if a locust moves too slowly amid its million cousins, the cousins directly behind might eat it."​

Is this similar to a person transforming from warm-adapted SAD eater to cold-adapted epi-paleo eater? I'm thinking of my own change in body composition and I know others have noted remarkable physical changes. Our genes didn't change, but maybe the way they are expressed has?

Is the mediocrity of modern man just a phenotypic expression of the current lousy field? Are neolithic diseases a field impact on genetic expression? Our rapid adaptability (facilitated by genetic expression that responds quickly to the environment) has probably been the cause of our success as a species - but could it also make us more vulnerable?

So much to think about.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T06:16:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103033>

So..........if you are following this.......The theories and discoveries of thousands of physicists since the 1930s have resulted in a remarkable insight into the fundamental structure of matter: everything in the universe is found to be made from a few basic building blocks called fundamental particles, governed by four fundamental forces. Our best understanding of how these particles and three of the forces are related to each other is encapsulated in the Standard Model of particle physics. Developed in the early 1970s, it has successfully explained almost all experimental results and precisely predicted a wide variety of phenomena. Over time and through many experiments, the Standard Model has become established as a well-tested physics theory. — Matter particles All matter around us is made of elementary particles, the building blocks of matter. These particles occur in two basic types called quarks and leptons. Each group consists of six particles, which are related in pairs, or “generations”. The lightest and most stable particles make up the first generation, whereas the heavier and less stable particles belong to the second and third generations. All stable matter in the universe is made from particles that belong to the first generation; any heavier particles quickly decay to the next most stable level. The six quarks are paired in the three generations – the “up quark” and the “down quark” form the first generation, followed by the “charm quark” and “strange quark”, then the “top quark” and “bottom (or beauty) quark”. Quarks also come in three different “colours” and only mix in such ways as to form colourless objects. The six leptons are similarly arranged in three generations – the “electron” and the “electron neutrino”, the “muon” and the “muon neutrino”, and the “tau” and the “tau neutrino”. The electron, the muon and the tau all have an electric charge and a sizeable mass, whereas the neutrinos are electrically neutral and have very little mass. — Forces and carrier particles There are four fundamental forces at work in the universe: the strong force, the weak force, the electromagnetic force, and the gravitational force. They work over different ranges and have different strengths. Gravity is the weakest but it has an infinite range. The electromagnetic force also has infinite range but it is many times stronger than gravity. The weak and strong forces are effective only over a very short range and dominate only at the level of subatomic particles. Despite its name, the weak force is much stronger than gravity but it is indeed the weakest of the other three. The strong force, as the name suggests, is the strongest of all four fundamental interactions. — Three of the fundamental forces result from the exchange of force-carrier particles, which belong to a broader group called “bosons”. Particles of matter transfer discrete amounts of energy by exchanging bosons with each other. Each fundamental force has its own corresponding boson – the strong force is carried by the “gluon”, the electromagnetic force is carried by the “photon”, and the “W and Z bosons” are responsible for the weak force. Although not yet found, the “graviton” should be the corresponding force-carrying particle of gravity. The Standard Model includes the electromagnetic, strong and weak forces and all their carrier particles, and explains well how these forces act on all of the matter particles. However, the most familiar force in our everyday lives, gravity, is not part of the Standard Model, as fitting gravity comfortably into this framework has proved to be a difficult challenge. The quantum theory used to describe the micro world, and the general theory of relativity used to describe the macro world, are difficult to fit into a single framework. No one, yet, has managed to make the two mathematically compatible in the context of the Standard Model.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T06:18:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103034>

While physics is infinitely deep, biology is infinitely complex.........so how do we resolve this?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T06:31:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103035>

These difference may, however, mainly reflect the fact that in physics we have a broad general theory of how all things work in unison, called the Standard Model. Yet, biology is devoid of any unifying theory. Do I think this is what is holding biology and medicine back at a fundamental level? Yes. Dawkins and those of his ilk have tried for 35 years to make Darwin theory of evolution mesh with Mendel's genetics. He uses slick words and writes brilliantly to make us think they mesh and are symmetric with one another.........but they are not. Why? If the subatomic world has particle that can morph their own form why can life? It means the level of action of biology is also at a subatomic level and not a gene level. We have sepnt far too much time on molecular nuts and bolts of genes and it has gotten us no where in medicine but more confused. My next post gets to the crux of this crossroads. If you sense I think Dawkins is dead wrong you're right. If you think I hold him in low regard for being wrong you'd be a fool. We need people to stick their neck out with guesses but we need the experiments to back up the theory or all we have is fancy prose. that is what Dawkins brings to biology. A great writer who cultivates interest but has nothing to back him up experimentally. He is as religious in his beliefs as the Christians he persecutes for believing in a deity. That irony is not lost on me, because everything Dawkins believes today is what I believes as well 10 years ago before I looked at the three fundamental parts of nature that life uses to organize around. As we move towards a grand unified theory in biology, the field will become greatly deeper as physics has. Physics has CERN to experimental prove theoretical physics guess. Biology has no CERN. I'm looking to change that. In 2012 we finally found the Higgs boson. This helps us understand mass a lot better because the Higgs boson is the charge carrier for mass. It give matter its mass. At the same time of this discovery, disturbing new observations in physics that require extensions of the Standard Model are making that field more complex. Now we have dark energy, dark matter, quantum gravity and gravitons to explain.

I have a hunch that what is going on in CERN is going to create biology Standard Model soon. And Dawkins will be looked at as a foot note in the path to wisdom like Lamarck was.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T06:32:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103036>

Systems biology based upon coherence and cooperative quantum effects offers an approach to dealing with the complexity, but is not likely to get far until we discover ways of quantifying inter and intra-cellular signal strengths and creating equations that reflect how signaling works in real organisms.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T06:38:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103037>

So why do I think Dawkins is important to remember as a dying dinosaur theorist? It is important not to sweep away all old models because newer more-sophisticated models show they are wrong. In both physics and biology, for any phenomena there are multiple levels of explanation which can all be valid and useful on their own terms. Some very simple explanation can be very useful despite the fact that from a more sophisticated viewpoint an explanation is crude or incorrect. Newtonian physics is all you need to design the mechanical components of a well functioning Mercedes or Cadillac, so his work was still useful in a sort of way. Eating a lot of fresh fruits and vegetables and getting plenty of exercise is still decent general medical health advice. Eleven-dimensional String Theory may bring the two grand theories of Particle Physics and Relativity closer together but is quite useless for a pool or tennis player in their every day life. Details of the steroid pathways in biochemistry fascinate people but few really understand the complexity because they do not understand how charge separation decodes the maps they contain.

It is important not to confuse the map with the actual territory that these metabolic pathways contain. No matter how effective we become in creating a sophisticated maps of biochemistry, the result will still be only a map and will miss some of the features of the real territory that life acts within. This is what I am looking to change.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T06:43:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103038>

We are descendents of animals that lived underground in fear of dinosaurs. Our ancestors grunted and howled and waved their limbs to signal to each other. This gave us an important evolutionary advantage over our nearest ancestors. We eventually learned how to communicate some of the essential features of our territory to other animals in our species. This was also advantageous to our mutual survival to get us to the present. That is still the situation and is what we are trying to do here with big idea theories today. Our cherished biologic theories are sophisticated descendents of our grunts and howls. This is why Dawkins ramblings are important. Even biology's grand theories are no more or less than that. Let’s not get so far carried away by our complicated ideas that we forget that basic principle. Afterall QED says we are the easiest person to fool with our own ideas.

### Josh — 2013-12-10T06:43:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103039>

Thank you for an exceptionally coherent and straightforward review of the current limit of physics as a discipline. At the point that I last looked at this when I was taking physics at Bryn Mawr College in the summer of 1988, I remember my professor was studying electron cloud probability and tried to explain these things to me in the context of his work. I got that the atom was not just a static entity at the lower limit of matter and that there were clearly many smaller dynamic forces and particles at play inside what had previously been my working limit on the scale of matter. 3 years later, completely by accident as best I can tell, I found myself in India and strangely enough, one my first experiences in the ashram that I found myself in was to watch a video of J. Krishnamurti talking about physics and time with David Bohm the physicist. I was struck by the contrast between JK the "Mystic" and Bohm the "Physicist". I admit I was so overwhelmed with India that I do not recall what they were talking about, yet looking at Krishnamurti, he seemed far "vaster" than anything "physics" could define. I later read his biography "The Years of Awakening" and U. G. Krishnamurti's "The Biology of Enlightenment" both of which are arguably at the other end of the epigenetic/evolutionary debate. I met too many people who confirmed such experiences to dismiss them as fantastic or imaginary. Over the years I have met too many people that can manifest things that are considered physically "impossible". Many of these can more or less be modeled through subatomic physics, but as of yet they can not be replicated. If I did not have such a peripatetic body, I would probably still be in the hills of India drinking tea and growing vegetables. Somehow I have been drawn back to reconcile experience and theory and to find a meeting place to apply it to life in the physical universe. Your explanations are clear and short enough that I get to the concept I need to open the next door. Like you I am what I guess is called a "biohacker" which was always part of the yogic, sufi and taoist traditions that I admire. I am always looking for experiments to make in my own life that demonstrate and reveal all of this. For me without application, this stuff is no fun. Thanks for the invitation.

In essence what is emerging for me is "follow the energy" that we can currently track fairly well with electron flows. I have looked at Popp's work on Photons and the theories that say cells communicate over distances with photons, bacteria as well. In addition there is now the concept that subatomic particles and atomic particles can have a "resonant" informational individuality. Combine this with non-local effects and it makes it a far more dynamic play and at the same time more straightforward. For now, we have no reliable and readily available ways to track transitory phenomena such as compounds that may only exist in a plasma state in the brain for fractions of a second. Yet, I imagine if we follow the flow of particles/energy, it may become clear that such intermediate states must exist to get from A to B so to speak. I ask myself, "how can I experience water as a structured crystalline phenomena in my daily life"? "Where would I look and how might it vary"? And I know that many of the great physicists went to the edge of madness on this path. For perspective:

One day the Lord Vishnu was sitting in a deep cave within a far mountain meditating with his disciple.
Upon the completion of the meditation the disciple was so moved that he prostrated himself at
Vishnu’s feet and begged to be able to perform some service for his Lord in gratitude. Vishnu smiled
and shook his head, ”It will be most difficult for you to repay me in actions for what I have just given
you freely.” ”Please Lord,” the disciple said, ”allow me the grace of serving you.” ”Very well,” Vishnu
relented, ”I would like a nice cool cup of water.” ”At once Lord,” the disciple said, and he ran down
the mountain singing in joy.
After a while he came to a small house at the edge of a beautiful valley and knocked at the door.
”May I please have a cool cup of water for my Master,” he called. ”We are wandering sannyasins
and have no home on this earth.” A wondrous maiden answered his call, and looked at him with
undisguised adoration. ”Ah,” she whispered, ”you must serve that holy saint upon the far mountain.
Please, Good sir, enter my house and bestow your blessing therein.” ”Forgive my rudeness,” he
answered, ”but I am in haste. I must return to my Master with his water immediately.” ”Surely, just
your blessing won’t upset him. After all he is a great holy man, and as his disciple you are obligated
to help those of us who are less fortunate. Please,” she repeated, ”just your blessing for my humble
house. It is such an honor to have you here and to be enabled to serve the Lord through you.”
So the story goes, he relented, and entered the house and blessed all therein. And then it was time
for dinner, and he was persuaded to stay and further the blessing by partaking of her food (thereby
making it also holy), and since it was so late – and so far back to the mountain, and he might slip
in the dark and spill the water – he was persuaded to sleep there that night and get an early start
in the morning. But in the morning, the cows were in pain because there was no one to help her
milk them, and if he could just help her this once (after all, cows are sacred to the Lord Krishna, and
should not be in pain) it would be so wondrous.
And days became weeks, and still he remained. They were married, and had numerous children.
He worked the land well and brought forth good harvests. He purchased more land and put it under
cultivation, and soon his neighbors looked to him for advice and help, and he gave it freely. His family
prospered. Temples were built through his effort, schools and hospitals replaced the jungle, and the
valley became a jewel upon the earth. Harmony prevailed where only wilderness had been, and
many flocked to the valley as news of its prosperity and peace spread throughout the land. There
was no poverty or disease there, and all men sang their praises to God as they worked. He watched
his children grow and have their own children, and it was good.
One day as an old man, as he stood upon a low hill facing the valley, he thought of all that had
transpired since he had arrived: farms and happy prosperity as far as the eye could see. And he
was pleased.
Suddenly there was a great tidal wave, and as he watched, it flooded the whole valley, and in an
instant all was gone. Wife, children, farms, schools, neighbors – all gone. He stared, bewildered, at
the holocaust that spread before him.

And then he saw riding upon the face of the waters his Master, Vishnu, who looked at him and smiled
sadly, and said, ”I’m still waiting for my water!”

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T06:48:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-neo-darwinism-is-blinding-us-to-qed-principles.9016/post-103040>

# Thread: Dr. Oz and Dr. Weil

### nicld — 2013-12-09T05:02:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-oz-and-dr-weil.9017/post-102945>

I used to like him but now I think he is a sell out and is not consistent on anything and feeds into women's insecurities about their weight.

With that being said, I still watch a bit of some episodes once in a while.

Just recently saw one that he did an episode called Should you be worried, with Dr. Andrew Weil that made me go hmmmm, society might be getting it, at least a little.

Mentioned that the thing that people should be most worried about is sugar, well DUGHHHHHHHH.

First they talked about if Wi-Fi was dangerous. He downplayed it quite a bit but did say it was something people should be cautious about. They also talked about saturated fats versus sugar and the effects on the arties (sat fat won!!) and full body scanners, Those should be avoided at all costs. Next time I fly, not going to go through one.

Fluoride in toothpaste was dismissed as not to be worried about and said that the fluorinated water is good for kids, whatever.

The one thing that I did take away was the use of coenzyme Q10. This is not one of the supplements that I am currently taking but will start (trying to find the balance of what to take and what I can get with diet). The amount of food that one would need to eat to get a beneficial amount is way way way more than I can ever eat in a day. 4 pounds of sardines or 8 pounds of red meat, uhmmm no can do. In this case it would be more beneficial to take the 120mg a day.

Anyway, at least some main stream media is at least mentioning it and maybe it will get more people to think about what Wi-Fi is doing and that sugar is bad.

[http://www.doctoroz.com/episode/controversial-health-scares-are-you-safe](http://www.doctoroz.com/episode/controversial-health-scares-are-you-safe)

# Thread: New in the News:  A transition metal getting called out.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-in-the-news-a-transition-metal-getting-called-out.9024/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-10T05:35:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-in-the-news-a-transition-metal-getting-called-out.9024/post-103026>

Tungsten is that metal and it is hard, rare metal under standard conditions when it is manufactured in its metal state. Here is the rub, in nature on Earth it is never found in its metal form. This means that life forms would have never ever once been exposed to this metal unbound in a reaction. So what do you think happens when life meets a new transition metal?

[http://www.foxnews.com/health/2013/...-consumer-electronics-may-double-stroke-risk/](http://www.foxnews.com/health/2013/11/12/metal-found-in-many-consumer-electronics-may-double-stroke-risk/)

The metal has the highest melting point of all metals, and at temperatures over 1650 oC also has the highest tensile strength. Pure tungsten is ductile, and tungsten wires, even of a very small diameter, have a very high tensile strength. This is why the semiconductor industry is using it. When in a compound tungsten exists mostly in the oxidation state VI. So that means it has a propensity to have many D shell electrons which can delocalize and cause issues. Tungsten Electron Configuration: \[Xe\] 6s2 4f14 5d4

Number of Energy Levels of electrons: 6

First Energy Level: 2
Second Energy Level: 8
Third Energy Level: 18
Fourth Energy Level: 32
Fifth Energy Level: 12
Sixth Energy Level: 2

Tungsten is one of the five major refractory metals. These are metals with very high resistance to heat and wear. Another reason tech companies like using them. The other refractory metals are niobium, molybdenum, tantalum, and rhenium.

Tungsten is also used as the filament in halogen tungsten lamps. These lamps use halogens like bromine and iodine to prevent the tungsten filament from degrading and are therefore more energy efficient than standard incandescent light bulbs.

High speed steel (which can cut material at higher speeds than carbon steel), contains up to 18% tungsten.

Tungsten is used in heavy metal alloys because of its hardness and in high-temperature applications such as welding.

Tungsten carbide (WC or W2C) is extremely hard and is used to make drills. It is also used for jewelry because of its hardness and wear resistance.

Tungsten is made in militaries explodables.......so soldiers and people in war will be expected to suffer its consequances (PTSD ANYONE?)

So why might we be getting tungsten in us and why does it cause problems? Could it be injectables?

[http://www.americanpharmaceuticalre...from-a-Formulation-Scientist-s-Point-of-View/](http://www.americanpharmaceuticalreview.com/Featured-Articles/38372-Challenges-in-the-Development-of-Pre-filled-Syringes-for-Biologics-from-a-Formulation-Scientist-s-Point-of-View/)
Tungsten

Tungsten pins are routinely used by manufacturers of glass pre-fillable syringes in the syringe manufacturing process to keep the bore open while the cone is being shaped. The shaping process requires application of high temperatures. Although tungsten is quite resistant, metal particles may be deposited from the pins during the process. In addition, oxidation processes occur leading to formation of tungsten oxides that may subsequently deposit in the bore and funnel area of the syringe. In this respect it is important to keep in mind that deposits of tungsten species can occur in staked-in needle as well as luer type pre-fillable syringes (although staked-in needle pre-fillable syringes contain the lowest amount of extractable tungsten as most of the bore is covered by the needle \[11\]).

When exposed to an aqueous solution with an acidic pH of about 6 or below, both tungsten metal and tungsten oxide can form a variety of soluble polyanionic species which can induce protein aggregation and formation of proteinaceous particles \[12-15\]. Protein oxidation was reported to be induced by tungsten as well leading to protein aggregation \[16, 17\]. Other formulation parameters which have been reported to have an influence on tungsten induced protein degradation besides pH are protein concentration and ionic strength \[18\]. Thus, pH, protein concentration and ionic strength of a formulation should be taken into account in the risk assessment for tungsten compatibility of a formulation.

The syringe-to-syringe variability of residual tungsten can be significant. Stability testing in the actual PFS can to a certain degree assess also compatibility with tungsten, however, with the caveat of variability of these deposits. Other approaches to assess tungsten compatibility are e.g. spiking with soluble tungsten species (e.g. Na2WO4), spiking with an extract from used tungsten pins or spiking with an extract from the glass barrel. It should be mentioned that depending on the approach chosen, results can significantly differ. Spiking with an extract of a used tungsten pins induced protein degradation more effectively than spiking with soluble tungsten species including WO3, H2WO4 and Na2WO4 in an example by Jiang et al. \[14\].

Overall, the number of reported tungsten incompatibilities in biological products at tungsten concentrations relevant for PFS is low, given the widespread use of PFS in the field of biologics.

Nevertheless, pre-fillable syringes with low(er) amounts of residual tungsten levels are meanwhile available by different syringe vendors. Some suppliers also tried replacing tungsten pins by other materials, such as transition metals. However, also leachables from these components cannot be ruled out to generate significant and potentially more severe issues than tungsten.

BOOM

Coincidence?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-in-the-news-a-transition-metal-getting-called-out.9024/post-103031>

So why does tngsten cause problems in the brain? The low thermal expansion and high melting point and tensile strength of tungsten originate from **very strong covalent bonds** formed between tungsten atoms by the 5d electrons. These bonds alter protein folding in the tertiary and quaternary structure in brain molecules that use MO and Cu and Zinc. Did you know that at the center of an insulin molecule is a Zn atom?

### HoneyChild — 2013-12-10T17:40:10+0200

### Josh — 2013-12-10T19:11:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-in-the-news-a-transition-metal-getting-called-out.9024/post-103071>

Competition between cations for binding sites and the strength of the bonds formed is a fundamental variable everywhere and when we change the mix, we change the likely outcome. The bioavailabilty of organic material and nutrients and the availability of and holding of water in soils is an area where this is seen. "Salty" soils simply have too many strong cations such as Calcium tightly bound to the organic material. Because of this, water does not penetrate or stay around very long. When the soil is contaminated with strong metal cations that were not a normal part of the system, they often disrupt the nutrient cycling by more or less permanently binding the anionic sites that are part of the "normal" nutrient cycle and disrupt the cycle until when and if something or someone comes along and remedies it. Consider the use of zeolites to remedy the soils in post-Chernobyl Bulgaria...The soil ecosystem is more or less analogous to the human biological terrain and provides many good examples for study. Consider that the human intestine is considered by many to be the most complex and diverse ecosystem on the planet...imagine the Amazon and then some. Nutrient cycling and or the lack of it is pretty much it...no? Take away the nutrient cycling in the Amazon and it looks like someone paved the place with Iron, laterization....

# Thread: 13th century Optimal fitness
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/13th-century-optimal-fitness.9030/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JoeBranca — 2013-12-10T21:31:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/13th-century-optimal-fitness.9030/post-103079>

Came across this passage last night in a [biography](http://www.amazon.com/Genghis-Khan-Making-Modern-World-ebook/dp/B000FCK206) of Ghengis Khan.

> "The second unique characteristic of the Mongol army was that it traveled without a commissary or cumbersome supply train other than its large reserve of horses that always accompanied the soldiers. As they moved, they milked the animals, slaughtered them for food, and fed themselves from hunting and looting. Marco Polo alleged that the Mongol warriors could travel ten days without stopping to make a fire or heat food, that they drank horsesâ€™ blood, and that each man carried with him ten pounds of dried milk paste, putting one pound of it in a leather flask of water each day to make his meal. The warrior carried strips of dried meat and dried curd with him that he could chew while riding; and when he had fresh meat, but no time to cook it, he put the raw flesh under his saddle so it would soon be softened and edible."
>
> "The Chinese noted with surprise and disgust the ability of the Mongol warriors to survive on little food and water for long periods; according to one, the entire army could camp without a single puff of smoke since they needed no fires to cook. Compared to the Jurched soldiers, the Mongols were much healthier and stronger. The Mongols consumed a steady diet of meat, milk, yogurt, and other dairy products, and they fought men who lived on gruel made from various grains. The grain diet of the peasant warriors stunted their bones, rotted their teeth, and left them weak and prone to disease. In contrast, the poorest Mongol soldier ate mostly protein, thereby giving him strong teeth and bones. Unlike the Jurched soldiers, who were dependent on a heavy carbohydrate diet, the Mongols could more easily go a day or two without food."

High animal protein diet much of it raw, able to go long periods without food, sprung from the cold Eurasian Steppe, and boundless "warrior" energy superior to every sugar-burning foe. ; )

Vasopressin is normally released at night when water production in the brain is diminished. MS is an inflammatory disease of myelin-producing cells (oligodendroglia cells) that become filled with deuterium in Kreb's bicycle to block the aquaporin (AQA) 4 gates. # Thread: Optimal Treatment of Hashimoto's Disease - Searching for the unifying theory!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-treatment-of-hashimotos-disease-searching-for-the-unifying-theory.9043/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Arsaneus — 2013-12-11T18:15:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-treatment-of-hashimotos-disease-searching-for-the-unifying-theory.9043/post-103138>

Hello folks,

since I'm new here, I'd like to give you all an overview about me, before I get on with my question. Please read everything since I think many of this will be totally new ideas that you've never read in this form anywhere before. Thanks!

**My story so far**
I've been experiencing hypothyroid symptoms since about 2006, not knowing where they came from. At first, I ignored them, but in the end of 2011 they became so obvious (12 to 15 hours of sleep every day), that I finally went to the doctor's. They all told me I was fine. I've been diagnosed with Hashimoto's Thyreoiditis in April 2012 by my own effort of measuring Thyreoglobuline antibodies (TG-AB), which were the only definite clue that I have this disease at all. Other antibodies (Thyreoperoxidase aka TPO-AB, and TSH receptor antibodies) were negative on several occasions. Ultrasound is completely normal with a thyroid volume of approximately 15 mL total. No unusual echo patterns or anything like that at all, I've seen it with my own eyes.

The TG-AB were the only clue to what's causing my symptoms, they were about three times above the normal range. My mother also has Hashimoto's Thyreoiditis but didn't told me. Since the antibodies convinced one of the doctors to help me, I got a prescription of Levothyroxine. I started with 25 mcg of T4 and increased the dosage in 4 to 6 week intervals, until my TSH suppressed. I therefore went through the whole range of possible dosages, from being hypothyroid to being hyperthyroid, without any significant effect on my symptoms - besides, that I only needed to shower two times a day with boiling hot water instead of three times, and I only slept for 10 hours instead of 12 to 15 hours.

I also had an alarming amount of micronutritient deficites, which of course no doctor believed until I paid for the necessary lab tests myself and showed them the results made in "their" own lab. Needless to say, that I had to treat them myself.

Additionally, I had massive gastrointestinal problems. My bowel was bulged like a fat man (although I'm 1,81m tall, my weight is 63kg and my body fat is about 7%, no matter how much I eat, that's been normal for me all my life by the way). But of course, nobody believed me because a skinny person like me with a swallen abdomen just looks thin and skinny anyway to every doctor. I couldn't eat any tiny amount of lactose or fructose, and even histamine was problematic. I felt like my whole body just stopped functioning and was falling apart.

I later found out by myself that I've been suffering from gluten intolerance (not celiac disease! this was ruled out very consequently via antibody tests and gastroduodenoscopy!) all the time, which has actually been approved to be a "real" disease a few years ago, but cannot be measured by any lab test. With eliminating gluten, my symptoms got better within 4 weeks, and were almost gone after 8 weeks. By accident, I discovered that soy also causes problems for me. By eliminating both, gluten and soy, I can now eat everything I want again, except these two of course. Nobody told me that either, in fact both doctors and internet communities constantly tried to discourage me on my way to finding out what really is the underlying problem... .

With nothing left but the thyroid disease, I now try to figure out how to treat this, so that all of my symptoms disappear completely. This has been particularly annoing since everybody, both doctors and internet communities, tell you a different story everytime, but always based upon observations for themselves only, and (almost) never based upon scientifically proven facts or scientifically plausible hypothesis/ideas. Needless to say, that I had to fight my own way through this again.

After reading much, and by this I mean people's stories and scientific publications as well, and some testing of ideas by myself and some friends (in a German Hashimoto community), I came finally closer and closer to the solution. Then, I noticed an odd case of well-being in this forum, which seems to prove one of my theories. This is why I'm here, and what I want to talk to you about.

**What I've accomplished so far**
Without having to go into too much details, I can say that I managed to eliminate most of the problems that the usual methods of substitution thyroid hormones carry within. I'm only sleeping for 7 to 9 hours a day, I'm finally having good grades at university again, and I even started to do sports again three times a week (by sports I mean heavy lifting in the gym, because I obviously want to gain some weight). This is what is possible to me now without the need to suppress my TSH. Yeah, you read that correctly.

I currently use a physiologic approach, trying to mimic the function of a healthy thyroid gland, by using a combination therapy of synthetic T3+T4. That's the most brief description I can give. The basic tricks are to take T4 two times a day (directly after waking up, without eating for the next 30 min. - and directly before going to bed, without eating 2 hours before), and to take T3 four times a day in intervals of approximately 5 hours. Both is designed to minimizing peaks in the fT3 and fT4, and to optimize equal supply of thyroid hormones throughout 24 hours a day. Another important trick is to choose the total dosage wisely, so hat TSH is not suppressed; there are scientifically proven TSH receptors outside (!) the thyroid gland and pituitary gland, which no one knows of what they're good for, and I don't want to shut the TSH down unless we're absolutely sure that it wouldn't affect any of the body's other functions. Besides, this way the body can produce new thyroid hormones even in between two dosages one takes. Believe me, I feel like a new man since I started that method, although I still have a lot of symptoms and I still don't feel completely normal/healthy.

I tracked down the problem to an inadequate restoration of euthyreosis on a cellular level. Although TSH, fT3 and fT4 are normal, and the body is therefore euthyroid on a systemic level, the cells still seem to be hypothyroid. Of course, I read a lot about rT3 and the way it influences cells (and got a heart attack when I saw the so called "calculations" of the fT3/rT3 ratio ... man, did anyone ever use units when calculating this? Got a new approach on this as well). But I couldn't help but wonder: Why would the pituitary secrete a normal amount of TSH when the cells are still hypothyroid due to too much rT3?

The answer: Because rT3 does not influence the pituitary TSH secretion at all, as it has been proven with rats! _(Cettour-Rose et al. - Inhibition of pituitary type 2 deiodinase by reverse triiodothyronine does not alter thyroxine-induced inhibition of thyrotropin secretion in hypothyroid rats, [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16131606](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16131606))_

We seem to have two independently operating systems of thyroid regulation in the body: The thyreotropic regulation system (the pituitary regulating the amount of TSH secreted, by measuring the amount of free T3 and free T4 in the bloodstream) + the competitive inhibition system (rT3 competing with T3 about the T3-receptors). Since the study proves that rT3 has no effect on TSH, both systems are independantly regulating the thyroid status of every cell in the body. Probably designed as one system being a slow, long-term regulation system and the other being a fast, quick-response system.

Three people (two members of our internet community + myself) tried the already described substitution method with a T3:T4 ratio in the dosage that was identical to what the healthy thyroid gland produces, approximately 1:14 (T4:T3) if you take the resorption in the jejunum into account (approx. 75% of orally given T4 being resorbed, but approx. 95% of orally given T3) and use molar unity (molecules, not micrograms, since T3 and T4 have a different molecular weight).

What did we find out?


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Optimal Treatment of Hashimoto's Disease - Searching for the unifying theory! Part 2
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-treatment-of-hashimotos-disease-searching-for-the-unifying-theory-part-2.9044/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Arsaneus — 2013-12-11T18:17:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-treatment-of-hashimotos-disease-searching-for-the-unifying-theory-part-2.9044/post-103140>

It didn't work. Although everything was as closely to the normal human physiology as possible, the body still produced way too much rT3 in all three of us. Our fT3/rT3 ratios were in between 7.8 \* 10\[SUP\]-3\[/SUP\] and 12.0 \* 10\[SUP\]-3\[/SUP\]. (what would usually be called a ratio of "7.8" or "12.0" ... like I said, whoever invented this ratio fucked up with the units  )

Why doesn't the body normalize when everything is approximately as it were before, when one was healthy?
Our hypothesis is, that the body treats it's thyroid cells like they were a disease. A bacteria that wants to infect the body. This is what an autoimmune disease is all about, isn't it? The body confusing its own cells for an enemy, and therefore attacking them via the immune system?

It is scientifically proven that the body raises rT3 levels while undergoing an infection. So, our idea is, what if the body reacts the same way when undergoing Hashimoto's Thyreoiditis? It cannot distinguish between a bacterial infection and an autoimmune disease, from the body's perspective it's both the same. Therefore one would assume, that it reacts to Hashimoto's disease the same way like it would react to any bacterial infection: by producing way more rT3 than usual, to shut down the body's metabolism until the "infection" is dealt with. Which never happens, since thyroid tissue has regenerative abilities and it takes anything from 5 years to a whole lifetime to get rid of the thyroid completely. Therefore, rT3 levels stay up all the time.

This could even explain why people report having hypothyroid symptoms, despite having a normal TSH _(by the way - normal seems to be 0.3 to 2.1, as VÃ¶lzke et al. discovered for Germany in 2005: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15785248](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/15785248), the U.S. upper limit of somewhat like 5.0 to sometimes 10.0 is a joke if you ask me)_. It's possible that they start producing massive amounts of rT3 as an reaction to the beginning autoimmune process, way before any substantial thyroid damage occures. We've had reports like this serveral times from people who got lucky being diagnosed in the early stages of Hashimoto. They had positive antibody lab results, but their TSH often was about 0.5 to 1.5, and despite being healthy otherwise, they already suffered from hypothyroid symptoms way to strong in relation to the little thyroid damage they had.

So, apparently the only way to ever normalize the rT3 production was by killing off the T4 needed for it's production. Which would have several side effects because the body was never designed to operate in such a manner.

I was about to give up when I stumbled upon Shijin13 in this forum.

**The case of Shijin13**
As we all know, taking the "T3 only" method is an effective mean of reducing rT3 and therefore normalizing the fT3/rT3 ratio, leading to intracellular euthyreosis in all of the bodys cells. But it has some serious flaws as well:

-   It produces huge peaks in the fT3 (which the body was never designed to handle)
-   It suppresses the TSH (which we don't know what the long term effects will be, given extrathyreoidal TSH receptors been discovered)
-   It reduces the fT4 to a point way below the normal range (which robbes the body of any means of producing new T3 in between two dosages one takes)

In addition, many patients suppress their TSH, either by using high to abnormal (sorry) T3 amounts, or by using a lot of NDT. Which makes it impossible to determine how their bodys would have reacted if the TSH wouldn't have been suppressed.

Shijin13 instead took 2 Grains of NDT, causing a non-supressed TSH with almost normal lab results. Okay, the fT3/rT3 ratio was way higher than any healthy person would have had in their whole lifetime, but it seemed to work anyway.

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6576-New-set-of-labs-hack-em-if-you-feel-like-it](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?6576-New-set-of-labs-hack-em-if-you-feel-like-it)

Shijin13 took 1 1/2 grains in the morning and an additional 1/2 grain in the afternoon. Her lab results were exactly:

TSH: 0.470 (0.450-4.500) ÂµIU/mL
fT3: 6.2 (2.0-4.4) pg/mL
fT4: 1.09 (0.82-1.77) ng/dL
rT3: 11.9 (9.2-24.1) ng/dL

fT3/fT4: 0.68 (0.22-0.50 as determined in [Gullo et al., 2011](http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0022552#pone-0022552-g001))
fT3/rT3: 52.1 (normal: approx. 22 \* 10\[SUP\]-3\[/SUP\] as can be determined based on the healthy control group of [Chomard et al, 1985](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/4055428))

She said that she felt very well on that dosage.

Now, this proves that the fT3/rT3 ratio can be normalized (or, to be strict, raised above normal) without having to suppress the TSH or to lower the fT4 under the reference range. Even without having to set aside T4 in the substitution.

If we can replicate this and fine-tune it, we may be able to develop a method of substituting thyroid hormones and making all body cells euthyroid without losing TSH, and without losing the bodys ability to generate new T3 by conversion. In combination with what I've described as my own recently used method above, we could eliminate fT3 peaks altogether by splitting the dosage even further than Shijin13 does. Altogether, this would result in a normal healthy state of being, like we felt before we got the autoimmune disease.

The trick may be to lower the supply of T4 _just enough_ to force the body to lower its rT3 production (despite it wants to, because of the "infection" it fights), but not enough to lose the ability to generate new T3 via conversion from T4, which helps stabilizing fT3 levels.

**My questions to Shijin13 and all the others who may be interested in pursuing this:**

**Shijin13, what thyroid disease do you have exactly? Do you have a thyroid left at all? Do you have new lab results, and how do you feel?

Has anyone in this community ever archieved a normal fT3/rT3 ratio without having to go "T3 only", and without having to suppress the TSH? Or known anybody who did this?**

With best regards & greetings from Germany,
Arsaneus

**@MODERATORS: PLEASE COMBINE THIS WITH THE FIRST PART OF MY THREAD, THANK YOU! (I didn't know it had to be approved by moderators before being posted)**

### Arsaneus — 2013-12-15T12:54:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-treatment-of-hashimotos-disease-searching-for-the-unifying-theory-part-2.9044/post-103488>

Thanks for answering. Sounds good, maybe your problems are caused by the short half-life of T3? A bunch of people who tried it felt a lot better when taking T3 four times daily than two times. T3's half-life is only about 17 hours, meaning it decays rapidly in the bloodstream. Just a thought.

Gluten free diet is a good idea, I'm doing that myself since I found out that I'm gluten sensitive. There are rumors about a gluten-thyroid-connection. Some scientists believe that gluten is even a trigger for autoimmune diseases in general. I've got an article in German on that:

> "Typ-1-Diabetiker haben erhÃ¶htes ZÃ¶liakie-Risiko"
>
> AuÃŸerdem vermeide man mit der DiÃ¤t, dass weitere Autoimmunerkrankungen entstehen, betont Hummel. Diese Beobachtung stÃ¼tze die These, dass Gluten offenbar ein externer Trigger verschiedener Autoimmunerkrankungen ist.

([http://www.aerztezeitung.de/medizin...-1-diabetiker-erhoehtes-zoeliakie-risiko.html](http://www.aerztezeitung.de/medizin/krankheiten/diabetes/article/588042/typ-1-diabetiker-erhoehtes-zoeliakie-risiko.html))

> "Type 1 diabetics have an increased risk of celiac disease"
>
> The diet also helped to avoid developing further autoimmune diseases, Mr. Hummel says. This observation supports the hypothesis, that gluten apparently is an external trigger of various autoimmune diseases.

Although not proven for certain, a gluten free diet may help not only improving certain symptoms, but preventing the development of further autoimmune diseases as well.

### Tms — 2013-12-11T23:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungry-at-night-only.9048/post-103157>

Hi
I've been doing LR for about 4 weeks now. I feel good all day. I'm not hungry and i feel good. I eat dinner around 5 and by 8 I start to get hungry. I don't eat bc that would be snacking but going to bed hungry is really hard. I drink water and tea to help but I don't think I can live like this forever.
I do follow the protocol exactly.
My sleep is really off. I fall asleep but can't stay asleep. I'm getting my hormones checked this week.
Does anyone have a suggestion about the night hunger. A small snack would be so helpful.
Thanks
Tms

### AnnieW — 2013-12-11T23:32:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungry-at-night-only.9048/post-103159>

I think you need to eat more food earlier in the day. I front load all my food in the morning...ideally I would love to consume everything for the day in one meal but I haven't been able to get there yet. Lots of fat in the morning with your protein. I usually have a small dinner of protein and vegetables.

### caroline — 2013-12-12T00:56:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hungry-at-night-only.9048/post-103164>

Hi Tms .... and welcome. Are you having a BAB within 30 minutes of rising? Please reread the protocol again to make sure you are doing it properly. The easy start guides are great.

BAB within 30 minutes of rising .... at least 50gr of protein - you may need more ... I did

leave 4 hours between meals ....... absolutely no snacking!

leave at least 4 hours after last meal and before bed.

It sounds as if you are trying not to eat........ are you having 3 meals a day?

Please start a journal in the journal section and tell us a little about yourself - and what you are eating in a day. This will give Dr. Kruse some context.
Click on forum at the top of the page and go down 5 categories until you see the journal section.

Have you listened to Dr.K's podcasts? They are great for quick info. and also the webinars if you can manage that.
There is a fantastic bootcamp running right now "Heal your Hormones" with Dr. Tim Jackson.

Have you read Dr.K's book? The Epi-Paleo RX - It is a great overview of all of this.

Your non- native EMF exposure is critical .....your home? your work?

Are you observing circadian cycles? This is crucial.
When you rise - look at the sun....... and no fake lite. When the sun sets - no lites on. You need to reset your internal clock. When you sleep - that is when autophagy occurs. You need to reset your internal clock to get to optimal.

Avoid blue light .....computers/TV/ iphone/ipad etc. etc. ..... do you have blue blocking glasses?

Have you started C/T?

There is a lot to take on board .... but it is very doable....and we will help! keep reading and asking questions....it is so worth it!
